fr:mainmatter.html:p: Eat trash. Learn logic.
Exercises are at https://binchicken.one.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: In addition to the assessment tasks (discussed below), the unit consists of lectures and tutorials.

You're in the lectures right now; they are written in form, and interlinked. The Table of Contents is a great place to get an overview of the semester. There are 12 numbered lectures and 12 teaching weeks of the semester: a lucky coincidence! Each week, you should make sure you read that week's lecture and understand it as well as you can before you come to tutorial. (That said, if you haven't read the lecture on a given week, you are still very much welcome to come to tutorial!)

The lectures are written (rather than audio or video) so that they can serve a double purpose: they will introduce you to our material, but they will also serve as reference material for you. You do not need to memorise anything in this unit; you can always go back to the lectures and check.

(At least if you're in one of my tutes, you may well get sick of hearing me say "check the lectures" in response to some questions. But I only say it so much because it's good advice! When you have a question about exactly how something works, the answer is often in the lectures. Going through the work of digging the answer up when you need it will help you learn, much more than me just telling you the answer would.)

These lectures are an evolving thing; this semester marks a big shift: from pdf to the web-based format you're currently looking at, which uses the excellent Forester tool. Some lectures are not yet converted over; they will appear as we go.

If you find any errors or face any difficulties with these lectures, please let me know! These lectures have benefitted tremendously from the experiences, suggestions, and ideas that past students have offered. Future students can benefit from your experiences as well; just let me know any sugggestions!

Tutorials are where most of your learning will happen in this unit. No attendance is taken at tutorials, and no part of your semester mark depends on them; they are pure learning opportunities for you and nothing more. Each week, your tutor will give you a range of exercises (more straightforward) and puzzles (more devious) that you'll work on in various ways, and also answer any questions you have about the material.
This week, we'll return to thinking about models. We'll look at the most usual models used for intuitionistic logic, which is a logic we haven't so far seen through a model-theoretic lens.

Remember that when we're thinking about a logic model-theoretically, the central notion we use is the idea of counterexamples. We need to say what our models are, and we need to say what it takes for a model to be a counterexample to an argument. Then the arguments we count as valid are the ones that have no counterexamples.

In the first half of the unit, the models we looked at were valuations in structures. It is possible to make models for intuitionistic logic that way, but it's a pain in the rear. Intuitionistic logic is a matrix logic, so it is possible to give a single matrix that determines intuitionistic logic perfectly. But the structure that matrix is built on is infinitely large; it has infinitely many truth values. So it's not like our simple three- and four-valued critters from earlier in the semester.

Because of this, we'll use a different kind of model altogether, not involving structures at all. The keys are:
what a model is, and
what it takes to for one of them to be a counterexample to an argument.
That much is familiar at this point. But our models for intuitionistic logic will not be valuations in structures, so much will also be different!

The models we'll use for intuitionistic logic have two components: a frame and a valuation on that frame. (As we'll see, these valuations are different from the kinds of valuations in structures we looked at earlier in the semester.)
First, though, frames. The easiest way to think of frames, I think, is as a bunch of points with some collection of arrows between them. For example, this is a frame:

For later reference, let's name this frame "`Example frame 1"'. In Example frame 1, I've named the points 1, 2, 3, 4; in general I will use numbers for points. As so often when we use numbers, though, their numeric properties don't matter; it never matters which number we use for what, and we'll never add them or anything. I'm just using numbers as a convenient stock of tags.

In a frame, you can connect points with arrows any way you like. This could include no connections at all, or every point connected to every other point, or some selection in between, as we see above.

There are only two things we need to keep track of about a frame: what its points are, and which points are reachable from which points. To think about reaching a point x from a point y, imagine that you are standing at y and trying to get to x, and that you can only travel along arrows, and only in the direction that the arrows indicate. If it is possible to get from y to x in this way, then y can reach x, and we write y R x to have a quick way to say this. We can also say that x is reachable from y to mean the same thing. You don't have to move at all if you don't need to, so every point is automatically reachable from itself.

For example, in Example frame 1, all of the following reachability claims are true, and no others:
1 R 1, and 2 R 2, and 3 R 3, and 4 R 4
1 R 2
1 R 3
2 R 3
3 R 2

And that's all we need to know about Example frame 1. Note that we have 1 R 3 here, even though there is no arrow in the diagram directly from 1 to 3. We can still reach 3 from 1 along the arrows, by first stepping to 2 and then to 3. (Or by doing that and then stepping around the loop connecting 2 and 3 any number of times.)

In fact, since every point is reachable from itself, we don't even really need to keep track of the points in a frame separately; if we know what's reachable from what, we will automatically have a list of all the points.
Any way of setting up points and arrows determines some story about reachability among those points, so any way of setting up points and arrows gives us a frame.

You might note that, if we added to the diagram of Example frame 1 a new arrow directly from 1 to 3, that wouldn't have any effect on what's reachable from what, since we already have 1 R 3 in this frame. Since reachability is all that matters to a frame, if we're being picky we'd say that the points-and-arrows depiction I've given you isn't Example frame 1 itself, but rather a presentation of Example frame 1; and adding an arrow from 1 to 3 would give us a different presentation of the very same frame. For our purposes, though, we don't need to be this picky; points-and-arrows gives a nice way to think about frames, as long as you remember that all that really matters is reachability.

A frame is some set of things, called points, with some arrows between them, in any configuration. (We usually use numbers for points, but that's not very important; it's just convenient.)
All that matters about a frame is which points are reachable from which points. Every point can reach itself, plus points can reach along any arrows in the frame, and they can follow chains of arrows: anywhere that a particular point can reach in this way is reachable from that starting point. (Any reaching along an arrow or along a chain of arrows must follow the direction of all the arrows in question.)

Example frame 1 is finite, and of course any frame that I can depict like this will be finite. But there is no restriction in general to finite frames; a frame can certainly contain infinitely many points if you like. As long as it's clearly specified what's reachable from what, there's no problem with this.

If you're interested in the details: what matters about reachability here is just that it's a preorder. I won't define "preorder" here, since it's not a concept we'll need. But if you already know what a preorder is, or if you feel like looking it up, that's exactly what we're using here. Using points and arrows, and then thinking about reachability via those arrows, is just a hopefully-at-least-somewhat-intuitive way of getting at preorders.
That's all pretty abstract so far, and it's at that abstract level that logic really lives and breathes. It can sometimes help to have a more concrete way of thinking about things, though.

One way these models are sometimes thought of is as representing a kind of temporal growth of knowledge. In this way of thinking, each point in a model represents a particular stage of knowledge, and as we move around a frame, along the given paths, we get to points that have more and more knowledge. On this way of thinking about models, when x and y are points in a model, and x R y, that means something like: if we're at stage x of knowledge, there is something we could learn, without forgetting anything, that would move us to stage y.

On the other hand, when it's not the case that x R y, that means something like: if we're at stage x of knowledge, there's no way we could learn any more than we already know and end up at y. Since y doesn't preserve everything we already have at x, we would have to forget something to get from x to y.

On this interpretation, we're thinking about knowledge as something that can never be lost. (Or at least we're not thinking about what losing it would be like.) We're only concerned with which sets are subsets of which others: which can be extended without losing anything to reach which others. That's probably not a great theory of how knowledge actually works! (For example, I know where my keys are right now; but maybe tomorrow I won't.) So don't get too distracted by this kind of interpretation; it's just here to give the flavour. If you can imagine a kind of cumulative development of knowledge, that never loses anything as it goes, that's the kind of picture. Other interpretations of frames are of course possible, but hopefully this helps sink in an image.

Now, once we have a frame, to reach a model we need a valuation on that frame. These valuations are like our valuations on structures in some ways but not in others. What will be familiar is that these valuations start by assigning values to atomic formulas, and work up from there in specified ways to assign values to compound formulas. But the way this works will be new.

We work with two values in these models, which I'll call Y and ?. I've chosen these names to suggest the knowledge interpretaion explained above: roughly, something gets the value Y when it's known (Yes), and the value ? when it's not known. It's ? and not something like N because when something is not known, it might later come to be known or it might not; it's not yet a No, just a ?.

As I mentioned above, what's known or not might vary between points in a model. So different sentences can get different values at different points in a model. When we're keeping track of assigning these values to sentences, then, we always need to have in mind both a particular model and a particular point in that model. To indicate the value that a sentence A takes at the point x in the model M, I write M(A, x). So if A takes the value Y there, then M(A, x) = Y.

These valuations on a frame start from assigning values to atomic formulas, and then on this basis we can calculate the values assigned to compound formulas. We do this by assigning to each point in the frame some set of atomic formulas, to be understood as the set of atomic formulas that get the value Y at that point.

But unlike when we're dealing with valuations on structures, here the assignment of values to atomic formulas is not completely unconstrained. Recall the knowledge interpretation: when x R y in a frame, we want that to mean that we can move from x to y without losing any knowledge that's present at x. So our valuations are constrained in this way: if M(p, x) = Y, and x R y in M, then we must have M(p, y) = Y as well. That goes for any atomic sentence, not just p. In other words, when x R y in a frame, then the set of atomic formulas a valuation assigns to x must be a subset of the set it assigns to y.

A valuation on a frame is a choice of a set of atomic sentences for each point in the frame, subject to the condition that for all points x, y in the frame, if x R y, then the set assigned to x must be a subset of the set assigned to y.

For an example, return to Example frame 1. Let's assign sets of atomics to points as follows: 1 gets \{p, q\}; 2 gets \{p, q, r\}; 3 gets \{p, q, r\}; and 4 gets \{q, s\}. This meets our requirement: whenever one point is reachable from another in this frame, the point we're reaching from gets a subset of the set assigned to the point we're reaching to. Note as well that when we have a loop of reachability, like between 2 and 3 in this frame, then that means that every point in the loop must be assigned the very same set of atomics.

In this model, I've assigned only a finite set of atomics to each point. But this is not required at all; any choice of sets of atomics is a valuation, so long as it connects to reachability in the required way.

That's what an intuitionistic model is: a frame, plus a set of atomics chosen for each point in the frame, such that whenever x R y in the frame, the set assigned to x is a subset of the set assigned to y.
An intuitionistic model is a frame, plus a valuation on that frame.
On this basis, we can calculate values for every formula of our language at every point in the model. Here's how.

As before, for any atomic sentence A, we will have M(A, x) = Y if A is a member of the set assigned to the point x in the model M. For compound sentences, we proceed by way of the main connective of the sentence. I'll discuss \land , \lor , \top , \bot first, since those are simpler; I'll put off \neg , \rightarrow for now.

As you can see, this is just like the Boolean structure, but with Y playing the role of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and ? the role of \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The difference of the names of the values isn't really important; it's just there to be reminiscent of a difference in how these values are often interpreted. For example, where our clause for conjunction in the classical case is often taken to indicate that a conjunction is true iff both conjuncts are true, here the corresponding clause is meant to indicate that a conjunction is known iff both conjuncts are known.

This should lead you to wonder about disjunction: surely it's possible to know a disjunction without knowing either disjunct, but the clause we have here seems to rule this out. In fact, there is another way to proceed that would be more careful about disjunction, for just this reason; we're working this way because it's simpler, even though it strains the knowledge interpretation. Our method is based on what are sometimes called "Kripke models"; the more involved method would work with what are called "Beth models". Both kinds of model determine intuitionistic logic.

If that were all there was to it, though, then there would be no point in bundling up multiple points into a frame. Where that stuff comes into play is with the remaining connectives: \neg and \rightarrow . To give a value to \neg A at a point x in a model, we don't just look at x itself. Instead, we look at every point that's reachable from x in the model. Remember, every point is reachable from itself; so this includes looking at x. But it often includes more.

For \neg A to get the value Y at a point x, what's required is that at every point reachable from x in the model, A gets the value ?. The idea is that it's not enough in general for us to know \neg A just that we don't yet know A. Something more is needed. What's needed is that there is no way to extend our knowledge where we could come to know A. Or at least, that's the idea that these models go with.

The idea for implication is similar. Again, to give a value to A \rightarrow B at a point x in a model M, we look at every point reachable from x in M. For A \rightarrow B to get the value Y, what's required is this: that there's no way to extend our knowledge to know A without also knowing B. That is, we require that every point y reachable from x where M(A, y)= Y also gives us M(B, y) = Y.
Let's look at some examples, using a new example model, which I'l call M:

Here, I've indicated the valuation along with the frame, by putting at each point the set of atomic sentences assigned to that point. Here are some example calculations of values of various compound sentences at points in M:
M(\neg q, 5) = ?. Even though we have M(q, 5) = ?, the fact that M(q, 3) = Y, together with the fact that 5 R 3, forces this result.
M(\neg u, 2) = Y. Here, we have two points we need to check: 2 and 1. Since both of these points assign the value ? to the atomic sentence u, we have this result.
M(q \rightarrow r, 5) = ?. Since we have M(q, 3) = Y and M(r, 3) = ?, this result follows.
M(r \rightarrow q, 5) = ?. Since we have M(r, 4) = Y and M(q, 4) = ?, this result follows.
M((q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q), 5) = ?. We've seen that both of the components of this disjunction take value ? at 5, so the disjunction does as well.
Note what's happened here: we've got a reflection at 5 of what's going on at both 3 and 4: in particular, 3 gives us q without r and 4 gives us r without q. Our connectives are giving us a way to "look around" inside the model, in a limited way.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow s, 5) = Y. Here, we need to check every point, since every point is reachable from 5. What we see is that there are only two points that give the value Y\ to q \land r; these are 2 and 1. And both of these points also give the value Y to s. So we have the result.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow t, 5) = ?. Again, we check every point, and see that just 2 and 1 give the value Y\ to q \land r. This time, though, one of them (viz 2) gives ? to the consequent t. So we get this result instead.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow t, 2) = ?. This time we only need to look at 2 and 1, but since 2 still gives Y to the antecedent and ? to the consequent, it's the same result.
M((q \land r) \rightarrow t, 1) = Y. Here, we only need to look at 1 itself, since 1 is the only point reachable from 1 in M. And here, we have the value Y for both p \land q and t. So we get this result.
M(\neg ((q \land r) \rightarrow t), 2) = ?. Here, we look at 2 and 1, since these are the points reachable from 2 in M. At each of these, we ask: what is the value of (q \land r) \rightarrow t here? As we've seen, it's Y at 1 and ? at 2. So we have this result.
Let's wrap all this up in a definition for later reference:

Given an intuitionistic model M and a point x in it, we assign values to every sentence at x in the model as follows:
atomic: for any atomic sentence A, we have M(A, x) = Y iff A is in the set of atomic sentences assigned to x by the model, and otherwise M(A, x) = ?.
conjunction: for any sentences A and B, we have M(A \land B, x) = Y iff M(A, x) = M(B, x) = Y, and otherwise M(A \land B, x) = ?.
disjunction: for any sentences A and B, we have M(A \lor B, x) = ? iff M(A, x) = M(B, x) = ?, and otherwise M(A \lor B, x) = Y.
verum and falsum: always, M(\top , x) = Y and M(\bot , x) = vQ.
negation: for any sentence A, we have M(\neg A, x) = Y iff every point y reachable from x is such that M(A, y) = vQ; otherwise, M(\neg A, x) = vQ.
implication: for any sentences A and B, we have M(A \rightarrow B, x) = vQ iff there is some point y reachable from x with M(A, y) = Y and M(B, y) = ?; otherwise M(A \rightarrow B, x) = Y.

We can also do some more general reasoning about possible values at points. The most important thing to notice here is called persistence: in any model N, at any points x and y, if x R y and N(A, x) = Y, then N(A, y) = Y as well. That is, as our knowledge of atomic sentences grows, moving along the paths in our frame, the same thing happens with compound sentences as well. We never lose knowledge of any sentence, atomic or compound, as long as we move only to reachable points. For atomic formulas, we have forced this directly, with our restrictions on valuations. But for compound sentences, this is a more complex point; it's true because of the interactions between the various connectives.

To see that persistence is true, we can bring back our old friend induction. We want to show that persistence holds for absolutely every sentence A. Recall that induction has two main parts: first, we show that persistence holds for atomic sentences. Then, we go connective by connective, showing that for any compound sentence, as long as persistence holds of its components, then it holds of the compound sentence itself. That's enough to show that persistence holds for every sentence. We'd have no way to build a sentence where it failed, since we'd have to build the sentence ultimately from atomics using connectives.

So first, let's look at persistence for atomics. This says that in any model N, for any points x and y and any atomic sentence A, if x R y and N(A, x) = Y, then N(A, y) = Y as well. Here, though, this is required as we specify a valuation; any way to make this fail doesn't count as a valuation at all. That is, persistence does indeed hold for atomics. That's the first part done!
On to the connectives.

html:ul.html:li: For a conjunction A \land B: we need to show that A \land B is persistent, as long as A and B are. So we assume that A and B are persistent, since if they're not we don't need to worry about A \land B. Suppose, then, that x R y in some model N, and that N(A \land B, x) = Y. By the way values are assigned to conjunctions, this means that both A and B also get the value Y at x in N. And since both A and B are persistent, they both must get the value Y at y in N as well. But then N(A \land B, y) = Y, and this is all we need.

For a disjunction A \lor B, the reasoning is very similar to conjunction. Again, we assume that A and B are persistent, since if they're not we don't need to worry about A \lor B. Suppose, then, that x R y in some model N, and that N(A \lor B, x) = Y. By the way values are assigned to disjunctions, this means at least one of A and B also gets the value Y at x in N. And since both A and B are persistent, at least one of them must get the value Y at y in N as well. But then N(A \lor B, y) = Y, and this is all we need.
Showing that \bot is persistent is quick: it never takes the value Y anywhere, so we're all set!
Similarly for \top ; since it takes the value Y everywhere, we're good!

For a negation 
eg A: we need to show that 
eg A is persistent as long as A is. So suppose A is persistent, and that x R y in some model N with N(
eg A, x) = Y. That means that in every point reachable from x in N, the sentence A gets the value ?. Now, since y is itself reachable from x, every place reachable from y is also reachable from x---go from x to y first, and then on to wherever. So in all these points reachable from y, the sentence A gets the value ?. And that's enough for it to be the case that N(
eg A, y) = Y.

Finally, implication: we need to show that A \rightarrow B is persistent as long as A and B are. So suppose A and B are persistent, and that x R y in some model N with N(A \rightarrow B, x) = Y. Then in every point reachable from x in N, if the sentence A gets the value Y in that superset, then so does B. Now, since y is itself reachable from x, every point reachable from y is also reachable from x. So in all these points reachable from y, if the sentence A gets the value Y in that superset, then so does B. And that's enough for it to be the case that N(A \rightarrow B, y) = Y.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That's enough to show that persistence really does hold in these models, no matter what sentence we're looking at.

You can use persistence when you're reasoning about what's possible in these models. For example, let's suppose we have some model N and a point x in N where N(A, x) = Y. Now that we know persistence holds, this is enough to see that N(
eg 
eg A, x) = Y as well. Here's how the reasoning might go:
To show that N(
eg 
eg A, x) = Y, we need to know that for every point y reachable from x in the model N, we have N(
eg A, y) = ?. That in turn means that we need to know that there is some z reachable from y in N with N(A, z) = Y. So what we need to show is this: every point reachable from x in N itself has some point reachable from it in N where A gets the value Y.
But now take any y reachable from x in N. Since A is persistent, we know that N(A, y) = Y. Also, y is reachable from itself. So we've shown what was needed.
Note that this reasoning needed to appeal to persistence to get where it needed to go. This is usual; in reasoning about these models, you will often need to pay attention to persistence.

Okay; those are our models. To get a logic from these, we need to do the usual model-theoretic thing. That is, we need to say which models are counterexamples to which arguments. As you might expect, this will make use of the values assigned by the models at various points.

In fact, it's easiest to say what it takes for a point in a model to be a counterexample to an argument. A model then gets to be a counterexample to an argument by containing a point that's a counterexample to that argument.

A point x in a model M is a counterexample to the argument \Gamma \;/\; A when it meets two conditions:
for every sentence B in \Gamma we have M(B, x) = Y; and
M(A, x) = ?.

This is basically just a designated-value approach again, with Y designated and ? undesignated, except that we need to remember that sentences need both a model and a point in that model to get any value at all.

Before we see some examples, let me remind you: the logic this determines is exactly intuitionistic logic, the same logic determined by the proof system NJ. That is, if an argument has a proof in NJ, then no model provides a counterexample to it; and if an argument has no proof in NJ, then there is a model that provides a counterexample to it. So all the examples of counterexamples will be to arguments that have no proof in NJ, since those are the only arguments that have any counterexamples at all.

Do note that, at least for now, you have to trust me on this: that these models determine the same logic as the proof system NJ. That's certainly not obvious! And we haven't looked at all at why it might be so. But it is indeed so.
You might recall that in intuitionistic logic, the argument \neg \neg p \;/\; p is not valid. So it must have a counterexample. Here is one:
In this model, both points assign ? to \neg p; this is because each of them can reach some point that assigns Y to p. (For both points, this reachable place is 2.)

Because of this, in turn, both points assign Y to 
eg 
eg p; neither of them can reach anywhere in the model that assigns Y to 
eg p. So 1 assigns Y to 
eg 
eg p and ? to p; it is our counterexample, as it occurs in this model.

Here's how you might interpret this: in intuitionistic logic, we might see \neg A as saying something like "I know something that rules out my ever coming to know A". And we might know something that rules out our ever coming to know something that rules out our ever coming to know A, without yet actually knowing A.

Looking at this model, we've got a situation at the point 1 where we don't yet know p, but there's no way in the model to ever come to know 
eg p. So we do know 
eg 
eg p. That means that the argument 
eg 
eg p \;/\; p can have a known premise and an unknown conclusion, so intuitionistic logic judges it invalid.

Here's another example: in intuitionistic logic, the argument from (p \land q) \rightarrow r to (p \rightarrow r) \lor (q \rightarrow r) fails. So it must have a counterexample. Here is one: let the model M be:

Now, think about the values of the sentences in the argument at the point 1 in this model. We have M((p \land q) \rightarrow r, 1) = Y, since the only point reachable from 1 that assigns Y to p \land q in this model is 4, and 4 also assigns Y to r. But both p \rightarrow r and q \rightarrow r get value ? at 1, for p \rightarrow r because of the presence of 2 in the model and for q \rightarrow r because of the presence of 3 in the model. So their disjunction also gets a ? at 1. This gives the premise of this argument getting a Y at 1 in this model, and the conclusion getting a ? there. So we have our counterexample.

This model pictures a certain kind of dependency: if we have both p and q, then we must have r as well, but neither p nor q on its own is enough for r. This is a common kind of dependency: everything from light switches to the law includes situations where two things need to come together to suffice for something else, where neither one would suffice on its own. Our intuitionistic models can picture this very straightforwardly, as this example shows.

However, all of the logics K3, LP, FDE, and classical logic validate this argument. So this seems to be one way in which intuitionistic implication is more flexible than the implications of all those logics.

I'll close these notes by looking at two special kinds of models, models that obey certain extra restrictions. Since models are potential counterexamples, every extra restriction we impose on models removes some potential counterexamples. That means that we'll end up with stronger logics.

All the talk of "stages of development of knowledge" in these models suggests that we might be able to see reachability as a kind of temporal flow within a model, with earlier points being able to reach later points. And you might think that time is totally ordered: that for any distinct times x and y, either x is before y or y is before x.

Our models, though, don't have to reflect this. For example, think of this earlier example model. Points 2 and 3 can each reach the other one, and point 4 can't reach or be reached by anything but itself. There's no great way to understand reachability in this frame as a total temporal order.

We can restrict attention, though, to a special kind of model called total models, where this is required. For a model to be total means that, whenever there are two distinct points x and y in it, then either x R y or y R x. For example, the counterexample I gave above to the argument \neg \neg p / p is a total model.

Suppose we restrict our attention, then, to total models. What logic results? That is, what if we only take a counterexample into account when it is a total model? We won't lose any valid arguments from intuitionistic logic: if an argument has no counterexamples at all, then it certainly has no total counterexamples. That's how we can be sure the resulting logic is stronger than intuitionistic logic. But will we gain any valid arguments? Another way to ask this: are there arguments that do have counterexamples, but that have no total counterexamples?
As it happens, there are. Consider the argument \;/\; (q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q). Here is a counterexample to the argument:
Double-check that this model really is a counterexample. Which point in the model provides the counterexample?

It's 1. The sentence q \rightarrow r gets the value Y at 3, so (q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q) also gets the value Y there. And r \rightarrow q gets the value Y at 2. But at 1, both q \rightarrow r and r \rightarrow q get the value ?; the first because 2 is reachable from 1, and the second because 3 is reachable from 1. So (q \rightarrow r) \lor (r \rightarrow q) also gets the value ? at 1.

But although this argument has a counterexample, it does not have a counterexample that is total. To see this, think what a counterexample to this argument must be like. It must be a model M containing a point 1 where M(q \rightarrow r, 1) = ? and M(r \rightarrow q, 1) = ?. Since M(q \rightarrow r, 1) = ?, there must be some 2 reachable from 1 in M where M(q, 2) = Y but M(r, 2) = ?. And since M(r \rightarrow q, 1) = ?, there must be some 3 reachable from 1 in M where M(r, 3) = Y but M(q, 3) = ?. Think, though, about 2 and 3. Neither of these can be reachable from the other. (Make sure you see why!) So M cannot be total. As such, there are no total counterexamples to this argument.
The logic of total models, then, is properly stronger than intuitionistic logic. It's a logic typically known either as LC or as Gödel-Dummett logic.

We met Dummett in an earlier reading on natural deduction. Kurt Gödel was a 20th century Austrian logician. He is responsible for two of the most important results in 20th century logic: the completeness theorem and the incompleteness theorem. At the outset of WW2 he fled to the US, where he became close friends with Albert Einstein. He later developed a fear of being poisoned, so significant that he eventually refused to eat and starved himself to death.

We can go even farther, though. Suppose we restrict our models yet again, down to models that contain just a single point. In such models, the only point reachable from the point is just that point itself.
All these models are total, so this is really an additional restriction. Again, then, we must end up at a stronger logic, but the question arises: is it properly stronger?

As you can check, the point in a one-point model is basically exactly a Boolean valuation, even for implication and negation. After all, a Boolean valuation assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to A \rightarrow B unless it assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to A and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to B, while our lone point in a model assigns Y to A \rightarrow B unless there's somewhere reachable from it that assigns Y to A and ? to B; if the valuation is itself the only thing around to be reached, these amount to the same thing. The same thing happens with negation. And for conjunction, disjunction, verum, and falsum, they already matched.

This means you can see Boolean valuations as just a special kind of intuitionistic model. Our intuitionistic models are more flexible, since they can contain multiple points, but they do not need to do this, and so Boolean valuations remain available even in this more general setting.

This means that if we restrict our attention to one-point models, we end up with a different logic than intuitionistic logic: we arrive at classical logic. What about LC; is there a difference between classical logic and LC? Indeed there is.
We've already seen a counterexample to \neg \neg p \;/\; p: the model

As you can see, the model is total. So this argument is invalid in LC, just like it's invalid in intuitionistic logic. But it is valid in classical logic. So classical logic is not just stronger than LC; they are distinct logics.

There is a whole field of logic devoted to studying logics in between intuitionistic logic and classical logic in strength. When such logics are reflexive, monotonic, transitive, and substitution invariant, they are known as intermediate logics. The study of intermediate logics, like the study of many other kinds of logics, proceeds by using both proof systems and model systems.

This week, we saw one way to provide models for intuitionistic logic. Each of these models has two components: a frame, and a valuation on that frame. You can think of a frame as a bunch of points with some collection of arrows between them; all that really matters is which of these points are reachable from which. A valuation on a frame assigns some set of atomic formulas to each point in the frame, subject to the condition that when x R y, the set assigned to x must be a subset of the set assigned to y. Given a model, we can calculate a value for every sentence at every point in the model.

A counterexample to an argument is a model containing a point at which all the premises get the value Y but the conclusion gets the value ?. Using this notion of counterexample with these models, we arrive at intuitionistic logic. We have also looked at some stronger logics that we can arrive at by considering certain special kinds of these models.

fr:title.text: How to get a logic from a natural deduction system

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: How to get a logic from a natural deduction system

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Last time, we met three natural deduction systems: NJ, NM, and NK. We spent the most time on NJ, and that will continue to be the case. We'll think of NM and NK basically as variations on NJ. Just remember that they differ from NJ in two different ways: NM by lacking the rule of \bot E, and NK by having in addition the rule of LEM.

This week, we're going to start thinking about the logics determined by these proof systems. Remember, a logic is a set of arguments. So we need a way, given a proof system, to determine a particular set of arguments. Here as everywhere in logic, there are options; but we're going to use a standard way to get the job done.

Recall that the idea behind proof systems like natural deduction is this: we say what it takes for a proof to be a proof of a particular argument, and then we count an argument as valid as long as there is at least one proof that is a proof of it. It's the reverse of what we did with valuations, where we said what it takes for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument, and then count an argument as invalid when there is at least one valuation that is a counterexample to it.
So what we need is just a story about what it takes for a natural deduction proof to be a proof of an argument. That's where this lecture starts.

From any natural deduction proof, we can determine a single argument \Gamma \;/\; A as follows: let A be the conclusion of the proof, and let \Gamma be the set of formulas that occur in the proof as open assumptions. Remember: sets don't care about multiplicity! So it doesn't matter if something occurs as an open assumption once or twenty times; the only thing that matters is whether it occurs at least once, or not at all. Any proof at all determines an argument in this way; we'll call it the smallest argument of the proof. That is:

Given a proof in a natural deduction system, the smallest argument of that proof is the argument \Gamma \;/\; A determined as follows:
\Gamma is the set of sentences that are open assumptions in the proof, and
A is the proof's conclusion.
Using that, we can say what it takes for a proof to be a proof of an argument:

A proof is a proof of an argument \Sigma \;/\; B iff, where the proof's smallest argument is \Gamma \;/\; A:
\Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , and
B is A.

To put that same idea in different words: a proof counts as a proof of \Sigma \;/\; B when the proof's conclusion is B and the proof's open assumptions are all among the members of \Sigma . It's totally fine if \Sigma has other members as well, but it has to contain all the proof's open assumptions.,

Because of this, every proof is a proof of very many different arguments. Again, this is just like our counterexamples were: a single valuation can be a counterexample to many different arguments at once, and now a single proof can be a proof of many different arguments at once.

The idea behind this way of thinking is this. Think of an argument \Sigma / B as saying that, if we established everything in \Sigma , that would be enough to work with to allow us to establish B. Now, if we have a proof of B whose open assumptions are all in \Sigma , then that shows us a way to do this. If we established everything in \Sigma , then we'd have established all the open assumptions of the proof, since all those assumptions are in \Sigma . And the proof shows us how, if we took those assumptions for granted, we could reason to the conclusion B. So if we've actually established those assumptions, rather than just taking them for granted, the proof would finish the job for us, showing us how to establish B. From this perspective, it's totally fine if there are extra things in \Sigma that aren't open assumptions in the proof; that doesn't get in the way at all.

You can see the smallest argument \Gamma \;/\; A of a proof as being the common core of all the arguments that the proof is a proof of: every argument that the proof is a proof of has a set of premises that includes everything in \Gamma , and has the conclusion A. It's absolutely crucial to remember, though, that any proof is a proof of many arguments, not just its smallest argument.
Here's an example proof from last time:
What arguments is this a proof of?

Well, the conclusion of this proof is p \rightarrow (p \land p), so every argument that it's a proof of has that conclusion as well. And the open assumptions of this proof are p \lor q, p, and q \rightarrow p, so every argument that it's a proof of has these among its premises. And that's all we need. So this is a proof of very many (in fact, infinitely many) different arguments. For example, it is a proof of p \lor q, p, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p); and it is also a proof of r, t \rightarrow s, \neg p, p \lor q, p, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p). Indeed, for any set \Gamma of sentences, this is a proof of \Gamma , p \lor q, p, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p).

It is not, however, a proof of p \lor q, q \rightarrow p \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p). The reason is that the proof has an open assumption, p, that is not among the premises of this argument. Similarly, this is not a proof of r, p \lor q \;/\; p \rightarrow (p \land p), for basically the same reason.
Here's another example proof from last time:

This has the conclusion (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), and the sole open assumption r. So it is a proof of any argument \Gamma \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), as long as \Gamma includes r. For example, it is a proof of r \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), and it is a proof of p \rightarrow p, r \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)).

However, it is not a proof of q \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)) or of \;/\; (p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)), since the open assumption r doesn't occur among the premises in either of these arguments.
One final example proof from last week:

This has the conclusion (p \land q) \rightarrow p, and it has no open assumptions. So it is a proof of absolutely any argument of the form \Gamma \;/\; (p \land q) \rightarrow p; since there are no assumptions at all, we say they're all among \Gamma no matter what \Gamma is. In particular, this holds even if \Gamma is empty; so this proof is a proof of \;/\; (p \land q) \rightarrow p. It's also a proof of, for example, (p \land q) \rightarrow p \;/\; (p \land q) \rightarrow p, and of every other argument with the same conclusion.
This last example reveals something: if you want to have a proof of an argument with no premises, you need to have a proof with no open assumptions.

Now we have three natural deduction proof systems, and we know how to tell when a proof is a proof of an argument. This gives us three logics (yes, they're all different), via the following definition:

fr:title.text: Logic determined by a proof system

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Logic determined by a proof system

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The logic determined by a particular proof system is the logic that contains an argument iff that argument has a proof in the system.
Note that there's no guarantee in general that two different proof systems must always result in two different logics. For example, imagine we added this rule to NK:

The resulting proof system would be different from NK, since there is no rule like this in NK; we would get a bunch of new proofs using this rule that aren't proofs in NK. However, it turns out that the resulting proof system still determines classical logic; this particular addition to the proof system hasn't changed the resulting logic. That is, every argument that has a proof in this larger system also has a proof in NK. There are more proofs in the larger system, but not proofs of anything new.

A bit of jargon we won't use this semester, but can lead you down a rabbit hole if you want: when a rule and a proof system are like this---adding the rule to the proof system doesn't change the resulting logic---the rule is called admissible in the proof system. To say that the logics determined by NM, NJ, and NK are all different, then, is to say that \bot E is not admissible in NM, and LEM is not admissible in NJ.

The logic determined by NJ in this way is called intuitionistic logic. The logic determined by NM in this way is called minimal logic. And the logic determined by NK in this way is good old classical logic.

That claim about classical logic is a substantial one: I really am saying that the logic determined by the proof system NK is exactly the same one we defined via matrices weeks ago! You should not think that that's obvious; it very much is not.
The other two, though, we'll treat as definitional, as saying which logics we mean by the names.
Intuitionistic logic is the logic determined by the proof system NJ. Minimal logic is the logic determined by the proof system NM.
Let's take a look at some of the similarities and differences between these logics, and see how they fit together.

The first thing to notice is that we have a simple order of strength among these three. Every NM proof is an NJ proof, and every NJ proof is an NK proof. So if an argument is valid in minimal logic, it has an NM proof, which means it has an NJ proof, and so is valid in intuitionistic logic too; that is to say, minimal logic is weaker than intuitionistic logic. For the same kind of reason, intuitionistic logic is weaker than classical logic.

In fact, they are properly weaker, in both cases. For example, p, 
eg p \;/\; q is valid in intuitionistic but not minimal logic, and 
eg 
eg p \;/\; p is valid in classical but not intuitionistic logic.
How do minimal and intuitionistic logics compare to our other logics? Here's the situation:

You might note that classical logic seems to play a special role here: every logic that we've explored is weaker than it! That's not entirely a coincidence. Classical logic has a property called Post completeness: this means that the only logic that is reflexive, monotonic, transitive, substitution invariant, and properly stronger than classical logic is the logic that includes every argument. As many widely-studied logics are indeed reflexive, monotonic, transitive, and substitution invariant, this means that they won't be properly stronger than classical logic. This doesn't mean, though, that they'll be weaker! But we won't look this semester at any example logics that are not weaker than classical logic.

Outside this side note, that is. One of the coolest logics out there that's not weaker than classical logic is called Abelian logic. This is a logic that's been studied a fair bit at Monash in particular; see for example this paper, written by two (at the time) Monash-associated logicians. The characteristic way Abelian logic ends up not weaker than classical logic: it has ((p \rightarrow q) \rightarrow q) \rightarrow p as a theorem. This is not a theorem of classical logic (although ((p \rightarrow \bot ) \rightarrow \bot ) \rightarrow p is).

Recall four properties that a logic might or might not have: reflexivity, monotonicity, transitivity, and substitution invariance. We've seen that every logic given by a matrix has all four of these properties. What about logics given by natural deduction systems?

First, think about reflexivity. As long as we allow ourselves to assume any sentence at all, then we will always have a proof of A \;/\; A, no matter what sentence A is: just the assumption of A constitutes such a proof. So reflexivity is assured. If we were to work with a system with restrictions on what could be assumed, then there would be a question about reflexivity. But we haven't looked at any such systems.

Second, think about monotonicity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. Then that proof itself is also a proof of \Sigma \;/\; A for any \Sigma where \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma . This is because of how we've defined what it is to be a proof of an argument. So any logic determined by a proof system in the way we're doing it here will be monotonic.

Third, think about transitivity. Suppose we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A and a proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B. The question is: given that much, must we always have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B? Yes, we must; here's why. Consider the proof of \Gamma , A \;/\; B---call this proof P---and think about its open assumptions. We don't know what these are, but we do know that each of them must either be in \Gamma or be A. If they're all in \Gamma , we're all set: the proof P is already also a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, which is what we're after.

But they might not all be in \Gamma . In particular, there can also be open assumptions of A in the proof P, and A might not be in \Gamma . If this is the case, then P is not yet a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A. But we can make use of the other proof we've assumed we have: the one of \Gamma \;/\; A.

Imagine plugging a copy of this other proof of \Gamma \;/\; A in at the top of P, wherever there is an open assumption of A. So instead of assuming A in P, we'll instead prove it via the proof of \Gamma \;/\; A we have. If we do this for every open assumption of A in P, we'll end up with a new larger proof of B, and now one where all its open assumptions are in \Gamma . That is, we'll have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; B. So the logics determined by our natural deduction proof systems are transitive.

That was notably more complex than reflexivity or monotonicity, so let's look at an example to see how it works. It's important to recall, though, that just one example isn't enough to show that transitivity holds. This needs to work for every possible example. That's why the schematic and general reasoning above was important. This one example is just to give the flavour; but you should look over it while keeping an eye on the general reasoning, to convince yourself this kind of thing is indeed always possible.
This example uses only rules from NM, so it works in any of our three systems. Here are two simple proofs in NM:

The first of these is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; q, and the second of p \rightarrow q, p, r, q \;/\; r \land q. So if the logic this system determines is transitive, there had better be a proof somewhere in the system of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q. And indeed there is: we take the first example proof here, and plug it into the top of the second, replacing each open assumption of q in the second proof with a copy of the first proof. The result looks like this:

See how, in this result, the bottom is made up of the second example proof above, while the top bits are filled in with the first proof. This result is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p, r \;/\; r \land q; just what we needed.

The key to making sure this is possible is this: all of our rules for building larger proofs out of smaller proofs (which is to say, all of our rules other than assumption, \top I, and LEM) are always applicable based only on the conclusions of the smaller proofs they build on. In the above example, the rule \land I doesn't care whether its inputs are assumptions, as they are in the original example, or proofs that do actual work, as they are in the finished product. The rule \land I acts on them just the same either way. So when we plug in full proofs for open assumptions, we can leave everything else alone and be confident that it will all work out: the result will still be a proof.

It's worth pausing here to think through how this would look with rules that do some discharging hanging around in the proofs. I promise it will work! But it is worth thinking through an example or two.

Finally, substitution invariance. This one is more straightforward: applying a substitution to an entire proof always results in another proof. So whenever we have a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A, if we want a proof of some substitution instance of that argument, we can just apply the appropriate substitution to the proof itself, and we're all set. To be sure this works, you need to check that it works for every rule. After all, imagine we had a rule like this:

Think about adding this rule to NJ. Then we reach a proof system that does include a proof of p \lor q \;/\; p. But this proof system still does not include any proof of r \lor s \;/\; r. The reason is that the rule FAKE RULE is not itself substitution invariant: it applies only to the particular sentences that occur in it. The actual rules of NM, NJ, and NK, though, are not like that. Every actual rule in these systems applies to every substitution instance of anything it applies to. So the logics these systems determine are indeed substitution invariant.

In philosophy, the words "realism" and "antirealism" get used for lots of different distinctions. But one pretty well-established use is connected to the difference between classical and intuitionistic logic, and it's that use I want to tell you a little about here.
One way to get into the idea is to think about the following question: how many seconds old was Socrates when he drank the hemlock?

Context: Socrates was an ancient Athenian philosopher who was sentenced to death by drinking hemlock in 399 BCE. He is mainly remembered through the writings of his student Plato. Socrates didn't write anything himself, and indeed is portrayed in Plato's dialogue Phaedrus as not being super-friendly to writing, saying (tr. Robin Waterfield):
"[T]here’s something odd about writing, Phaedrus, which makes it exactly like painting. The offspring of painting stand there as if alive, but if you ask them a question they maintain an aloof silence. It’s the same with written words: you might think they were speaking as if they had some intelligence, but if you want an explanation of any of the things they’re saying and you ask them about it, they just go on and on for ever giving the same single piece of information. Once any account has been written down, you find it all over the place, hobnobbing with completely inappropriate people no less than with those who understand it, and completely failing to know who it should and shouldn’t talk to."

It seems like a pretty safe bet that nobody will ever know the answer to the question of how many seconds old Socrates was when he drank the hemlock. Even so, some people think the question still has a true answer. The fact that nobody will ever know the answer is beside the point; there is still some number that is the true answer. This is one thing some people mean by "realism": the belief that there are unknowable truths. On the other hand, others think that there can't really be any truth of the matter here, without some method of discovering it. Maybe we could know that Socrates was more than a billion seconds (about 32 years) old, and less than three billion seconds old, so those things can be true. But if the exact number of seconds is really unknowable, then people in this second camp would maintain that there is no truth of the matter at all. This second view is sometimes called "antirealism".

One aspect of the debate between realists and antirealists focuses on the so-called law of excluded middle, or LEM. LEM is the schema A \lor 
eg A. As we saw last time, the difference between NJ and NK is precisely that NK contains a rule allowing us to prove any instance of LEM from nothing at all. That shows us that, in classical logic, any argument \;/\; A \lor 
eg A is valid, since every such argument has a proof. (You could also recall our presentation of classical logic via Boolean valuations, and check that whatever sentence A is, in every Boolean valuation v we have v(A \lor 
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}.) That is to say, every instance of LEM is a classical theorem.

In intuitionistic logic, by contrast, not every instance of LEM is a theorem. For example, the argument \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is not valid in intuitionistic logic. Try all you like, you will never find a proof in NJ of p \lor 
eg p without some open assumptions. It would be wrong to conclude from this that no instance of LEM is an intuitionistic theorem, however; some are. For example, \;/\; (p ightarrow p) \lor 
eg (p ightarrow p) is intuitionistically valid. So it's not that intuitionistic logic disagrees with classical logic about every instance of LEM. It's rather just that classical logic takes all such instances to be theorems, and intuitionistic logic takes only some to be.

This is one reason that the difference between these logics gets connected to the difference between realism and antirealism. According to realism, either Socrates was exactly 2.25 billion seconds old when he drank the hemlock, or he wasn't exactly 2.25 billion seconds old when he drank the hemlock. That we can't establish which is true doesn't matter; still it's either yes or no. On the other hand, antirealists want to resist this claim. They wouldn't want to say that either he was exactly 2.25 billion seconds old or he wasn't. Since we don't have any way to tell, many antirealists would not want to go along with the disjunction. As one antirealist puts the point: "Intuitionistic logic is most celebrated for rejecting the law of excluded middle...It does so on the ground that we may not be in a position to judge correct either an assertion or a denial of A". (Dummett, "'Yes', 'no', and 'can't say'") Note that by "rejecting the law of excluded middle", Dummett must mean rejecting some instances of excluded middle, not rejecting all instances of it. As we've seen, some instances of it are intuitionistic theorems.

The proofs of NJ are used by antirealists in this mould as a formal surrogate for the kinds of deductive reasoning people actually do to try to gain knowledge. On this approach, then, the fact that there is no proof in NJ of the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p fits well with the antirealist refusal to accept claims like "Either Socrates was exactly 2.25 billion seconds old when he drank the hemlock or he wasn't". We have no way to demonstrate this disjunction, antirealists would maintain, and so no right to assert it.

fr:title.text: Proof and meaning, truth value and meaning

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Proof and meaning, truth value and meaning

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I've described both valuations and proofs as ways of giving meaning to our languages. But what does that mean? There's a ton of philosophical work about this, and I won't be able to do more here than scratch the surface; but I'll try to give a broad-brush overview.

On one way of thinking about meaning, the meaning of a piece of language is mainly a matter of what part or parts of the world that language picks out. You can think of this way of handling meaning as a kind of generalization from cases like proper names. "Carl" is the name of my (most excellent) dog, and it might seem like that kind of thing is all there is to the meaning of the name "Carl": if you know who it picks out, you know all there is to know about the meaning of the name.

Where this kind of theory of meaning tends to get a bit stickier is with words like "very", or "because", or "and"; what things in the world do these words pick out? That's not a rhetorical question: in fact, linguists, together with philosophers and logicians, have developed sophisticated and interesting answers to this question. Many of those answers go beyond what we can see this semester, although the semester will leave you well-prepared to pursue such answers in the future, if you want.

But we can see, at least for a word like "and", something of what an answer might be like by thinking about our structures from earlier in the semester. Perhaps a truth table, like the ones we've seen for \land in our various structures, can be the right kind of thing for a word like "and" to pick out. Pursuing this line of thought leads to the development of what's often called "truth-conditional semantics", and this is currently the mainstream method for giving theories of meaning in linguistics and philosophy.

Truth-conditional semantics, though, is not the only option for thinking about linguistic meaning. On another way of thinking, the meaning of a piece of language is mainly a matter of how that language is used. You can think of this way of handling meaning as a kind of generalization from cases like "hello". You surely know the meaning of "hello". If you take a minute to try to explain what that meaning is, you'll probably find yourself describing how the word is used: it's something you say to greet someone. It can seem that, if you know exactly how "hello" is used, you know exactly what it means.

This kind of theory works well, too, for words like "and", but now it tends to invoke something like our introduction and elimination rules, rather than structures. When can you assert "A and B"? Whenever you can assert both A and B. That idea can be seen in our NJ rules for conjunction. Again, there is difficulty with words like "very" and "because"; but again, there are approaches that people take. This way of proceeding leads to an approach known as "proof-theoretic semantics".

Sticking points for proof-theoretic semantics include words like proper names. Again, think about the name "Carl" for my dog. When do we use that name? It's not a matter of proximity to Carl. For example, I'm in the office as I'm writing this, and Carl is not with me at the moment, but here I am, using the name Carl perfectly sensibly; and you understand me, even though Carl is probably not with you at the moment either. (If he is, give him some peanut butter! He loves it.) The easiest way to describe the use of "Carl" is to say that it's a name we use to refer to Carl; that is, to give the same answer that the truth-theoretic semanticist gives.

As I've indicated here, truth-conditional theories of meaning tend to sit well with model-theoretic approaches to language; and proof-conditional theories of meaning tend to sit well with proof-theoretic approaches to language. This is one reason it can be useful to see both kinds of approach to logic, as we're doing in this subject. Each of these ways of thinking about meaning has places where it works more smoothly and places where it works less smoothly. It's not completely obvious how to combine the two---and they are not the only options anyhow---but perhaps combining them is a useful way forward. For now, though, I'll leave it there.

Intuitionistic logic and minimal logic are weaker than classical logic, but in a different kind of way from the other logics we've considered. A lot of the interest in intuitionistic logic is driven by features of its implication, so I'll start there. Unlike all of K3, LP, FDE, but like classical logic, both intuitionistic and minimal logics obey principles called modus ponens and conditional proof. These are two properties often taken to be key to the meaning of implication, so it's worth pausing here to explore them a bit.
An argument is an instance of modus ponens when it has the form A \rightarrow B, A \;/\; B.

So, for example, the argument (p \land 
eg r) ightarrow (s \lor r), p \land 
eg r \;/\; s \lor r is an instance of modus ponens. One thing many thinkers take as a constraint on a good theory of implication is that every instance of modus ponens should come out to be valid. Suppose that the premises (p \land 
eg r) ightarrow (s \lor r) and p \land 
eg r hold. Since we've supposed that p \land 
eg r holds, it seems that everything it implies should also hold. And we've supposed that it implies s \lor r. Thus, it seems to many that s \lor r must follow from these premises.
Classical logic validates modus ponens---by which I mean that it validates every instance of modus ponens. Recall that implication works in the Boolean structure according to this table:

Since we arrive at classical logic by using the matrix that designates just \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} in this structure, to have a classical counterexample to p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q, we would need to assign both p \rightarrow q and p the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} while assigning q the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; as the above table shows, this is not possible. So p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q is valid in classical logic, and since classical logic is substitution invariant, this means that all instances of modus ponens are classically valid as well.
Now, in the strong Kleene structure, implication works according to this table:

If we designate just \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to reach the logic K3, you can see that the reasoning works just as in classical logic: there is no strong Kleene valuation v where v(p \rightarrow q) = v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, but where v(q) \neq \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. So p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q is valid in K3 as well. However, things are different when it comes to LP. Recall that LP can be specified by a matrix that uses the strong Kleene structure, but with both \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} counting as designated values. Then there is a valuation w with w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; that gives us w(p \rightarrow q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} by the above table. So w is a counterexample to p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q, and thus modus ponens is not valid in LP. As FDE is weaker than LP, this means that modus ponens must not be valid in FDE either.

When it comes to NJ, the reasoning around modus ponens is different. For any A and B, there is a proof of A \rightarrow B, A \;/\; B, consisting of just assumptions of A \rightarrow B and A, followed by a single step of \rightarrow E. For example, the following proof is a proof of p \rightarrow q, p \;/\; q:

As this same idea would work for any other sentences, every instance of modus ponens is valid in minimal logic, and thus in intuitionistic and classical logics as well. So minimal and intuitionistic logics are like classical logic and K3, and unlike LP and FDE in this regard.
Conditional proof is not an argument like modus ponens; it is a rule.
A rule instance is an instance of conditional proof when it fits the following form:

That is, when a logic obeys conditional proof, whenever we have a valid argument in the logic with at least one premise, we can reach a new valid argument in the logic by removing one of its premises, and replacing the conclusion with a particular implication: the one that has the removed premise as antecedent and the original conclusion as consequent. (Recall that "antecedent" and "consequent" are special names for the left and right components of an implication, respectively.) Just as with modus ponens, some logics do obey conditional proof and others do not.

For example, classical logic does obey conditional proof. Imagine that we have a classical counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A \rightarrow B. This must be a Boolean valuation v that takes every sentence in \Gamma to \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and takes A \rightarrow B to \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The only way, though, that v(A \rightarrow B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} is if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So v is also a classical counterexample to \Gamma , A \;/\; B. That shows that whenever an argument of the form \Gamma \;/\; A \rightarrow B is invalid in classical logic, the corresponding argument \Gamma , A \;/\; B is also invalid in classical logic, which shows that classical logic obeys the rule. By identical reasoning, LP also obeys conditional proof.

K3 and FDE, on the other hand, do not. Each of these logics is reflexive, and so in particular they all validate p \;/\; p. Any logic that validates p \;/\; p and obeys conditional proof must also validate \;/\; p \rightarrow p, since the following is an instance of conditional proof:

But take a strong Kleene valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. This gives us w(p \rightarrow p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, so w is a K3 counterexample (but note well, not an LP counterexample!) to the argument \;/\; p \rightarrow p. Thus, that argument is not valid in K3, and since FDE is weaker than K3, it is not valid in those either. Since all these logics do validate p \;/\; p, though, this means that they don't obey conditional proof.

Notice too that the empty logic, while it is weaker than K3, does obey conditional proof. (Remember logicians' weird habit number 2!) Because the empty logic does not validate p \;/\; p, the fact that it also does not validate \;/\; p \rightarrow p is no obstacle to its obeying conditional proof.

How does conditional proof go in our new logics? Suppose that we have some proof of an argument \Gamma , A \;/\; B. That is, we have some proof with the conclusion B, all of whose open assumptions are contained in \Gamma , A. Then we can extend this proof with a single step of \rightarrow I, discharging any open assumptions of A there happened to be, reaching the conclusion A \rightarrow B. Since we've discharged any open assumptions of A, the resulting proof will have all its open assumptions contained in \Gamma ; thus, it is a proof of \Gamma \;/\; A \rightarrow B. For example, consider the following proof of p, q \lor r \;/\; (p \land q) \lor r:
We can extend this proof with one step of \rightarrow I\ to reach the following proof of p \;/\; (q \lor r) \rightarrow ((p \land q) \lor r):
And the same kind of thing will work no matter what sentences or proofs we're dealing with. So minimal logic, intuitionistic logic, and classical logic all obey conditional proof as well.

That all might make intuitionistic and minimal logics seem more like classical logic than our various other logics: after all, of the logics that we've seen, only these three both validate all instances of modus ponens and obey conditional proof. But we can turn the point around: those other logics all share certain similarities with classical logic that intuitionistic and minimal logic don't share.
Intuitionistic logic and minimal logic, however, validate only the last three of these, not the first.

As always, by this I mean that there are some instances of the first that are invalid in these logics. (Some other instances remain valid.) For an example, 
eg (p \land q) \;/\; 
eg p \lor 
eg q is invalid in intuitionistic logic, but valid in every matrix logic we've looked at in these readings so far.

(It is possible, however to make matrix logics where this fails. Indeed, intuitionistic logic itself is a matrix logic! We won't look at a matrix for intuitionistic logic, however, since all such matrices require an infinite structure, which can be a pain. We'll look at a different way to build models for intuitionistic logic in the next lecture.)
It can help to look at a proof of this argument in NK to see what's up:

Note the uses of the rule LEM here. That means that this is only a proof in NK, not also in NJ or NM. (As there is also a use of \bot E, that's another reason this isn't a proof in NM.) On its own, that gives no guarantee that there isn't some other proof in NJ of this argument; but in fact there isn't. And I think, by meditating on this piece of the above proof, you can start to see why that's so:
If we wanted to discharge both the p and the q, we could even do that, arriving here for example:

This is still not a proof of 
eg (p \land q) \;/\; 
eg p \lor 
eg q; it is instead a proof of 
eg (p \land q) \;/\; p ightarrow 
eg q. In intuitionistic and minimal logic, assuming 
eg (p \land q) is enough to get us to the conclusion that p implies 
eg q, but it's still not enough to get us to the conclusion 
eg p \lor 
eg q. That means that there's an important difference in these logics between 
eg A \lor B and A ightarrow B: these are very much not interchangeable in intuitionistic and minimal logic.

So there are commonalities intuitionistic logic has with classical logic that are not shared with the other matrix logics we've looked at, and there are commonalities those other logics have with classical logic that are not shared by intuitionistic logic. Intuitionistic and minimal logics thus give us a new kind of family of logics, different from the ones we've seen before. (Logics like these are often called "constructive" logics.)

This week focused on taking some stock of our proof systems, and the resulting logics. We've seen how to get a logic from a proof system, learned a little bit about some of the properties of intuitionistic and minimal logic, and looked at two key philosophical ideas: one about realism and antirealism, and one about ideas of meaning.

So far, we've approached logics on their own, just as sets of arguments, and also through the lens of structures, valuations, matrices, and counterexamples. We're now going to shift gears again, and look at logics from a different angle.

Some of these ways are what's known as model-theoretic: they focus on relationships between the language itself and things outside the language. Our matrix methods give an example of model-theoretic methods: the structures and matrices we've used are outside the language, and the valuations we've used establish the needed relationships. Model-theoretic methods are usually linked to logics through the notion of counterexample, just as we've done. To specify a logic model-theoretically, we set up some things (in our case, valuations) and say what it is for one of those things to be a counterexample to an argument; then we count an argument as valid when none of the things is a counterexample to it. Valuations of the sort we've used are very simple models, and model-theoretic methods get much more complex. (Later in the unit, we'll see a different kind of model.) But the basic shape of these methods is just like what we've done so far.

A different kind of way to approach logics is what's known as proof-theoretic, and that's what we'll start to look at here. Proof-theoretic methods focus on working with the language itself (maybe with a few auxiliary kinds of critter), rather than on relationships to things outside the language. Proof-theoretic methods are not focussed on the notion of counterexample. To specify a logic proof-theoretically, what we do is this: we set up some things we'll call proofs, and we say what it is for one of these to be a proof of an argument. Then we count an argument as valid when at least one of these things is a proof of it.

Conceptually, then, proof-theoretic methods for finding valid arguments are a bit simpler than model-theoretic methods. To show that an argument is valid model-theoretically, we need to show that there is no model that's a counterexample to it; that requires considering all the models of a particular model system. But to show that an argument is valid proof-theoretically, all we need to do is come up with a single proof of it. There's no need to consider every possible proof.

The reverse is true, though, when we think about invalidity. To show that an argument is invalid model-theoretically, all we need to do is come up with a single counterexample to it. But to show that an argument is invalid proof-theoretically, we need to show that there is no proof in our proof system that is a proof of it.

One of the best ways to explore logics, then, is with models in one hand and proofs in the other. This allows you to shift back and forth between these tools, using whatever's the most convenient at a particular time. When you want to show that something is valid, you can use proofs. When you want to show that something is invalid, you can use models.

This time, we'll start in on the kind of proof system we'll be looking at for most of the rest of the semester, known as natural deduction systems. In this reading I'll focus on one natural deduction system. This system will give us a logic known as intuitionistic logic, and we'll spend the next weeks doing a deep dive into this proof system in particular and intuitionistic logic in general.

Natural deduction systems are called that because they give a more or less reasonable logical approximation to the kinds of deductive reasoning people sometimes actually do. In particular, when people are reasoning through something, it's common to say something like "Well, suppose BLAH. Then SUCH AND SUCH, and so THIS OTHER THING would happen too". Focus in here on the "suppose". Here, we can just take any old sentence, and try it out and see what follows from it. For example, consider the following line of reasoning:
Suppose the moon were made of green cheese. Then it would be a dairy product.

Here, the reasoner is asking you to consider the moon's being made of green cheese, and claiming that its being a dairy product would follow. They're not actually claiming that the moon is made of green cheese, just supposing it. Even though this is a ridiculous supposition, there's nothing out of order in supposing it to see what would follow. Similarly, they're not actually claiming that the moon is a dairy product, only that if we suppose the moon to be made of green cheese, then its being a dairy product would follow from that supposition.

Natural deduction systems imitate this process; supposing things is a key part of how they work, and we can suppose absolutely anything. The main proof system we'll work with is called NJ, although we'll also consider a few variants of it. In NJ, you can include any sentence in a proof, without incurring commitment to that sentence. You can just try it out and see what follows from it.

(NJ was invented by Gerhard Gentzen, who was instrumental in the development of proof theoretic methods in logic. Gentzen was not only a Nazi but even a member of the Sturmabteilung (aka the Brownshirts, the predecessor to the SS) and later the NSDDB (the Nazi party organization aimed at controlling universities). So truly a despicable person.)

In fact, the most basic building block for proofs in NJ is just that: taking a sentence and supposing it. You do that just by writing the sentence down. Imagine I wanted to suppose, say, p \land (q \rightarrow r). I would do that like so:
What appears above---just the sentence p \land (q \rightarrow r) by itself---is our first natural deduction proof.

Every natural deduction proof has a top and a bottom. The top tells us what we're supposing, and the bottom is the conclusion. For such a small proof as this one, the top and the bottom are the very same sentence. So you should read this proof as supposing p \land (q \rightarrow r), taking it for granted for the purposes of exploration, and noting that, if we make such a supposition, we can conclude p \land (q \rightarrow r). That is, if we're willing to take a sentence for granted, we can conclude it.
Most proofs, unsurprisingly, are more complex than this. But this is the key starting point for our proofs: almost all proofs begin by simply taking some sentence for granted in this way.

Natural deduction systems allow you to build up more complex proofs from less complex ones by using rules. Rules in natural deduction systems add on to the bottom of existing proofs, yielding new conclusions. (Sometimes, they also fiddle with the top of existing proofs; more on that in a moment.) Although they're called "rules", they really function more like building blocks. Proofs are built up out of rules.

For each of our connectives, NJ includes two kinds of rules: introduction rules and elimination rules. Introduction rules for a connective let us draw a conclusion with that connective as its main connective.
