Elimination rules for a connective let us take an existing proof whose conclusion has that connective as its main connective, and build further from it.
For example, \land has one introduction rule and two elimination rules. Here they are:

You should read these rules as follows. First, take \land E_l. This says that if you already have a proof with a conclusion of the form A \land B---that is, a conclusion that is a conjunction---then you can extend that proof at the bottom with a step of \land E_l, giving you a proof of A. (As always, remember that A, B, and such are not sentences! They are used here in presenting the rule to let you know that you can do this whatever conjunction you have.)

So, for example, take our earlier proof: just the plain assumption of p \land (q \rightarrow r). Since this is a proof whose conclusion is a conjunction, we can extend it via \land E_l. That would give us the following proof:

This is a proof with p \land (q \rightarrow r) at the top and p at the bottom. You should read it as saying that if we take p \land (q \rightarrow r) for granted, to explore what follows from it, then we can find that p follows from it.
The rule \land E_r is very similar, but it allows us to conclude the right component of the conjunction rather than the left. So a new example proof using \land E_r might look like this:
This proof begins by assuming (p \lor q) \land (r \rightarrow \neg r), and then extends this assumption with a step of \land E_r, concluding its right component r \rightarrow \neg r.

The rule \land I is only a bit more complex. Since it has two sentences above the line, this rule combines two preexisting proofs into a new one. It can be used to combine any two proofs: if the conclusion of the left proof is A and the conclusion of the right proof is B, then the conclusion of the resulting proof will be A \land B. For example, we can use this rule to combine the two most recent example proofs into a larger proof, giving us this:
This proof takes two things for granted: p \land (q \rightarrow r) and (p \lor q) \land (r \rightarrow \neg r). Supposing these things, it shows that p \land (r \rightarrow \neg r) follows.

I'll call p \land (q \rightarrow r) and (p \lor q) \land (r \rightarrow \neg r) the open assumptions of this proof, and p \land (r \rightarrow \neg r) its conclusion. (We'll see what's "open" about open assumptions in just a moment.) Try not to get too confused by the word "conclusion": we're now using this word in two ways. In one use, which you've had for a few weeks now, it picks out a particular place in an argument. In this new use I'm telling you about now, it picks out a particular place in a proof. Since arguments and proofs are different kinds of things, these are two different uses. The uses are very similar, though, and as we'll see next time they're also closely related. So I think it's not too awful to use the same word for both. (Do note, though, that I'm definitely not using the same word for the open assumptions of a proof and the premises of an argument. More on that, too, next time.)

In a moment, I'll introduce introduction and elimination rules for our other standard connectives. Before we go on to that, though, it's worth pausing to think about where rules like the conjunction rules above might come from.

These rules give us a different kind of way than we've yet seen to assign a meaning to conjunction. Remember, in our language \land is just a binary connective, just the same kind of thing as \lor or \rightarrow . There's nothing specially "conjunctiony" about it until we give it some meaning somehow. In the first half of the semester, we looked at some ways to assign meaning to connectives via structures. By choosing one table rather than another for conjunction, we were giving a picture of how its meaning operates.

Now, though, there are no structures or valuations in view. Within natural deduction systems, we build proofs out of sentences themselves, not out of other things like values or whatever. It's the rules we assign to each connective that give us a picture of its meaning.

Indeed, there is a standard kind of picture some logicians bring to natural deduction, according to which it is the introduction rules that really give meanings to our sentences, with the elimination rules following along as a kind of afterthought. A slogan here is "[t]he meaning of each [connective] is to be given by specifying, for any sentence in which that [connective] is the main [connective], what is to count as a proof of that sentence".

I admit this is maybe not a very catchy slogan. The quote is from a book called Elements of Intuitionism by Michael Dummett (p. 8). Dummett was an English philosopher in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. He was, to say the least, an interesting cat.
Among his works you can find some of the very deepest reflections on logic, work on voting systems, dedication to antiracist activism, an attempt to reunite the Catholic and Eastern Orthodox Churches, and several books on the history of playing cards in general and tarot cards in particular.

On this way of thinking, then, it is \land I that gives meaning to conjunctions, by telling us that to prove a conjunction, we provide a proof of its left component and a proof of its right component, and combine these proofs via \land I itself.

What about \land E_l and \land E_r, then? For now, I'll just try to give the general impression; we'll return to this idea, though, before too long. If the idea is that \land I tells us what it takes to have a proof of a conjunction, then when we do have a proof of a conjunction, it must at least implicitly contain a proof of each component. The rules \land E_l and \land E_r, then, simply allow us to extract these proofs of components from a proof of a conjunction.

The general idea here is explained by Sara Negri as follows: "Whatever follows from the sufficient grounds for deriving a formula must follow from that formula". Sara Negri is an Italian logician who has been central to 21st-century developments in proof theory. Her work has significantly extended the range of logics that can be covered by well-behaved proof systems. This quotation is from her paper "A normalizing system of natural deduction for intuitionistic linear logic".

Just like conjunction, our other connectives receive introduction and elimination rules in NJ. The introduction rules for disjunction allow us to prove a disjunction when we have a proof of one component or of the other:

You should read these rules just like the conjunction rules. For example, \lor I_l says that if you have a proof of any sentence A, you can extend that proof with a step of \lor I_l to become a proof of A \lor B. Here, B can be any sentence at all.
So, for example, the following is a proof that uses every rule we have so far:

This proof has two open assumptions: p \rightarrow q and s \land (r \rightarrow q). Taking these two sentences for granted, the proof works its way through to the conclusion (s \land t) \lor (p \rightarrow q).

You might notice that there is a quicker way to reach this conclusion, if you're willing to take these things for granted. In fact, you don't even need to take s \land (r \rightarrow q) for granted at all! You could instead reason as follows:

This reaches the same conclusion as the longer example, and takes nothing for granted beyond what the longer example did. Indeed, it even takes less for granted. So in some sense it might seem more efficient: taking less for granted, and using fewer steps of proof. There is indeed an important relationship between these two examples, and it's something we'll be studying a bit later. For now, though, only one aspect of this is important for you to keep track of: both of these are perfectly good proofs. There is nothing "wrong" with the first one, just because the second is shorter and has fewer assumptions. You can think of the rules that compose our proofs as building blocks; your role in composing a proof is to make sure you fit these blocks together as the rules allow. Whether you build a large building or a small one is up to you!

Now let's turn to disjunction's elimination rule. This introduces the final new idea we need this week: the idea of discharging an assumption. When we discharge an assumption, that means that we stop assuming it, stop taking it for granted. Without the ability to discharge an assumption, we could only ever take more things for granted as we go. In fact, though, in a natural deduction proof it is typical to have different assumptions in play in different parts of the proof, and to be able to remove open assumptions as we go.

The idea behind disjunction elimination is pretty intuitive. Take an example like this: imagine you want to know whether or not a local friend is currently at home. Imagine as well that you do know the following: either they're out visiting their parent, or they're overseas on holiday. Then it's pretty straightforward: wherever they are, they're not at home!

Let's look at this reasoning a bit more deeply. First, suppose they're out visiting their parent. It would follow from this that they're not at home. Or, alternatively, suppose they're overseas. Again, it would follow from this that they're not at home. Now, you may not know which of those two suppositions obtains. But since you know that at least one of them does (since you know that either they're visiting or they're overseas), you know enough to conclude that they're not at home. What's more, you can conclude this outright: you're not still assuming that they're out visiting their parent, for example. That was just a supposition along the way. By the time you conclude that they're not at home, you're done taking this for granted. Similarly for the assumption that they're overseas. You took it for granted as a step along the way, but you're not still taking it for granted when you're done. You've concluded that they're not at home, but you're not making any assumptions going forward about where in particular they are.

That's an example of disjunction elimination. To draw a conclusion C from a disjunction A \lor B, we can do the following: first, suppose A and show that C would follow; second, suppose B instead, and show that C would again follow. Having completed these two steps, we conclude that C follows from A \lor B. In this reasoning, we suppose A just for the first step; and we suppose B just for the second step. Once we conclude that C follows from A \lor B, we're no longer supposing either of these.
We put this in schematic form as follows:

This schematic form introduces some new notation, but it's just a way to express the ideas explained in the last few paragraphs. There are three pieces of notation here that are new: the \vdots , the [square brackets], and the superscript ^n. I'll explain each of these in turn. In order to do this, it's handy to look at a concrete example proof that uses the rule \lor E:

If you compare this example proof to the schematic presentation of the \lor E rule, you'll see that here I've used the sentence p for A, the sentence r \land t for B, and the sentence r \lor p for C. The \vdots in the schematic presentation have here been replaced with actual bits of proof. That's all the \vdots means: just "any bit of proof goes here". The dots \vdots should never occur in any actual proof.

Square brackets around an assumption tell us that the assumption has been discharged. When an assumption has been discharged, it no longer counts as an open assumption; we're no longer taking it for granted. So the above example proof has only one open assumption: q \land (p \lor (r \land t)).

The assumptions p and r \land t had been open during the creation of this proof, but they are closed by the time we get to the end. That is, you can think of the rule \lor E as combining three proofs into one. In this case, two of these proofs being combined are:

The left proof here has p as an open assumption, and the right proof has r \land t as an open assumption. It is only when these proofs are brought in to the larger proof via \lor E that these assumptions are discharged, and no longer count as open. That's what's marked by the square brackets.

Finally, the superscript ^n from the schematic presentation is instantiated as a superscript ^1 in the above example proof. This is what tells us, if we just have access to the final proof, exactly when the assumption was still active, and when it was discharged. Note that the ^1 on each discharged assumption matches the ^1 on the rule \lor E, in the example proof. That match tells us that these discharges were performed by this rule. Since we can have proofs with many different occurrences of rules that can discharge sentences, this is necessary to be able to tell exactly what steps went in to constructing the proof.

Every occurrence of a rule that can discharge assumptions must always have a number as a superscript; and if a proof has more than one occurrence of such a rule, each occurrence must use a different number.

(If we were very concerned with being maximally permissive, there would be ways to slightly liberalize this, to allow reuse of numbers in certain tricky-to-specify circumstances where they wouldn't interfere with each other. But it's not like we're going to run out of numbers, and it's simpler just to make this requirement.)

It's not important, though, what numbers we use; their arithmetical properties don't come into this at all. We're just using numbers because they're a convenient stock of symbols that we're not otherwise using. So you could just as well give the example proof with ^{23} everywhere instead of ^1; it would amount to the same. What's important is only which superscripts match which other superscripts. In practice I'll tend to use smallish numbers, but nothing at all hangs on this.

The only way a sentence can ever be discharged in a proof is by a rule that has the ability to discharge built in, like \lor E. So every time a discharged assumption appears in a proof, it must always have a superscript number that matches the number of some occurrence in the proof of a discharging rule. That's how we know that the discharge was done by that rule.

It will take some practice for you to get the hang of discharging; this is really where the challenge of natural deduction is. It's like anything else; keep trying, improving from your mistakes as you go!
For now, though, let's move on to our remaining connectives. Implication has one introduction rule and one elimination rule:
As you can see, the rule \rightarrow I involves discharge, and the rule \rightarrow E does not.

Let's start with the simpler \rightarrow E, then. This rule says that whenever you have a proof of an implication, and you also have a proof of its antecedent, then you can put those proofs together with a step of \rightarrow E, giving you a proof of the consequent. For example, here is a proof that uses \rightarrow E:

This proof has the open assumption (p \rightarrow (q \land t)) \land p, and from that draws the conclusion t. Note that, although the proof includes two occurrences of the open assumption (p \rightarrow (q \land t)) \land p, when I'm listing out the open assumptions of the proof I don't bother to list it twice. For our purposes, something is either assumed in a proof or it's not; we're not keeping track of number of occurrences.

However, some of the coolest work going in proof theory at the moment works differently here; it is sensitive to the number of occurrences of an open assumption. We won't go into it in these notes, but I can't come this close to the idea without mentioning it; there's too much good stuff over here! If you're interested in learning more, you might start by searching "substructural logic" or "linear logic", or checking out the Stanford Encyclopedia entry on substructural logic. (This article is by Greg Restall, a well-known Australian logician. Greg was one of the people I was coming to study with when I first came to Australia. Alas, he now lives in Scotland.)

Unlike \rightarrow E, \rightarrow I is a discharging rule. To see why, it helps to think about implication in a new kind of way. You might recall from earlier that it's difficult to give what seems like a good truth table for implication. But now that we're working with proofs, we don't actually need to say anything about truth values at all in order to give meaning to our connectives. This gives us a different kind of way to explore the meaning of a connective like implication.

An influential theory of implication holds that an implication like p \rightarrow q has the same kind of meaning as a proof of q that assumes p. On this kind of theory, to believe p \rightarrow q is to believe in some path of reasoning that can take you from p to q.

This kind of theory of "if...then" sentences has been around for a while, but nowadays is gaining new life by connecting up to cognitive science pretty directly. Interesting work in this direction is being done by a number of researchers. One particularly excellent researcher in this area is Karolina Krzyżanowska; if you're interested I'd encourage you to check out some of the papers on her website: http://karolinakrzyzanowska.com.

It's this kind of theory of implication that's encoded in our rules. For the rule \rightarrow E, look at it this way: if we have a proof of p \rightarrow q, then that licenses us to reason from p to q. So if we also have a proof of p, then we can combine the proofs we have to get a proof of q. For the rule \rightarrow I, look at it this way: what it should take to prove p \rightarrow q is to produce a proof of q from the assumption p. In this process, you take p for granted in the course of the proof of q; but once you produce that proof, your final conclusion is not q itself, but just that p implies q. As you don't any longer assume p when you draw this final conclusion, you can discharge the assumption. There are other ways we might encode a theory like this into proof rules; but this is at least one standard way to do so.

That, anyway, is the kind of view of implication that lies behind these rules: thinking of implication as recording in a single sentence the same kind of connection between distinct sentences that we build up in the course of a proof. So, for example, we can have a proof that uses \rightarrow I like so:
This proof is the first example we've seen of a proof with no open assumptions. The total proof takes nothing at all for granted, and still produces a proof of (p \land q) \rightarrow p.

Of course, when I say it takes nothing for granted, I'm not taking account of the rules themselves! These might seem to come from nowhere, and there's definitely an important sense in which we've been taking them for granted as well. Changing the rules gives us different proof systems. So when I say we're taking nothing for granted in this proof, I mean within the current proof system. Like any other aspect of logic, though, we're always free to choose or build a different system!
Let's turn now to \bot . Recall that this is meant to mean like THE FALSEST THING POSSIBLE: MAXIMUM FALSE.

Although we didn't spend much time focussed on it while we were working with structures and valuations, it's pretty simple to handle in that kind of setting: since \bot is a nullary connective, each structure must assign a value to \bot , and then \bot takes that value in every valuation on that structure. To give \bot its intended meaning within a structure, we just need to choose some falsey value within that structure.

When it comes to proofs, though, things are a bit more delicate. The reason is that proofs, at least as we're dealing with them here, aren't really about ruling anything out. They're instead focused on demonstrating that something does hold, or assuming it. There's not a lot negative about our proofs: they're pretty relentlessly positive. So how, in this kind of setting, can we give rules to make \bot into MAXIMUM FALSE?

The method NJ uses is twofold, involving (as usual) introduction and elimination rules. It's particularly simple on the introduction side: \bot simply has no introduction rules at all. What would it take to prove \bot ? It's so false that it can't be done.
The flip side of this is the elimination rule:

Remember, A can be any sentence at all. So this elimination rule says that \bot is so false that if we ever did have a proof of \bot , fuck it, we might as well have a proof of anything at all. And indeed we can extend any proof of \bot to a proof of anything at all via this rule.

Now, you might wonder, if \bot has no introduction rule at all, can we ever be in a position to apply its elimination rule? And the answer, maybe surprisingly, is yes! The reason is that we can assume any sentence we like. Since \bot is a sentence, we can assume it. It also occurs in more complex sentences, and we can assume those as well, or even prove some of them. So here is a proof using \bot E:
Overall, this proof has no open assumptions; the assumption of \bot is discharged at the last step. But it still uses the rule \bot E; the \bot came into the proof via assumption.
Here's another example proof using \bot E:

Here, there is no assumption of \bot directly anywhere in the proof, not even a discharged one. Instead, there is an (open) assumption of (q \land r) \rightarrow \bot ; that's how \bot got in to this proof. Note as well that \bot E allows us to conclude any sentence at all; since \bot is a sentence, that includes \bot itself, as this proof shows!
And for one more example, here is a third example proof using \bot E:
Here \bot doesn't occur in any open assumption at all; it's rather part of the internal workings of the proof.

So even though there's no introduction rule for \bot , it can still end up in our proofs, because of how assumptions work. Since assumptions are totally unconstrained, they can bring \bot in, either directly, or as part of a more complex assumption; and there are times when \bot can come into a proof without figuring in any assumption.
When it comes to verum, things are simpler. Remember that \top is meant to mean MAXIMUM TRUE: THE TRUEST THING. So it should be very easy to prove. And indeed it is, via its introduction rule:

Note that there is nothing above the line here; an application of \top I counts as a proof of \top from no assumptions at all. It's just directly a proof in its own right. So this rule gives us another way to start a proof, besides assuming a sentence. (So far, though, those are the only two ways we can begin; all our other rules only build up bigger proofs from already-existing ones.)

And just like \bot has no introduction rule, \top has no elimination rule. The idea is something like this: \top is just so stinking true that knowing that it's true gives you no information at all. Thus, it doesn't allow you to go on to conclude anything in particular.
You can still continue a proof past a use of \top I; you just have to do it with some other rule. For example, here is a proof:

The last connective to look at is negation. Negation is the reason that \bot is so important in NJ. Negation, after all, presents the same kind of problem as \bot did: how can we say that something is not the case, when our proofs are all about assuming or demonstrating what is the case? We saw above how NJ handles that issue in the case of \bot . In the case of \neg , NJ simply reuses that same solution, deferring everything back to \bot itself.
Here, then, are the rules for negation:

Both of these rules involve  ot . You can see 
eg I as saying that, if from the assumption of A we can prove our way to  ot , then A itself must be false, so we can discharge the assumption and conclude 
eg A. And you can see 
eg E as saying that any contradiction suffices for MAXIMUM FALSE: if we have proofs of both 
eg A and A, then we can put them together via 
eg E to get a proof of  ot . (So this gives us yet another way  ot can get into proofs.)

Consider, for example, the following line of reasoning, inspired by Zeno of Elea, as an argument against the possibility of motion. Nowadays this is sometimes called one of Zeno's "paradoxes", but it appears that in its original use it was not meant to be a paradox, but simply a straightforward argument for the conclusion that nothing moves. Zeno was a follower of Parmenides, and like Parmenides held that everything is one, and does not change. Since motion is a kind of change, this includes denying the existence of motion.

Suppose someone, call them Mo, has moved some distance D. Before Mo could move the full distance D, they must first have moved a distance of D/2. And before they could have done that, they must first have moved a distance of D/4. Before they could have done that, however, they must first have moved a distance of D/8. And so on, all the way to infinity. So Mo must have moved an infinite number of distances. But this is impossible: nobody could move an infinite number of distances. So the supposition must have been wrong: Mo didn't really move any distance D at all.

Some of this reasoning we don't have the power to capture in NJ. In particular, the reasoning that turns on "And so on, all the way to infinity" doesn't seem to nicely fit into the resources we have available. But we can still fit the overall shape of this reasoning, in its broad outlines, into NJ. Consider, for example, the following proof:

To see the structure of the Zenoish argument reflected in this proof, think of m as the claim that Mo moved distance D, and i as the claim that Mo moved an infinite number of distances. Then the proof takes two things for granted: that Mo's having moved distance D implies Mo having moved an infinite number of distances, and that Mo didn't move an infinite number of distances. From these assumptions, the proof concludes that Mo didn't move distance D. The shape of the proof roughly aligns with the reasoning in the passage: the main difference is that the prose passage gives us some reason to believe m \rightarrow i, where the proof just takes it for granted.

That, then, is our full proof system NJ. Getting comfortable with when and where you can discharge assumptions is probably the trickiest part about natural deduction, but it's also what gives natural deduction its power. The best way for you to get the hang of it is simple, if not necessarily easy: practice making proofs! You have a number of exercises at https://binchicken.one that involve you in constructing proofs, so you will get plenty of practice.

Another website to check out: https://incredible.pm is a website with a very nice interface for building up natural deduction proofs. The proofs there don't look like our proofs here; they're scattered and involve connecting up wires. But deep down they really are just the same, and practice there will give you important perspective on how to think about proofs in general and discharging assumptions in particular.

In this section, I'm going to include a number of examples of correct proofs and incorrect proofs involving discharges, to help give you a clearer idea about how they work. When you find yourself with a question about what you can or can't do with discharges in a proof, come back to these examples and have a look; it may be that there's an example here that answers your question!

fr:title.text: Correct proof: extra assumptions, multiple discharge

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Correct proof: extra assumptions, multiple discharge

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This proof demonstrates a few things about discharge. First, note the presence of the open assumption r. This doesn't interfere with the discharge steps at all; indeed, it doesn't interact in any way with them. Discharging p and p \rightarrow ((p \land r) \rightarrow q) both go ahead just fine, even though there is another open assumption hanging around. Discharges work like this in general: whether you're allowed to discharge a sentence never depends on what other sentences you have as open assumptions.

Second, note that at the step marked ^2 this proof discharges two instances of the assumption p. That's fine: when you're allowed to discharge a sentence, you're allowed to discharge any number of copies of that sentence. (This is another case where doing things differently would lead us into the fascinating world of substructural logics.)

This proof includes two occurrences of the discharge rule \rightarrow I. These are labeled appropriately with numbers, as all occurrences of discharge rules must be. Note, however, that they don't actually discharge anything!

Recall that logicians tend to be a cheeky bunch, and revisit this phrase from the last example: "when you're allowed to discharge a sentence, you're allowed to discharge any number of copies of that sentence". Well, "any number" here includes zero!

In the above proof, the step labeled ^1 discharges zero occurrences of the assumption q, and the step labeled ^2 discharges zero occurrences of the assumption p. (You can tell that it's q and p, respectively, because of the antecedents of the introduced implications.) Note too that ^2 has discharged zero occurrences of p even though the proof actually has an occurrence of p available to discharge. That's fine; nothing says that you must discharge everything you can. So the above example proof still has p as an open assumption.
For contrast, consider the following proof:

This second proof does discharge the assumption p at the step marked ^2, and so has no open assumptions. Again, it discharges zero occurrences of q at the step marked ^1. Both of these are fine proofs. They are, however, two different proofs, and the difference between them is important.

Note that, in both of these, I've given a numeric discharge label to every step of \rightarrow I, even when these steps don't discharge anything. Every discharge rule must always have a label: one way we see that these rules aren't discharging anything is by seeing that there are no discharged assumptions with matching labels.

fr:title.text: Correct proof: interweaving discharges, choosing occurrences to discharge

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Correct proof: interweaving discharges, choosing occurrences to discharge

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Each of the discharge rules in this proof discharges only some of what it could.
Consider the state of the proof when it reaches the step \lor E^1. Before we decide exactly how to discharge p with this rule, we're looking at something like this:

Here, I've marked the three open assumptions of p with the symbols \star , \dagger , \ddagger for reference. Since we can choose whether or not to discharge anything available for discharge, we have some options: we can choose to discharge both \star and \dagger , one of those but not the other, or neither.

We don't have total freedom here, though. Even if we wanted to, we could not discharge \ddagger . The reason is that the rule of \lor E is specific about where we can discharge: when we're eliminating a disjunction, we can discharge its left component in the left subproof and its right component in the right subproof. So we cannot choose to discharge the \ddagger occurrence of p at this step. The others, though, are fair game. (I've assumed we're going to discharge the q at this stage, although that too is optional.)

In the example proof, I chose to discharge just the \star occurrence of p at this step, leaving the \dagger occurrence open. Later in the proof, we reach the step labeled \rightarrow I^2, and again face a choice about what to discharge. Here, both the open assumptions \dagger and \ddagger are fair game; I chose to discharge the \dagger one only.

So you can pick and choose which occurrences of an assumption to discharge, and even if you choose to leave an assumption open at one stage in building a proof, you can discharge that assumption later on.

fr:title.text: Incorrect proof: clashing and missing discharge labels

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Incorrect proof: clashing and missing discharge labels

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Since that last example proof was a bit complex, I'm going to make most of the examples of incorrect proof by varying it as little as possible, to give more of a sense of what's vital about it.

One thing that's gone wrong here: both the step of \lor E and one step of \rightarrow I have the same label ^1. That means that we can't tell, when a discharged assumption is labeled 1, where it was meant to be discharged. In this case, either one could be fine---but that's not enough. There must be, for every discharged assumption, a unique place it was discharged. So every discharge rule needs its own label; labels can't be reused in this way.

Another thing that's gone wrong here: both the final \rightarrow I and one of the discharged assumptions lack numeric labels altogether. Every discharge rule must have a label, however, and every discharged assumption must have one as well. So this is no good. In this case, just providing matching labels to these would be enough; the rule is almost applied correctly. It just needs to be labeled correctly as well.
This non-proof attempts to discharge an occurrence of p that isn't an open assumption, because it's not an assumption at all! That's no good; the only things we can discharge are assumptions.

Once you know what you're looking for, this is an easy kind of mistake to spot. No correct proof will ever have square brackets in the middle like this. All discharged assumptions must be assumptions, and so there won't be any proof work above them.

fr:title.text: Incorrect proof: discharging from the wrong place

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Incorrect proof: discharging from the wrong place

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is not a proof because it hasn't correctly handled the \lor E discharge. The rule \lor E allows for discharges in two of its subproofs, but not willy-nilly: the left component of the disjunction being eliminated can only be discharged in the left subproof, and the right component only in the right subproof. In this case, we have an occurrence of p being discharged in the right subproof; since p isn't the right component of the eliminated disjunction, that's not allowed.
Here's a simpler example of the same kind of problem:

Here, the step of \rightarrow I tries to discharge an occurrence of p that isn't allowed. The reason is that \rightarrow I, like every other rule, can only act on what happens above it in the proof. Here, it can only act on the assumption of q. The assumption of p isn't in view.

fr:title.text: Incorrect proof: discharging the wrong sentence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Incorrect proof: discharging the wrong sentence

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Finally, here's a quick variation on that last one.

Here, the discharged q is in a fine location to be discharged by the \rightarrow I; that's not the problem. The problem is that \rightarrow I has to follow a strict pattern: the consequent of the introduced implication must be the sentence immediately above the \rightarrow I, and only the antecedent of the introduced implication can be discharged. This case meets the first restriction, since q is the sentence immediately before the \rightarrow I, and q is the consequent of the introduced implication. But this case does not meet the second restriction: since p is the antecedent of the introduced implication, only p can be discharged by this step. Discharging q instead doesn't work.
I hope these examples help give you some idea about how discharge works in NJ. Practice is the best way forwards from here!

Finally, I'll close by telling you about two simple variations on this proof system. NJ itself will form the main proof system we look at, but it'll be handy to know about these two variations as well.

The first variation is called NM. It's very simple: it's exactly the same as NJ, except that it doesn't include the rule \bot E. The connective \bot is still present in the language. In NM, this connective has no rules of any sort associated with it. Everything else is just the same as in NJ. So any proof you make in NJ, if it happens not to use the rule \bot E, is also a proof in NM. And every proof in NM is itself a proof in NJ directly.
The second variation is called NK. Instead of taking away from NJ, as NM does, the system NK adds to it. In particular, NK includes every rule of NJ, plus also the following rule:

This rule is called "LEM" for the Law of Excluded Middle. It allows us to prove any sentence of the form A \lor \neg A, out of nowhere, anywhere we like in a proof. It's kind of like a specialized rule of assumption, except that it does not involve any open assumptions. It counts as an outright proof of such sentences; like \top I, it creates a new proof from nothing.
For example, here is a proof in NK that is not also a proof in NJ:

This is a proof of (p \rightarrow r) \lor (q \rightarrow r), with the sole open assumption (p \land q) \rightarrow r. We'll think next time about the relation between proof systems and logics. It will emerge there that NJ, NM, and NK all determine different logics from each other. Two of these logics are new to us: NJ determines a logic called "intuitionistic logic", and NM one called "minimal logic". But NK determines a logic we've already met in our earlier lectures. More on this next time!
This time, we learned about proofs in the natural deduction system NJ, as well as two close relative systems NM and NK.
Proofs are made up of rules, and you can use whatever rules you like to build a proof, so long as the rules are used correctly.
Here's a summary of all the rules we've seen:

html:ul.html:li: The rule of assumption: just write down a sentence to assume it, giving a proof of that sentence from itself.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: NJ has all the rules in NM, plus:
NK has all the rules in NJ, plus:

We've now spent a few weeks looks at logics determined by matrices, and some of the ways we can use those matrices to learn about these logics. The aim this time is to think about the limits of this approach: which logics can we reach via matrices, and which can't we?
Key to this lecture is the idea of a matrix logic:
A matrix logic is a logic that is determined by some matrix.
For example, classical logic, K3, LP, and FDE are all examples of matrix logics. For each of these, we've seen a matrix that determines the logic.

Remember, though, that a matrix logic is a kind of logic, and a logic is just a set of arguments. So even when we know about a logic through some mechanism other than a matrix, it still might be a matrix logic.

For example, recall the universal logic, the logic that contains every argument. When you first met this logic, we hadn't thought about matrices at all yet, or even structures or valuations! I introduced you to this logic directly, without any detour through matrices, because it's an easy example of a set of arguments.

But despite this, the universal logic is a matrix logic. To see this, consider a one-valued structure. (There are no choices here; there is only one one-valued structure, where every connective just gives the same value back.) And consider the matrix built on this structure where the one value is designated. The logic determined by this matrix is the universal logic.
How could you know that the logic determined by this matrix is the universal logic?

Take any argument \Gamma \;/\; A. A counterexample to this argument in the matrix in question would have to be a valuation v in the one-valued structure where:
for everyA in \Gamma , the value v(B) is designated, and
v(A) is not designated. But this is impossible: there is only one value, so v(A) must be that value, which is designated in this matrix. So there are no counterexamples to any argument, and so the logic determined by this matrix is the universal logic.
So the universal logic is a matrix logic, since it is determined by some matrix.

That example helps me make another important point: although every matrix determines one particular logic, we can't in general go the other way. When a logic is a matrix logic, there is not just one matrix that determines it; a logic can be determined by many different matrices. (I won't go through the reasoning here, but in fact every matrix logic is determined by infinitely many different matrices.)

For example, consider a very different matrix: this time, take the strong Kleene structure, and consider the matrix on that structure that uses \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}\} as its set of designated values. This matrix also determines the universal logic, by essentially the same reasoning as before; but it is a very different matrix, built on a completely different structure.

So when we say that a logic is a matrix logic, this doesn't mean that there is any particular matrix pinned down by the logic. It just means that there is at least one matrix, somewhere out there, that determines the logic.

To set the stage for the rest of this lecture, let's also look at the empty logic, the logic that contains no arguments. This too was one of the first logics you met in these lectures. But unlike the universal logic, the empty logic is not a matrix logic. That is, there is absolutely no matrix that determines the empty logic.

At first, it might seem hard to see how we could know that. Structures themselves come in a bewildering variety, and then almost every structure underlies many different matrices. So how could we know that none of these options ever determines the empty logic?

Here's one way. Think about the argument p \;/\; p. Like all arguments, this argument is invalid in the empty logic. So if the empty logic were a matrix logic, there would be some matrix in which this argument has a counterexample.

Now I'll show, however, that that can't be the case. Imagine we have some matrix, and some valuation v on the matrix's structure, so that v is a counterexample to p \;/\; p in the matrix. What would we know about v(p)?

Well, since we're imagining that v is a counterexample to p \;/\; p, we would know that v(p), whatever it is, would have to be designated in the matrix, since p is a premise of the argument p \;/\; p. And we would also know that v(p), whatever it is, would have to be undesignated in the matrix, since p is the conclusion of the argument p \;/\; p. But no value in any matrix could ever be both designated and undesignated, so this just isn't possible. That is, there is no counterexample to p \;/\; p in any matrix.

That means, though, that the argument p \;/\; p must be valid in every matrix logic. And since this argument is invalid in the empty logic, it follows that the empty logic is not a matrix logic. This means that the techniques we learned in the last lecture that let us use matrices to explore the logics determined by those matrices, those techniques won't work to help us learn about the empty logic, since there is no matrix that determines it.

Now, the empty logic is very simple, so this isn't a big problem; we can probably answer any questions we have about the empty logic in some other way. But it reveals a bigger issue: matrices are just not flexible enough to handle all logics; only certain logics are matrix logics. This bigger issue forms the basis of the rest of this lecture, which approaches the question: exactly which logics are, and which are not, matrix logics? (We won't fully answer the question in this lecture, but we'll get close.)

To start exploring this issue, we can generalise the example from above. There was nothing special about the choice of the sentence p in the argument p \;/\; p. Take any sentence A at all, and consider the argument A \;/\; A. By the same reasoning as before, there can't be any counterexample to this argument in any matrix. (Imagine there was such a counterexample v. Then v(A) would have to be both designated and undesignated, which is impossible.)
So this means that every matrix logic has to include, not only the argument p \;/\; p, but every argument with the form A \;/\; A.
A logic is reflexive iff it validates all arguments with the form A \;/\; A.
What we have seen, then, is that every matrix logic is reflexive. That already narrows down what we can use matrices for.

Consider these three logics. Which of them is reflexive?
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is p,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is in \Gamma ,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has five or fewer members,

html:ol.html:li: This logic is not reflexive; although it does contain p \;/\; p, it does not contain (for example) q \;/\; q or p \land q \;/\; p \land q.
This logic is reflexive; in every argument of the form A \;/\; A, the conclusion is one of the premises (indeed the only premise), so all such arguments are valid in this logic.
This logic is reflexive; every argument of the form A \;/\; A has exactly one premise, so it indeed has five or fewer; so all such arguments are valid in this logic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Some logics, then, are reflexive and others are not. But as we've seen, all matrix logics must be reflexive.
As an example that we'll make some use of in a moment, note that there is a reflexive logic that's weaker than every reflexive logic; let's call this one the weakest reflexive logic, or WRL:
The weakest reflexive logic, also called WRL, is the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma = \{A\}.

The definition just tells you what WRL is; it doesn't actually show that WRL really is weaker than every reflexive logic. But you now know enough to see that it is. For a logic to be reflexive means that it contains every argument of the form A \;/\; A---but these arguments are exactly the ones valid in WRL. So every argument valid in WRL is valid in any reflexive logic; that shows that WRL is weaker than every reflexive logic.
Note also that WRL is itself reflexive: it itself contains every argument of the form A \;/\; A.

But we can go further, now asking: well, is every reflexive logic a matrix logic? This is an example of how logicians learn things: once we manage to answer one question, that answer often suggests an additional, more specific, question. Over decades (but not yet quite centuries!) of work, the global community of logicians has managed to learn a ton---but there's always more. That's why puzzles are so central to what we do; it's to give you a sense of the kind of questioning and answering that is core to the actual practice of logic.

In fact, not every reflexive logic is a matrix logic; there are other things that all matrix logics have in common, things not guaranteed by reflexivity. To see this, think about WRL. WRL is reflexive, as we've seen. But is it a matrix logic?

In fact, it isn't. Take, for example, the argument p, q \;/\; p. This is invalid in WRL, since it doesn't have the form A \;/\; A. But think about what a counterexample to p, q \;/\; p in a matrix would have to do. It would have to be some valuation v where v(p) and v(q) are both designated but v(p) is undesignated. And that means that it would have to already be a counterexample to p \;/\; p, which we've seen is impossible. So WRL isn't a matrix logic, even though it is reflexive.

The reasoning there can be generalised: if a valuation v is a counterexample to any argument \Sigma \;/\; A, and \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , then v must also be a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A. This is because if v takes every member of \Sigma to a designated value, and every member of \Gamma is a member of \Sigma , then v takes every member of \Gamma to a designated value as well.

Unlike in the case of reflexivity, then, we haven't just discovered here certain arguments that must be valid in all matrix logics. We've discovered something more nuanced: a certain connection among arguments that all matrix logics must respect in a certain way. To explore this more systematically, let me pause to introduce an idea that will come in handy both here and going forward: the idea of a rule.
To get to rules, let's start with the idea of a rule instance:

fr:title.text: Rule instance, obeying a rule instance

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Rule instance, obeying a rule instance

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A rule instance has two parts: first, a set of arguments (called the premise-arguments), and second, a particular argument (called the conclusion-argument). A rule instance is written with a horizontal line, with the premise-arguments occurring above the line and the conclusion-argument below it.

A logic obeys a rule instance if either:
the rule instance has some premise-argument that is invalid in the logic, or
the rule instance's conclusion-argument is valid in the logic.
A rule instance is kind of like an argument, except that where arguments use sentences, rule instances use arguments. So we're up a level now.

Also, although officially rule instances can have any number of premise-arguments, for this lecture we will only need to think about rule instances with one or two premise-arguments. So don't worry too much about going wild here.

You can think of a rule instance as setting a kind of condition on logics; that's what the idea of obeying a rule instance is about. I like to think about it like this: a rule instance makes a demand on every logic that contains all the rule instance's premise-arguments. In particular, it demands that all those logics must contain the conclusion-argument. A logic obeys the rule instance when either the rule instance makes no demand on the logic (since some premise-argument is invalid in the logic) or else the logic meets the demand.
For example, think about the rule instance

Which of the following logics obeys this rule instance?
the logic that contains \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has exactly one member
the logic that contains \Gamma \;/\; A iff all members of \Gamma are atomic
the logic that contains \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has three or more members

html:ol.html:li: This logic does obey the rule instance, since the conclusion-argument is valid in the logic.
This logic does not obey the rule instance, since the premise-argument is valid in the logic but the conclusion-argument is not.
This logic does obey the rule instance, since there is a premise-argument that is not valid in the logic.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A rule is a set of rule instances. A logic obeys a rule iff it obeys every member of the rule.

A rule collects up a bunch of rule instances into a single package. You can think of a rule as placing a whole lot of conditions on a logic at once: one for each rule instance in it. To obey a rule, a logic has to obey all its instances.
Now that we've got the idea of a rule, let's look at our first example rule: a rule called monotonicity:
Monotonicity is the rule whose instances are those that fit the form:
where \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma .

Another way to say the same thing: a rule instance is a member of monotonicity iff:
it has one premise-argument,
the conclusion of its premise-argument matches the conclusion of its conclusion-argument, and
the set of premises of its premise-argument is a subset of the set of premises of its conclusion-argument.
A logic is monotonic iff it obeys monotonicity.

To get some practice with monotonicity, think about these logics. Which of them are monotonic?
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is p,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff A is in \Gamma ,
the logic that contains any argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff \Gamma has five or fewer members,

html:ol.html:li: This logic is monotonic. Whenever we have an instance of monotonicity, the conclusions of the premise-argument and the conclusion-argument must match. If they are both p, then the conclusion-argument is valid in the logic, so the logic obeys the instance. And if neither of them is p, then the premise-argument is not valid in the logic, so the logic obeys the instance. So the logic obeys all instances of monotonicity.
This logic is monotonic, because whenever \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma , then if A is in \Gamma , it must also be in \Sigma .

html:li.html:p: This logic is not monotonic; here is an instance of monotonicity that it does not obey:

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So a logic is monotonic iff, whenever it contains an argument \Gamma \;/\; A and \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma , then the logic contains \Sigma \;/\; A as well. And in fact, every matrix logic is monotonic. To show this, I'll proceed by a method called showing the contrapositive.

What I'll show directly is that, when A is a subset of \Sigma , then if a matrix logic invalidates \Sigma \;/\; A it must also invalidate \Gamma \;/\; A. What I want to show is that if \Gamma \;/\; A is valid then \Sigma \;/\; A must be valid too; what I do show both switches the order of these and changes from validity to invalidity. If this seems like a weird thing to do, just come along for the ride for now; hopefully it will make more sense in a paragraph or two.

Suppose, then, that we have a matrix logic where \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid. Since the logic is specified by a matrix, that must mean that there is some counterexample in the matrix to the argument \Sigma \;/\; A. And that, in turn, must mean that there is a valuation v that assigns a designated value to everything in \Sigma and also assigns an undesignated value to A. Now, supposing that \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma , this means that v must assign a designated value to everything in \Gamma as well, since everything in \Gamma is in \Sigma and everything in \Sigma gets a designated value. So v is also a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A, since it does what a counterexample must do: it assigns a designated value to every premise and an undesignated value to the conclusion.

I've now shown that if \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid then \Gamma \;/\; A is invalid (in any matrix logic, when \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma ). What I want, remember, is to show that if \Gamma \;/\; A is valid then so is \Sigma \;/\; A. But that follows: if \Gamma \;/\; A is valid, then it can't be that \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid---we just saw that when \Sigma \;/\; A is invalid then so is \Gamma \;/\; A. So when \Gamma \;/\; A is valid, that must mean that \Sigma \;/\; A is valid too. That's monotonicity.

Again, this doesn't depend on any details of the matrix: it doesn't depend on which structure is involved, or on which values are designated, or even on what language we're using. The key step in the reasoning is this: if a valuation takes every sentence in \Sigma to a designated value, and \Gamma is a subset of \Sigma , then the valuation takes every sentence in \Gamma to a designated value.

We've shown, then, that every matrix logic is both reflexive and monotonic. That means that lots of logics are not matrix logics! That is, for lots of logics there is no way of giving a matrix that determines them. Working with matrices is a powerful tool, but it only works for certain special logics.

But there's more; matrix logics are in fact even more restricted than this. Think about the following example logic, which I'll call L1 (we won't use it for more than a few paragraphs, so it doesn't need a snazzy name). L1 contains an argument \Gamma \;/\; A iff either:
A is a member of \Gamma , or
A is q, and p is a member of \Gamma , or
A is r, and q is a member of \Gamma .

Let me try to convince you first that L1 is reflexive and monotonic. For any argument of the form A \;/\; A, we indeed have that its conclusion is among its premises, so all these arguments are valid in L1 by the first dot point. So L1 is reflexive.

To see that L1 is monotonic, suppose we have any argument \Gamma \;/\; A that is valid in L1, and any set \Sigma such that \Gamma \subseteq \Sigma . Then \Gamma \;/\; A must fit one of the three conditions for L1, since that's the only way it could be valid in L1. But you can check each of these conditions, to see that if \Gamma \;/\; A fits the condition, then \Sigma \;/\; A must fit that same condition, so \Sigma \;/\; A must be valid in L1 as well. That shows that L1 is monotonic.

Despite its being reflexive and monotonic, however, L1 is not a matrix logic. Again, let's think about what a matrix that determines L1 would have to be like, to see that it's not possible. This time, the key facts we need are that p \;/\; q and p, q \;/\; r are both valid in L1, but p \;/\; r is not valid in L1. (Make sure you see that this is so.)

Since p \;/\; r is invalid in L1, if L1 is indeed determined by some matrix, then there would have to be some valuation v so that v(p) is designated in the matrix but v(r) is undesignated. But then what about v(q)? There are two possibilities: either v(q) is designated in the matrix or it is undesignated. But if it is designated, then v is also a counterexample to p, q \;/\; r, and so it does not determine L1. And if it is undesignated, then v is a counterexample to p \;/\; q, and so again it doesn't determine L1.
So we have found another constraint that matrix logics impose, besides reflexivity and monotonicity. There are simpler and more complex versions of this constraint; here we'll work with a simple one:
Transitivity is the rule whose instances are those that fit the form:
A logic is transitive iff it obeys transitivity.

(To make something explicit: the given general form of transitivity has \Gamma , A \;/\; B as the form of its second premise-argument. But every argument, as you know, has a single set of premises. So what is "\Gamma , A" here? You should read it as the union of \Gamma with \{A\}. That is, if A is already a member of \Gamma , then "\Gamma , A" just refers to \Gamma . And if A is not a member of \Gamma , then "\Gamma , A" refers to the set that contains everything in \Gamma , and also contains A (and contains nothing else). )
This is a more complex rule than monotonicity, since its instances have two premise-arguments each. So let's pause and think through some examples.
First, a simple one, revisiting L1. Consider the following rule instance:

This rule instance is an instance of transitivity. To see this, return to the general form of transitivity; you can see this instance as fitting into it with:
\Gamma as \{p\},
A as q,
B as r.

Thinking about this particular instance of transitivity, note that L1 does not obey it, since both premise-arguments of the instance are valid in L1 but the conclusion-argument is not. So L1 is not transitive, since there it at least one instance of transivity that it does not obey.
For another example instance of transitivity, consider this rule instance:

This also fits the general form of transivity, with:
\Gamma as \{p, q\},
A as r,
B as s.

Unlike our previous example, L1 does obey this instance, since p, q, r \;/\; s is invalid in L1. But L1 still is not transitive, since being transitive requires obeying every instance of transitivity, and we have already seen that there is an instance L1 does not obey.
As you might have guessed, I'm about to argue that every matrix logic is transitive. To see this, consider any instance of transitivity:

To show that every matrix logic must obey this instance, it is enough to show that if the conclusion-argument is invalid in a matrix logic, then one or the other premise-arguments must aslo be invalid in that matrix logic.

So take any matrix logic where \Gamma \;/\; B is invalid. This logic must be determined by some matrix where we have a valuation v that is a counterexample to this argument. That means that v(C) must be designated in the matrix for each C in \Gamma , and that v(B) must be undesignated.

But now think about v(A). Either this is designated in the matrix in question, or it is undesignated. If it is designated, then v is also a counterexample to \Gamma , A \;/\; B; and if it is undesignated, then v is also a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A. So either way, it can't be that both \Gamma \;/\; A and \Gamma , A \;/\; B are valid in the logic determined by the matrix. Thus, the logic determined by the matrix does obey this rule instance.
And since any instance of transitivity fits this form, that means that every matrix logic must obey all such instances. That is, all matrix logics are transitive.

I mentioned above that transitivity comes in many forms, some simpler and some more complex. The one I look at in the main thread of the lecture is a particularly simple one; I've chosen it for being easier to understand than some others. (That doesn't mean it's easy!) But in fact there are some other forms of transitivity that are more closely connected to what's really happening with matrices. In this side note, I'll look at one of the most important. This is totally skippable, though; it's just for if you find yourself interested!

The key is to note that the same reasoning about transitivity given above can apply to lots of sentences at the same time, not just a single sentence A. So suppose you have some matrix counterexample to some argument \Gamma \;/\; A. Then consider any set \Sigma of sentences. Either the counterexample you have assigns a designated value to every sentence in \Sigma , or it assigns an undesignated value to at least one member of \Sigma .

If it assigns a designated value to every sentence in \Sigma , then it is also a counterexample to \Gamma , \Sigma \;/\; A. And if it assigns an undesignated value to some member B of \Sigma , then it is a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; B.

This means that, for any matrix logic, any sets \Gamma and \Sigma , and any sentence A, if \Gamma \;/\; B is valid for all B in \Sigma , and if \Gamma , \Sigma \;/\; A is also valid, then \Gamma \;/\; A must be valid as well. This gives us a different rule than the one I've called 'transitivity' above. How many premise-arguments an instance of this rule has depends on how many members \Sigma has for that instance. In particular, there are instances of this rule with infinitely many premise-arguments.

That's important; there are logics that are transitive (in this lecture's sense of 'transitive') but that do not obey this rule, because of these infinite instances. Basically, transitivity just requires that we can do this when \Sigma has one member. And by repeating the reasoning, that turns out to be enough to show that we can do it wheneven \Sigma has finitely many members. But to make the leap to the infinite instances really is something more, something that does not follow from transitivity.

For consideration of a wider range of different transitivity properties, you can also see my paper "On the 'transitivity' of consequence relations"; like almost all my papers, this is on my website at https://negation.rocks/writing.html. That paper talks about the rule I'm calling 'transitivity' here under the name \text {F}_{\text {SF}}, and the stronger rule I'm discussing in this side note under the name \text {C}_{\text {SF}}.
Finally, let's return to the idea of a substitution. Here's a rule, called substitution invariance:
Substitution invariance is the rule whose instances are those that fit the form:

where there is some substitution s so that:
\Sigma is the set of results you get by applying s to each sentence in \Gamma , and
s(A) = B.
A logic is substitution invariant iff it obeys substitution invariance.

So when a logic is substitution invariant, if we have an argument valid in the logic, we can take any substitution we like, apply it to that argument, and get a (potentially) new argument that is also valid in the logic.
For example, the universal and empty logics are both substitution invariant, since neither of them has any cases where a substitution could take us from a valid argument to an invalid one.
More interestingly, though, every matrix logic is substitution invariant. Let's see why. We'll start by thinking about classical logic, but the strategy we'll use is general.

First, think about the argument p \land q, r \lor s \;/\; p \land s, and a substitution f where:
f(p) = \top
f(q) = p \land q
f(r) = t \rightarrow s
f(s) = s Then the substitution instance of the argument we need to think about is \top \land (p \land q), (t \rightarrow s) \lor s \;/\; \top \land s. That is, because of the substitution f, the following is an instance of substitution invariance:

Now, let's think about whether classical logic obeys this instance. But let's do it in a certain way that will reveal an idea: let's start by looking at the conclusion-argument. Is this argument classically valid?

Now, we can use this valuation v and the substitution f to reach a valuation u that is a counterexample to the premise-argument, as follows. For any atomic sentence A, we let u(A) = v(f(A)). Here's how this works:
since f(p) = \top and v(\top ) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we let u(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}},
since f(q) = p \land q and v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we let u(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}},
since f(r) = t \rightarrow s and v(t \rightarrow s) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we let u(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
since f(s) = s and v(s) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, we let u(s) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

Now, note that u must be a counterexample to the premise-argument. The reason is that, for every atomic sentence in this premise-argument, u gives it a particular value: the value that its f-substitution instance receives in the conclusion-argument. Whatever those values are, we already know that they come together in the form given to produce a counterexample to that conclusion-argument. So that must happen in the premise-argument as well.

Although this is just one example, it illustrates a general phenomenon. The key features of matrices that make this work are:
For any way of assigning any values to any atomic sentences, there is always a valuation that does that. This is what we need to be sure that there is always some valuation like u, no matter which valuation like v we start with. If, for example, there were some values that atomic sentences could not take, but that compound sentences could take, this reasoning would no longer work. But we know, from what valuations are, that this isn't so.
The value assigned by a valuation to a compound sentence depends only on its main connective and the values assigned to its components. This is what we need to be sure that all the sentences in the premise-argument take the same value on u that their substitution instances in the conclusion-argument take on v. We are making sure that the values of all the atomic sentences in the premise-argument do this, but we need to be sure that that stays true for all the sentences that serve as premises or conclusions. So we need to know that the value of a compound sentence can't depend on anything else. But that also comes from what valuations are.
Finally, we need to know that whether a valuation is a counterexample to an argument in a matrix depends only on the values that that valuation assigns to sentences in the argument. That way, after we've made sure that u assigns all the same values to the premise-argument's sentences that v assigned to the conclusion-argument's sentences, we can know that, since v is a counterexample to the conclusion-argument, u must be a counterexample to the premise-argument. And this is something else we do know, now from what a counterexample in a matrix is.

With those three ingredients in place, everything else works out. Nothing in particular mattered in the above reasoning about how many values we have in our structure, or which of those values are designated in our matrix, or what the connectives do to the values in the structure. So whenever we have a matrix logic and an instance of substitution invariance, the same reasoning will work to show that the logic obeys the instance. Thus, all matrix logics are substitution invariant.

This lecture focussed on the limits of matrix methods: what kinds of logics we can determine via matrices, and so also what kinds we can't. The key ideas are the idea of a matrix logic, the idea of a rule, and the properties of reflexivity, monotonicity, transitivity, and substitution invariance. As we've seen, every matrix logic has all four of these properties. That means that matrix methods are harder to use (and usually not the right idea at all) when exploring logics that lack one or more of these properties.

fr:title.text: Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Lecture 6: Using matrices to compare logics

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Last lecture, we saw what a matrix is, and how, once we have a matrix, we can use that matrix to determine a logic. This time, we'll look at one of the ways matrices can be used to compare logics. In particular, we'll use the matrices from last time to compare classical logic and the logics K3, LP, and FDE in terms of strength.

fr:title.text: A first example: classical logic and K3

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A first example: classical logic and K3

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let's start with an example, before we zoom out to look at the big idea. For this example, I'll walk you through some reasoning that compares K3 to classical logic in terms of strength.
Before we start, you might want to ask yourself: is K3 stronger than classical logic? is it weaker? both stronger and weaker? neither? Pause and think; what do you expect? How sure are you?
Okay, let's find out!

When we're asking about strength and weakness, we're asking about which arguments are in these logics. And there's a sense in which we already know exactly which arguments are in these logics: an argument is in classical logic iff it has no counterexamples in the classical matrix; and an argument is in K3 iff it has no counterexamples in the K3 matrix.

Our job here, then, is to draw these two ideas together, to make them comparable. What relationships can we find between classical counterexamples and K3 counterexamples? Here it can help to drill down to a more basic level: classical counterexamples are a certain kind of Boolean valuation, and K3 counterexamples are a certain kind of strong Kleene valuation. So what relationships can we find between Boolean valuations and strong Kleene valuations?

fr:title.text: Boolean valuations and strong Kleene valuations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Boolean valuations and strong Kleene valuations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Well, imagine we have some Boolean valuation v. This must assign either the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to every atomic sentence in the language, and then it must accord with the Boolean tables for every connective.

What I want to point out now, though, is that this is actually enough for v to also be a strong Kleene valuation. To see this, start with atomic sentences. The strong Kleene structure has the three values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and any way of assigning these values to the atomic sentences results in a strong Kleene valuation. But if we take our Boolean valuation v, we can see that it does exactly this! It just does it in a way that doesn't use the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} for any atomic sentence.

Assigning legit values to the atomic sentences, of course, is not enough for v to be a strong Kleene valuation. It also has to assign values to compound sentences in a way that accords with the strong Kleene tables. But have a look at the Boolean tables compared to the strong Kleene tables, connective by connective.
For example, let's look at the tables for conjunction:

Note that the Boolean table here lives inside the strong Kleene one. That is, if we're calculating the value of a conjunction in accordance with the strong Kleene table, if both components take values from \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}\}, then the result will be exactly the same as if we were calculating the value in accordance with the Boolean table instead.

And it's not just conjunction: this same relationship holds for all our other connectives. As long as we start from just \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, we always get the same results in the strong Kleene structure that we get in the Boolean structure. The difference between the structures only comes up when some sentence gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}: this can't happen in the Boolean structure. But as long as all the atomic sentences get values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then there can't be any divergence.
This means that every Boolean valuation is at the same time a strong Kleene valuation. That's an important link between the two structures.
With that link in hand, we're ready to return to counterexamples, to see if we can find a connection between classical counterexamples and K3 counterexamples.

Start by thinking: what is a classical counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A? It's a Boolean valuation v where:
for every B in \Gamma , we have v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
we also have v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

Now, we've just seen that every Boolean valuation is also a strong Kleene valuation. So imagine that we have some classical counterexample to some argument \Gamma \;/\; A. Is it a K3 counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A as well? Since it's a classical counterexample, it's a Boolean valuation, so it's also a strong Kleene valuation, which is a start. What does a strong Kleene valuation need to be like to be a K3 counterexample to this argument? It needs to give:
for every B in \Gamma , we have either v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
we also have either v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

If v is a classical counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A, then, it is also a K3 counterexample to that same argument: it brings all the premises to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, which is designated in both matrices, and it brings the conclusion to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, which is undesignated in both matrices. So every classical counterexample to an argument is at the same time a K3 counterexample to that argument as well.

fr:title.text: What does this tell us about strength?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What does this tell us about strength?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That's the key fact that we need. Getting there depended on a few things: we had to think in detail about all the tables for all the connectives in both structures, as well as about which values count as designated in both matrices. But as a result of looking through that detail, we've now got something we can use.

Take any argument that is invalid in classical logic. Since it is invalid in classical logic, it must have a classical counterexample. But we have just seen that that means that it also has a K3 counterexample: the very same valuation that is its classical counterexample. So that means that it must also be invalid in K3.

So any argument invalid in classical logic is also invalid in K3. That means that any argument valid in K3 must also be valid in classical logic: if it wasn't valid in classical logic, we've seen that it would have to also be invalid in K3. That is, K3 is a subset of classical logic: K3 is weaker than classical logic.

There's no magic tricks here! This is just a matter of moving slowly and methodically through the ideas we have so far, and seeing how they fit together. But the result gives us a discovery: we now know that K3 is weaker than classical logic.

Moreover, you already know from last time that K3 is not stronger than classical logic, since the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p is valid in classical logic but not in K3. So we've now got the full story about the relationship between these two logics in terms of strength: K3 is weaker than classical logic, but not vice versa.

That's the main idea I want to look at in this lecture: how we can move from one kind of counterexample to another, and use that sort of motion to compare two logics to each other in terms of strength.

As you've seen, in the above example we got to a place where, whenever we have a counterexample to an argument in one matrix, then that is also a counterexample to that same argument in another matrix. Whenever that situation obtains, we can be sure that the logic determined by the second matrix is weaker than the logic determined by the first.

But sometimes it's useful to be slightly more general. It's not really important to this reasoning that the counterexample in the second matrix be the exact same valuation as the counterexample in the first matrix. All we're doing is showing that an argument has a counterexample, so that it comes out invalid.

So really, the idea here is to get to a place where, whenever we have a counterexample to an argument in one matrix, then we can be sure that there is some counterexample to that same argument in another matrix. That's enough to be sure that the logic determined by the second matrix is weaker than the logic determined by the first.

In our above example, we could be sure that there was some K3 counterexample, because it just was the Boolean counterexample. But more generally, we might use one kind of counterexample to find a different kind. The next example we'll look at is of that sort.

fr:title.text: A second example: LP and FDE

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A second example: LP and FDE

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let's take a look at the logics LP and FDE, to try to compare these to each other using the same technique. As before, we should start by thinking about the valuations: how do strong Kleene valuations relate to Dunn-Belnap valuations? Then we can turn to thinking about counterexamples, just as before.

fr:title.text: Strong Kleene valuations and Dunn-Belnap valuations

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Strong Kleene valuations and Dunn-Belnap valuations

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When it comes to valuations, the relationship is more complex this time: we don't have simply that every strong Kleene valation is a Dunn-Belnap valuation or vice versa. The reason is simple: there are strong Kleene valuations that use the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, which is not a value in the Dunn-Belnap structure, and there are Dunn-Belnap valuations that use the values \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, which are not values in the strong Kleene structure.
So to find a relationship between these structures, we need to be a bit more clever. It helps to start by looking at the connective tables and seeing what we see. Here are the conjunction tables:

One thing that you might notice---and if you didn't, let me now draw your attention to it---is that the Boolean table for conjunction is contained in both of these. Whenever we use the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} exclusively, these tables both agree with the Boolean table, and so they agree with each other. The same is true for all our other connectives as well: as long as we have only the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} in play, all three of these structures match perfectly.

But that's not enough yet: we need some way to compare the remaining values as well. I think at this point that there are three natural things to try. It will turn out that not all of them work! But let's step through each possibility in turn.

Suppose we have a strong Kleene valuation v. That valuation itself need not be a Dunn-Belnap valuation, as noted above, since it might assign the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} to some atomic sentence. But perhaps we can use it to find a particular Dunn-Belnap valuation u that we can work with.

Here's one way we might do it. For every atomic sentence A, if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then we let u(A) = v(A).
