That is, if we're already using a value that's present in the Dunn-Belnap structure, we leave it alone and let the Dunn-Belnap valuation u match our strong Kleene valuation v for that atomic sentence. On the other hand, if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then we let u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

Now we've set up a way where, given any strong Kleene valuation, we can determine a Dunn-Belnap valuation, and we know how they're related for atomic sentences, because of how we've set them up: they match on values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and they also kind of match on other values, except that wherever the strong Kleene valuation has \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} the Dunn-Belnap valuation has \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, and vice versa.

What about compound sentences, though? Unlike atomic sentences, we can't just declare that these work however we want; we have tables to deal with in the two structures. So we need to look into these tables. As an example, let me reproduce the negation tables here:

As I pointed out before, here we have agreement between these tables on what they do with \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. But what about \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}? Here, too, we have a certain kind of "agreement": each of these values gets taken by negation to itself. So if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} then v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and if u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} then u(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

Here it helps to be a bit more precise in a certain kind of ritualised way. (With all our symbols flying around, this is probably the class where you're most likely to accidentally summon a demon anyhow; let's lean into it!) The ritualised reasoning I want to introduce here is called reasoning by induction.

Actually, there are at least two different kinds of reasoning that get called reasoning by induction, and they are very much not the same! One is the kind that plays a role in the so-called "problem of induction"; that's not what I'm talking about here. The other is one that's sometimes called "mathematical induction" or "structural induction", and that's what we are doing.

The core of the idea is this: suppose we want to securely demonstrate that all formulas are such-and-such a way. The idea of induction doesn't depend on what we're trying to show about all formulas, so for now let's just say we want to show that all formulas are happy.

We can use what we know about how formulas are built up to systematically think about all formulas, and make sure we haven't left any of them out. Since there are more than six or seven formulas, we need some kind of system! The structure of formulas themselves is a very useful system much of the time, and it's what I'll use here.

We want to start, then, with the atomic sentences, showing that they are all happy. In practice, how we do this will depend on what it is we're really showing about all sentences; induction provides us no help here. But suppose we can do it: we've shown that all atomic sentences are happy.

What remains is all the compound sentences, and this is where induction really gives us its power. All we need to do here is to show that if all the components of a compound sentence are happy, then the compound sentence itself is also happy. That is, in showing that the compound sentence is happy, we can help ourselves to the assumption that all its components are happy.
This is important enough that it's handy to have a word for it:

A connective in a language preserves a property of sentences iff: whenever that connective is the main connective of a sentence, if all the components of that sentence have the property, then so does the sentence itself.
So what we're aiming to do is to show that all the connectives preserve the property of happiness.

Think about why this works: suppose it's true that all atomic sentences are happy, and that all the connectives preserve happiness. How, then, could there ever be a sentence that wasn't happy? How could it be built? We have to start from atomic sentences that are happy, and build up using connectives that can't ever bring us from happy sentences to not-happy ones. So any sentence we can manage to build in this way must be happy---but that's all the sentences!

If we can show the two parts of what we need, then:
that all atomic sentences are happy, and
that all connectives preserve happiness, then we've shown "by induction" that all sentences are happy.

Okay, back to what we're doing. Here, what we want to show about all formulas is a bit complex. But really, it's exactly what we've set up to be true for all atomic sentences---we want to show that that's true of all sentences. That is, we're going to reason by induction, but instead of showing that all formulas are "happy", we're going to show that all formulas A are such that:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then u(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

To show by induction that all formulas have this complex property, we start by showing that all atomic sentences have it. Here our job is easy: the way we set up the Dunn-Belnap valuation u, it does exactly this for all atomic sentences! So the first half of what we need is already done.

The second half is to work connective by connective, and see that each connective preserves this property. Consider negation, for example. We want to see that, whenever any sentence A has this property, then \neg A also has the property. So imagine that we have some sentence A with the property. Then one of three things is the case: either
v(A) = u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(A) = u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, or
v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.

Now, looking at our negation tables, we can see that these three cases give us, respectively:
v(
eg A) = u(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, or
v(
eg A) = u(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and u(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. And in all three of those cases, we can see that 
eg A has the property we're concerned with. So 
eg preserves this property.

To finish the job, we need to think about every connective in turn. I won't go through them all here, since it gets pretty repetitive. But I will look at one binary and one nullary connective, so that you can see how this reasoning goes at various different arities. You should read this looking for the general pattern of reasoning. The details are important, but they are also just details. Make sure you see the big picture too! That way you can use this technique to answer your own questions (and puzzles).

Let's look at a binary connective: \lor . We want to show that \lor preserves the property. So consider any sentence A \lor B, and suppose that both A and B have the property; we want to show that A \lor B has it as well.

This gives us nine possibilities to think through, as each of A and B gives three possibilities: either it has value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} in both v and u, or it has \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} in both v and u, or it has \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} in v and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} in u. Since these are independent, and we have two components to consider, there are nine overall possibilities. These possibilities are nicely summed up in the strong Kleene table for disjunction, which also tells us v(A \lor B) for each of these possibilities:
It remains, then, to check u(A \lor B) on these same nine possibilities. Here we can start from the Dunn-Belnap table for disjunction, since u is a Dunn-Belnap valuation:

Notice, though, that we can ignore the row and the column headed by "\mathsf {\mathbf {B}}". After all, we're supposing that both A and B have the property, and that means that both u(A) and u(B) must not be \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}.
Look at it, then, more like this:

And now note, if you focus just on what's not faded out, how similar this looks to the strong Kleene table. You can use these tables to walk through all nine possibilities, and see that in each case, indeed A \lor B has the property.

That is, suppose v(A) = v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; then we are in the top left corner of the strong Kleene table. Since both A and B have the property, this means that u(A) = u(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so we are also in the top left corner of the Dunn-Belnap table. Comparing those two top-left corners, we see that v(A \lor B) = u(A \lor B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so in this case A \lor B has the property.

That preceding paragraph is one of the nine cases that need considering. Take a moment to walk through at least a few of the other cases at that level of detail, making sure you see how every sinew of the reasoning connects. If you do it properly, you'll see that it really does work!

As is usual, the situation with our nullary connectives is maybe a little weirder-feeling, but in fact much simpler. Let's look at the case of \top . In both strong Kleene and Dunn-Belnap valuations, this sentence always receives the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so it has the property. That's it!

Let's take stock. We've seen that, for any strong Kleene valuation v, we can set up a Dunn-Belnap valuation u so that every atomic sentence A has the following property:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then u(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then u(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.
We've also seen that every connective preserves this property. So we can conclude by induction that the property holds of every sentence, not just the atomic ones.

This doesn't show that strong Kleene valuations are Dunn-Belnap valuations. But it does show a way to translate any strong Kleene valuation into an importantly-related Dunn-Belnap valuation: leave all the \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} values alone, and change every \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} to \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. For now, put a pin in that; we'll come back to it in a moment. We've got other possibilities to consider first!

Again, suppose we have a strong Kleene valuation v. Let's try a different tack this time, to see if we can treat the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} like \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} instead. That is, let's set up a Dunn-Belnap valuation t so that, for every atomic sentence A, we have:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} then t(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} then t(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}.

This certainly determines some Dunn-Belnap valuation; but as before, we can't say much about the result yet. It would be convenient if the above property turns out to hold for all sentences, not just the atomic ones. As before, we can attempt to show this via induction. For convenience, let's call this property "the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property"; note that it's a different property from the one we looked at before. (Note, though, that there's nothing particularly important about this property in its own right; we're just using it for a moment, to reason by induction.) I'll go through the reasoning a bit quicker this time, so you can see the pattern again.
Let's start with \neg again. Here are the strong Kleene and Dunn-Belnap tables for \neg :

Now, we want to show that if any sentence A has the property, then 
eg A has it as well. There are three possibilities:
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, or
v(
eg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

This shows that, no matter what v(
eg A) is, t(
eg A) is related to it in the right way. So as long as A has the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, then 
eg A has it as well; this shows that 
eg preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property.

Again, I'll only look at one binary connective, since they all follow the same pattern of reasoning; this time I'll pick \rightarrow for the sake of variety. Here are the strong Kleene and Dunn-Belnap tables for \rightarrow :

We want to show that \rightarrow preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, that whenever both A and B have the property, then A \rightarrow B has it as well. But if indeed both A and B have the property, then we know that u(A) and u(B) can't be \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, so we can set aside those rows and columns of the Dunn-Belnap table:

And now you can compare that to the strong Kleene table to see that these tables match on \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and that they use \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} in the same places as each other. So \rightarrow too preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property.

To conclude that every sentence has the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, we need to know that every connective, not just \neg and \rightarrow , preserves the property. But here I'll just tell you: they do.

You should know now, though, how you would go about discovering this; what you would be looking at about each connective in turn in order to detect whether that connective does or does not preserve the property.

Since every connective preserves the \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} property, and every atomic sentence has the property, we can conclude that every sentence has the property. This lets us know that, given any strong Kleene valuation v, we can generate a Dunn-Belnap valuation t that matches v on all \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} values, and that uses \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} wherever v uses \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

Combining this with what we learned before, we now know that every strong Kleene valuation can be turned into a Dunn-Belnap valuation two different ways: we can treat \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} everywhere as \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, or we can treat it everywhere as \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Again, put a pin in that for now.

fr:title.text: Can we treat both \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} as \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Can we treat both \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} as \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A third thing that we might think about is coming back the other way. We've seen two different ways to get Dunn-Belnap valuations from a strong Kleene valuation. But suppose we have a Dunn-Belnap valuation instead; can we use that to reach a strong Kleene valuation in a useful way?

Spoiler: this is the idea I want to explore that won't work out, so that you can see up close what it looks like when this doesn't go smoothly. This is important: when you're doing reasoning like this to explore a question, you can't know in advance how things will go. That's why you're doing the reasoning: to find out! We've seen two cases where it does work, but those are very special. More usual would be a case like this, where it doesn't work. Let's dive in!

Suppose we begin from some Dunn-Belnap valuation v, and we want to generate a strong Kleene valuation s. One natural thought, based roughly on what we've just looked at, might be to leave \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as they are, and to treat both \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} as \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. That is, let's think about the strong Kleene valuation s such that for all atomic sentences A, we have:
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then s(A) = v(A), and
if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, then s(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

Since valuations can do whatever they like with atomic sentences, there surely is such a strong Kleene valuation s. Again, it's handy to have a name for the property we're looking at, the one we set up for all atomic sentences, so that we can explore whether it applies to all sentences. Let's call it the "\mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property".
So now we want to see whether all connectives preserve the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property.
Let's start with our tables for negation:
Now, supposing that A has the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property, can we show that \neg A does as well?

Here there are four possibilities to consider:
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, or
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, or
v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.
This checks out! So indeed, if A has the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property, then \neg A does as well; this means that \neg preserves this property.
Let's move on to a binary connective. Here are the tables for \land in the two structures we're thinking about:
Again, we want to show that whenever sentences A and B have the property, then A \land B does as well.

This time, assuming that A and B have the property doesn't let us ignore any parts of these tables, though; we've got to consider all possibilities, since we're starting from any Dunn-Belnap valuation v. Instead, we want to think of what we're trying as like "smooshing" the two values \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} down into the one value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, while leaving \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} alone. So you might even look at the Dunn-Belnap table here like this:

But now we can see a problem. Looking to the strong Kleene table, we can see three internal cells with the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. These are the values s gives to A \land B when either A or B has the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and neither of them has the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} That is, when either s(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} or s(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and both s(A) and s(B) are not \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then s(A \land B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

Now look to the Dunn-Belnap table. When s(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(A) must be either \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. And similarly, when s(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(B) must be either \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. (Remember, we're supposing that A and B have the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property.) Similarly, if both s(A) and s(B) are not \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then both v(A) and v(B) must not be \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} either. So we have eight possibilities to consider; these are the distinguished cells in the table.

For A \land B to have the property, we would need to see only \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} in those distinguished cells. But there are two \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}s in there! This gives us the clue we need to show that \land does not preserve the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property.

It's time to fill in a concrete case. Suppose our initial Dunn-Belnap valuation v is such that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. Then v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. But the strong Kleene valuation s we've determined from v has s(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and s(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, so s(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. The \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property fails for p \land q. But we set up s so that the property would hold for p and q; so we can see that \land does not preserve the property.

So, given a Dunn-Belnap valuation v, we can indeed reach a strong Kleene valuation s as specified. But this strong Kleene valuation is not related to v in any particularly convenient way, at least as far as we've seen. The attempt to extend the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property from atomic sentences to all sentences has failed.

Moreover, it's not just like we tried to show this and came up empty: we even found a particular Dunn-Belnap valuation v where the \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} / \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} property does not hold of p \land q. So there's no point in continuing to try to show that this property holds of all sentences; we know it doesn't.

Here's another way to look at what we've shown: given a Dunn-Belnap valuation v, we might set up a function r from sentences to strong Kleene values that matches v on \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and collapses both of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} to the single value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. What we've just shown, though, is that this function r won't generally be a strong Kleene valuation! It will match our strong Kleene valuation s on atomic sentences, but not on all sentences. So there isn't much we could do with r to connect Dunn-Belnap valuations to the strong Kleene structure.

Unfortunately, that doesn't really tell us much else. Is our strong Kleene valuation s related to v in some other way worth exploring? For all I've said here, maybe it is and maybe it isn't. Or is there some other way to take a Dunn-Belnap valuation and generate a strong Kleene valuation that's usefully related to it? For all I've said here, maybe there is and maybe there isn't.
That's just life, I'm afraid: it's nice when things work out, but it's not always so!

Let's take stock now. We've explored three different possibilities for connecting strong Kleene valuations to Dunn-Belnap valuations. Two of these ways worked out: we can take any strong Kleene valuation and replace all \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}s with \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}s to reach a Dunn-Belnap valuation, or we can replace them all with \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}s to reach a different Dunn-Belnap valuation. The third way didn't work out: if we take a Dunn-Belnap valuation, collapsing all \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} values to the single value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} doesn't generally give us a strong Kleene valuation at all. Going forward, let's forget about what didn't work, and focus on what did.

This is all in the service, remember, of using our matrices to compare the logics LP and FDE. Now that we've got a sense of how things work out with valuations, let's turn to the matrices for LP and FDE, so that we can think about these.

We know that our methods for turning strong Kleene valuations into Dunn-Belnap valuations are the methods that work, so we'll focus on that direction. Suppose we have a strong Kleene valuation v that is an LP counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A. That is:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , v(B) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and
v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

What can we do with this? What we'd like to do is to create an FDE counterexample to the same argument. If we can do that, we'll have shown that FDE is weaker than LP, by the same reasoning we used to show that K3 is weaker than classical logic.

And we have two tools we can try. We know that there is a Dunn-Belnap valuation u that is just like v except that it uses \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} wherever v uses \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and there is a Dunn-Belnap valuation t that is just like v except that it uses \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} instead.

Think about this valuation t in particular. Because of what we know about v, we know that:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , t(B) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and
t(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

But that's enough for t to be an FDE counterexample to the argument \Gamma \;/\; A: the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} are designated in the matrix for FDE, and the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} is not. (The value \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} also is not, but that doesn't matter here, since t doesn't assign \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} to any sentence.)

So we've got another instance of the overall pattern described above: whenever there is an LP counterexample to any argument, there is also an FDE counterexample to that same argument. So if an argument is invalid in LP, it must also be invalid in FDE. This guarantees that FDE is weaker than LP.

fr:title.text: A third example for free: K3 and FDE

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A third example for free: K3 and FDE

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given the above, though, we also get some useful information about K3 and FDE. Here, we've already done almost all the work; we just need to put together the pieces in the right way.

Suppose we have some strong Kleene valuation v that is a K3 counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A. That is:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , v(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
v(A) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}.

As we've seen, we can use v to reach a Dunn-Belnap valuation u that matches v on values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and that uses \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} wherever v uses \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So:
for every sentence B in \Gamma , u(B) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and
u(A) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}.
And that's enough for u to be an FDE counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A. So FDE is also weaker than K3.

Besides giving us another example of the key reasoning pattern for this lecture, this also gives another lesson: when we work out the solution to a puzzle systematically, by trying different approaches and keeping track of which ones work, we often end up solving more than just the original puzzle. Here we set out to inquire about LP and FDE; we tried two different ways of transforming strong Kleene valuations into Dunn-Belnap valuations, and they both worked. One of those ways turned out to be the key to answering the original question about LP and FDE, but the other way wasn't useless at all: it is the key to answering this different question about K3 and FDE.
Moreover, we already saw that FDE is distinct from both LP and K3. So now we know just how these logics relate in terms of strength: FDE is weaker than both LP and K3, and not vice versa.

We're now getting to the real work of logic. Note that there's only one new definition in this lecture! I think this is the first time we've had so few. We're rather working with all the definitions we've built up over the preceding weeks, and exploring some of the texture that results.

The headlines this time are the strength facts we've shown: K3 is weaker than classical logic, FDE is weaker than LP, FDE is weaker than K3. (We haven't asked about LP and classical logic, but you now have enough to be able to figure this one out!)

More important than these headlines, though, is the techniques we developed on the way there. First, there is the key way we used matrices: when we have two logics given via matrices, if we can transform any counterexample to an argument in one of these matrices into a counterexample to the same argument in the other matrix, then we can conclude that the second logic is weaker than the first.

And second, there is the technique of reasoning by induction that is key for applying this technique to matrices built on different structures. Here, if we want to show that any property holds of all sentences in our language, we show that it holds for all atomic sentences and that all connectives preserve the property. Since all sentences are built from atomics via connectives, this is enough to ensure that every sentence has the property.

This week, we combine the work from lectures 3 and 4, showing how to extend a structure (lecture 4) to a new kind of thing called a matrix, and then showing how to use a matrix to determine a logic (week 3).

Recall that a logic is a set of arguments. We've seen some examples already, and we'll see more over the course of the rest of the semester. This week, we'll develop some more in-depth tools for specifying particular logics. As it happens, the structures and valuations that you already know about from last week give us one of the best ways we can use for talking about logics that are rich enough to be interesting. The rest of this week, that's what we'll look at.

One way into the idea starts from thinking of our valuations again as possible worlds. For simplicitly, let's start here with Boolean valuations. Let's say we want to count an argument as valid when, no matter what, the truth of its premises guarantees the truth of its conclusion. We can use our valuations, standing in for possible worlds, to understand the "no matter what" part here.

As a warmup, say that a Boolean valuation v is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A when every premise in \Gamma gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on valuation v and the conclusion A gets value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} on v. If an argument has any counterexamples, then we can see that the truth of its premises does not guarantee the truth of its conclusion---the counterexample shows a way to distribute truth values among the atomic formulas that makes the premises all true without making the conclusion true.

So we can get a logic from our Boolean valuations like this: if any Boolean valuation is a counterexample to an argument, this logic counts the argument as invalid. On the other hand, if no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to the argument, then this logic counts the argument as valid. This is a very widely used logic, called classical logic.

Recall the history here, though: this only goes back to the 19th century. So the "classical" here is more like the "classical" in "classical music" than it is like the "classical" in "classical architecture".

Let's return to some earlier example arguments, to see how they fare in classical logic. First, think about p \land q \;/\; p. To see whether this argument is classically valid or not, we should ask: is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? Remember, a counterexample to this argument is a valuation v where v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By looking at the Boolean structure, though, you can see that this is impossible: if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then no matter what v(q) is we also have v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So no Boolean valuation is a counterexample to p \land q \;/\; p. As a result, this argument is classically valid.

For a contrasting example, think about p \lor q \;/\; p. Is any Boolean valuation a counterexample to this argument? A counterexample to this argument would be a valuation w where w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. Is there any such w? Yes, there is. Since there is always a valuation for any combination of values for our atomic sentences, consider any valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. In any such valuation, we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so any such valuation is a counterexample to this argument. As a result, this argument is not classically valid.

Just like the Boolean structure, classical logic doesn't have to be interpreted as about truth. Recall that if we're thinking about electrical circuits, each sentence represents a component of the circuit that can be on or off. In this case, when an argument \Gamma \;/\; A is classically valid, it means that whenever all the components represented by sentences in \Gamma are on, then the component represented by A must also be on. A counterexample to the argument would be some way of setting things up so that all the premise components are on and the conclusion component is off.

What about arguments \Gamma \;/\; A where \Gamma is the empty set of premises? Remember, we can have arguments with no premises. What does it take for a valuation to be a counterexample to one of these? The same thing it takes to be a counterexample to any argument: it has to make all the premises take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and the conclusion take value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. To interpret this, we need to remember logicians' weird habit 2: if there are no Xs, then "all Xs are Ys" automatically counts as true. So when there are no premises, "all the premises take value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on this valuation" is always true, no matter what valuation we're looking at. That means that, for a valuation to be a counterexample to an argument \;/\; A with no premises, all it needs to do is take the sentence A to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The none premises are automatic.

That means that an argument \;/\; A is classically valid whenever the sentence A takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on every Boolean valuation. Such sentences are called theorems. Just like "valid" or "invalid", "theorem" is always relative to a logic. A sentence A might be a theorem in one logic but not another, because the argument \;/\; A is valid in the first logic but not the other. Indeed, we'll see examples like that soon.
A theorem of a logic is a sentence A such that the argument \;/\; A is valid in the logic.

One notable theorem of classical logic is p \lor 
eg p. In setting up our Boolean valuations, we forced every sentence to be true or false on each valuation. Since negation takes falsity to truth, on every valuation one or the other of p and 
eg p is true. And a disjunction is true when at least one of the disjuncts is true. So p \lor 
eg p comes out true no matter what value p has. This appears in the resulting logic: \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is a valid argument.

In fact, we can go farther. For any sentence A, the sentence A \lor 
eg A is a classical theorem. Make sure you see how to interpret this. There's no particular sentence "A \lor 
eg A" in our language; A isn't part of the language at all. Rather, what I'm saying is that p \lor 
eg p is a classical theorem, and q \lor 
eg q is a classical theorem, and (p \land q) \lor 
eg (p \land q) is a classical theorem, and 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) \lor 
eg 
eg 
eg (p \lor 
eg r) is a classical theorem, and so on. I'm using A as a variable, to make a claim about lots of different sentences all at once.

Before we move on, let's look at one more example. Think about the argument p, 
eg p \;/\; q. Is this classically valid? To find out, we need to think whether it has any classical counterexamples. A classical counterexample would be a Boolean valuation v where v(p) = v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, since it would need to make both premises true and the conclusion not true. But this can't happen; if v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(
eg p) can't also be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, because of how negation is handled in Boolean valuations. So there are no classical counterexamples to p, 
eg p \;/\; q. This argument is classically valid.

That sometimes can throw people for a loop; it seems like p and \neg p have nothing to do with q! So how can the argument be valid? This is where it's important to remember how logic works: we lay out precise and clear definitions, and we see where they follow. The way we've set up classical logic, it just falls out that p, \neg p \;/\; q is valid. If it seems to you like arguments like this shouldn't be valid, that might give you a reason to explore logics other than classical logic. This is one of the ways different logics will differ from each other.

Now, let's look at our second example logic. This will work with a similar idea: we're going to think of valuations as something like possible worlds, and we're going to think of an argument as trying to guarantee the truth of its conclusion given the truth of all its premises. But we're now going to use strong Kleene valuations instead of Boolean valuations. Recall that the strong Kleene structure has three values: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. We can assume that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} still represents truth, and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} still falsity. But what about \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}? As I presented the strong Kleene structure last time, I mentioned that this value can be interpreted in different ways. For now, let's assume we're interpreting it as something that isn't true: perhaps as neither true nor false, say. So we're supposing that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} is the only true value in this structure. Then in our new logic, a counterexample to \Gamma \;/\; A is a strong Kleene valuation that takes every premise in \Gamma to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and takes A to either \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. This logic is called K3. (The "K" here is for "Kleene", and the "3" is for the number of values in the strong Kleene structure.)

As in classical logic, p \land q \;/\; p is valid in K3. By revisiting (or recalling) the strong Kleene table for \land , you can see that for any valuation v, if v(p) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, then v(p \land q) must also be either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So there is no valuation that is a counterexample to this argument. Also as in classical logic, p \lor q \;/\; p is invalid in K3. For example, take a valuation w with w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Then we have w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; so this is a counterexample to the argument. (There is another value p could have in counterexamples to this argument as well; do you see it?)

Again as in classical logic, p, 
eg p \;/\; q is valid in K3. To have a K3 counterexample to this argument, we would need a strong Kleene valuation that assigned \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to both p and 
eg p (as well as assigning some other value to q). But no strong Kleene valuation can assign \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to both p and 
eg p. So there is no K3 counterexample to this argument.

Despite these similarities, however, classical logic and K3 are not the same. For example, we've seen that \;/\; p \lor 
eg p is classically valid. But it is not K3 valid. That is to say, p \lor 
eg p is not a theorem of K3. Take any strong Kleene valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Then since 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, we also have v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. And since \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, this gives v(p \lor 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. That is, assuming our interpretation of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as neither true nor false, on any valuation where p itself is neither true nor false, p \lor 
eg p is also neither true nor false. And if it's neither true nor false, then in particular it ain't true. So we have a K3 counterexample to \;/\; p \lor 
eg p.

Let's now look at a third example logic, different from both classical logic and K3. Like K3, this third logic makes use of strong Kleene valuations, but it involves treating the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} differently. Suppose that, instead of treating \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as a value for things that are neither true nor false, we instead treat it as a value for things that are both true and false. Then, if we want our valid arguments to preserve truth, we would say that a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A is a strong Kleene valuation that takes every premise in \Gamma to one of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, and the conclusion A to \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. The logic that results from this is known as LP. (This stands for the "Logic of Paradox"; it was given this name in the late 1970s by the English/Australian logician Graham Priest. The logic itself seems to have been first studied by the Argentinian/USian logician Florencio Asenjo in the early 1950s.)

Important: Even though K3 and LP use the very same valuations, built on the very same structure---the strong Kleene structure---they are different logics. This is because of the different ways counterexamples are determined.

For example, LP is like classical logic, and unlike K3, in counting \;/\; p \lor \neg p as valid. This is because, in a strong Kleene valuation, the sentence p \lor \neg p always takes either the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Either way, the valuation is not an LP counterexample to this argument. As we've seen, though, if it takes value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} on a valuation, then that valuation is a K3 counterexample to the argument.

On the other hand, LP differs from both classical logic and K3 in holding the argument p, \neg p \;/\; q to be invalid. Take a strong Kleene valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; such a valuation is an LP counterexample to this argument.

The argument p, 
eg p \;/\; q is sometimes known as explosion. It gets such a dramatic name because it seems to suggest that any old contradiction, like having p and 
eg p together, should entail absolutely anything else. And indeed, in classical logic and K3 this is the case: for any sentences A, B at all, A, 
eg A \;/\; B is valid in both classical logic and K3. From a contradiction, anything follows. This is not so in LP, as we've seen. As explosion seems like a very odd kind of argument to many people, this is often held up as a way in which LP more closely matches some of our intuitions about good and bad arguments than some other logics.

This nice feature, though, comes with a bit of a dark side. Think about the following line of reasoning: "The rabbit either went left or it went right. It didn't go left. Therefore, it went right". This line of reasoning strikes many people as if it should be valid: the truth of its premises, it seems, suffices for the truth of its conclusion. And since the argument just turns on "or" and "not", it seems that we can represent it at least decently well in our language, as something like p \lor q, \neg p \;/\; q.

This kind of argument has been studied for thousands of years; it's much much older than the kind of logic we're doing. It's often called "disjunctive syllogism" nowadays; the ancient Stoics knew it as the "fifth indemonstrable". Sextus Empiricus (in the secondish century CE, in his Outlines of Pyrrhonism I.69) had this to say, talking about the earlier Stoic Chrysippus (in the secondish century BCE):

"[Chrysippus] declares that the dog makes use of the fifth complex indemonstrable syllogism when, on arriving at a spot where three ways meet\ldots , after smelling at the two roads by which the quarry did not pass, he rushes off at once by the third without stopping to smell. For, says the old writer, the dog implicitly reasons thus: 'The animal went either by this road, or by that, or by the other: but it did not go by this or that, therefore he went the other way'".

This argument is classically valid, and valid in K3, but not valid in LP. Let's see why. To have a classical counterexample to the argument, we would need a Boolean valuation v where v(p \lor q) = v(\neg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. Let's think about what such a valuation would have to be like. We know v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. What about v(p)? Well, since v(\neg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, we must have v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. But then both p and q take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and this means that p \lor q must take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as well. That is, no Boolean valuation can meet the criteria we need for it to be a counterexample to this argument. As a result, the argument is classically valid.

What about a strong Kleene valuation? Well, we have a strong Kleene valuation w where w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. This gives us w(\neg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} and w(p \lor q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. So this valuation is an LP counterexample to the argument, and the argument is LP invalid. But it is not a K3 counterexample, since it does not make the premises of the argument take the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Indeed, there is no K3 counterexample to this argument.

The reason p \lor q, 
eg p \;/\; q is invalid in LP is the same reason that p, 
eg p \;/\; q is invalid: because LP allows for p and 
eg p to be true at the same time. This lenience towards contradictions is something we looked at a bit when we introduced Dunn-Belnap valuations, and if we use strong Kleene valuations in the way suggested by LP, we can see it here as well.

Classical logic, K3, and LP are three relatively well-known and widely-studied logics, and each of them forms a good potential topic for some puzzles. (They also give us some of the exercises at https://binchicken.one.) It's also nice, in addition to looking at specific example logics like this, to have some more systematic tools for exploring logics. In this bit, I'll talk about one standard way to do this; the example logics we've seen so far all fit this mould.

The idea is this: take a structure, any structure at all, and then indicate some set of the structure's values. For example, we might pick the Boolean structure and indicate the set \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}\}. Or we might pick the strong Kleene structure, and indicate the set \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}\}. Or we might pick the strong Kleene structure, and indicate the set \{\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}\}. Any such assembly---a structure, together with some distinguished set of values from the structure---is called a matrix.
A matrix has two parts: first, a structure; and second, a subset of the structure's set of values. The values in this subset are called the designated values of the matrix.

You might be aware that there are mathematical structures called "matrices" as well, studied in linear algebra and the like. It's just a coincidence that the two ideas use the same word; logical matrices don't have anything to do with those matrices, as far as I know.

The three example matrices a couple of paragraphs back weren't chosen at random: respectively, they determine classical logic, K3, and LP, once you know how to look at them. Here's the idea: one way to present a logic, the way we've been looking at, is to specify some kind of valuation, and then say what it takes for a particular valuation to be a counterexample to an argument. The logic presented in this way counts an argument as being invalid if any valuation is a counterexample to it, and valid if no valuation is a counterexample.

Now, suppose you have a matrix, which is to say you have a structure together with some set \Delta of the structure's values. (That's the Greek letter Delta. Remember, I'm using Greek letters for sets; I've chosen \Delta because "designated" starts with "d".) The valuations you get from this matrix are just whatever valuations you'd get from the structure. The set \Delta isn't involved at all in determining these valuations. The set \Delta is instead involved in saying when one of these valuations is a counterexample to an argument. A valuation v is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A exactly when v takes every premise in \Gamma to some value in \Delta , but takes A to a value outside of \Delta . We use this to get, for every matrix, a particular logic that the matrix determines.

fr:title.text: Counterexample in a matrix, logic determined by a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Counterexample in a matrix, logic determined by a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Given a matrix with structure S and set \Delta of designated values, a valuation v in the structure S is a counterexample to an argument \Gamma \;/\; A in that matrix iff both:
for every B in \Gamma , v(B) is in \Delta , and
v(A) is not in \Delta
The logic determined by a matrix is the logic that contains an argument iff that argument has no counterexamples in the matrix.
All our earlier examples fit this pattern; this is the key idea for this week.

The set \Delta of designated values here is playing the role of the values we're counting as "true". So in K3, where we don't usually think of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as being true, we don't have \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as a designated value. But in LP, where we do usually think of \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as being true, there we do have \mathsf {\mathbf {*}} as a designated value. This gives us an example where we can have two different logics, coming from two different matrices, built on the same structure. The only difference is in the set of designated values.

fr:title.text: Another example logic, presented with a matrix

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Another example logic, presented with a matrix

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To give you some practice with this idea, here's another example logic. This one I'll present directly via a matrix. The structure we'll use for this one is the Dunn-Belnap structure, and the designated values are \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. There; that's enough to fully specify a logic. This one is one we'll call FDE.

"FDE" stands for "first-degree E". E is a logic like FDE, but E uses a very different implication connective. The logic E was first developed to use this implication connective to give some understanding of entailment; that's why it's called E.

E is a member of a family of logics known as relevant logics (in Australia) or relevance logics (in the US). Relevant logics form a very important aspect of Australian research in logic---indeed, they're the reason that I first came to Australia. They're a bit too complex for these notes to spend much time on them, but if you want to look into them, you'll find a fascinating literature.
Like all the logics we've seen so far, FDE holds the argument p \land q \;/\; p to be valid, and p \lor q \;/\; p to be invalid.
Take a moment and see if you can find an FDE counterexample to this second argument.

There are a number of different ways to reach an FDE counterexample to the second argument. If you have a valuation v where v(p) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} and where v(q) is either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, then you have a counterexample to this argument; if your valuation isn't like this, then you don't have one.

Let's think it through: to be a counterexample to p \lor q \;/\; p, a valuation would need to take p \lor q to one of our designated values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and take p to some undesignated value. Since the undesignated values in this matrix are \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, we know that for v to be a counterexample we need v(p) to be either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. What, then, about v(q)? Well, we also need v(p \lor q) to be either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. By checking how the Dunn-Belnap structure treats disjunction, you can see that, with v(p) being either \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, setting v(q) to either \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} will work, and nothing else will.

What about the arguments that distinguished K3 and LP from classical logic? Here, too, FDE is different from everything we've seen so far. Like K3, and unlike classical logic or LP, FDE holds \;/\; p \lor \neg p to be invalid; any valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} provides a counterexample. And like LP, but unlike classical logic or K3, FDE holds p, \neg p \;/\; q to be invalid; any valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} provides a counterexample. For the same reason, FDE agrees with LP in holding disjunctive syllogism to be invalid, unlike classical logic or K3.

By now, you have a lot of different ideas to keep track of, and it's important to keep them clearly distinguished from each other. On one track, you know about languages, and then arguments, and then logics: a language is made up of atomic formulas and connectives; and then an argument is a set of formulas together with another formula; and then a logic is a set of arguments. On a separate track, you know about structures, valuations, and matrices: a structure is some values together with a specification of how the connectives work on those values; a valuation is any way of assigning values from a structure to the atomic formulas, which then results in assignments of values to every formula; and a matrix is a structure together with a set of designated values.

The key notion that ties these two tracks together is the notion of a counterexample, as you've seen. Given a matrix, a particular valuation is a counterexample to a particular argument when that valuation assigns designated values to all the argument's premises and also assigns an undesignated value to the argument's conclusion.

Then, with all that in hand, you can see how, given any particular matrix, we can determine a logic: given a matrix, we count an argument being invalid in the matrix's logic when some valuation is a counterexample to the argument, and valid otherwise.

Every one of these terms---structure, matrix, valuation, language, argument, logic, counterexample---means something different from all the others. It's a lot to keep track of, I know. Just try to choose your words carefully, and know that I'm doing the same.
We've also looked at some of the features these logics have in common, and at some of the features that distinguish them.

In the first week, we learned about the kinds of languages we're going to work with this semester. These languages contain sentences like p \land \neg (q \lor \bot ) and so on. At the moment, this is just an unintelligible string of symbols. This week we'll take our first steps into endowing our languages with meaning. For now, we won't worry about connecting arguments and logics to this work on meaning just yet; we'll bring it all together next week. This week, we're focusing just on languages. (Recall that our default language, the one to focus on for now, uses infinitely many atomic formulas, plus the connectives \land , \lor , \rightarrow , \neg , \bot with arities 2, 2, 2, 1, 0, respectively. If you have any question about what that all means, check back to lecture 1!)

The approach we're going to start with for bringing meaning to these sentences uses truth values. These start to give us a limited grip on something like meaning. Real sentences, in languages like English, are true or false, when they are, in part because of what they mean. The sentence "There are at least nine planets in our solar system" is false, in part because of what bodies are out there in space, but also in part because of a convention about the word "planet" adopted by the International Astronomical Union in 2006. (Before this convention was adopted, Pluto was also considered a planet, making the sentence true.) Of course, there's much more to the meaning of a real sentence than just whether it's true or false! But truth and falsity are at least wrapped up in the meaning of real sentences, so we're at least in the right area to be poking around.

fr:title.text: A first example: two truth values

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A first example: two truth values

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let's start out by sup posing that there are only two possibilities for a sentence: either it's true or else it's false. Furthermore, let's suppose that no sentence can ever be both true and false. (In a moment, we'll look at relaxing these assumptions, but it simplifies things to start out with them in place.) This gives us two truth values. I'll call them \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, but it doesn't really matter what we call them; we just need some names or other, and I'll use these.

We will connect our language to these truth values in two stages. The first stage involves just the atomic sentences of our language, and is characterised by total freedom. For each atomic sentence, we choose one of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} for it to have. Any way of making such a choice for every atomic sentence is called a valuation. So, for example, there is a valuation that assigns the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to every atomic sentence; and there is another valuation that assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to every atomic sentence. There is a valuation that assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} just to p and r and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to all the other atomic sentences. And there are very many other valuations besides.

Some notation: if we have a valuation named v, then we can talk about the value it assigns to an atomic sentence p by writing v(p). So if v assigns the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to p, then we can record this more efficiently by saying that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. And if a different valuation w assigns the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to p, then we can record this by saying that w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}.

When we work with these valuations, we're again bumping into infinity here, in two ways. First, since we have infinitely many atomic sentences, each individual valuation is itself infinite; it must assign one or the other of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to every one of the infinitely many atomic sentences. For this reason, while it's possible to fully specify a few example valuations, as I did two paragraphs ago, there are a lot of valuations out there that can't be fully specified at all, since they don't fit any kind of neat pattern.

Second, not only is each valuation infinite, but there are infinitely many different valuations. We're going to handle both of these sources of infinity in our usual blase way. The second source we can ignore entirely; it just doesn't really matter for our purposes how many valuations there are. The fact that there are so staggeringly many may be interesting, but isn't actually going to affect us at all. The first, though, we'll need to make some provision for. For the most part, this will be pretty subtle, and you won't need to lose any sleep over it, but I'll point it out here.

In the course of an example, I might say something like "Take a valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}". What I want to note here is that this does not fully specify a valuation v. A valuation must assign some value to every atomic sentence, and we're supposing a language with infinitely many. Lots of different valuations will meet the above condition on v. What I'm saying when I put something forward like this is just to take any valuation that meets the specification. The fact that lots of different valuations meet the specification is fine.

So that's our first stage of connecting our language to the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}: we use valuations, which are ways of assigning these values to the atomic sentences of the language. Absolutely any such way of assigning counts as a valuation, so there are lots of different valuations. In our second stage, we move on to compound sentences, letting each valuation assign values to these as well. Here, though, in contrast with the atomic sentences, there is zero freedom. A valuation can assign any values at all to the atomic sentences, but once that assignment is made, that fully determines what value the valuation will assign to every compound sentence.

This difference between atomic and compound sentences---total freedom for atomics, and total constraint for compounds---is the key to how we're going to put these valuations to use. We need total freedom for the atomic sentences because, well, there's not much to them. There's nothing about p that means it should get the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. It's just a structureless atom. Like, we might in one application let p represent the claim "Carl is a good dog"; in that case it's very definitely true. But we might in another application let p represent the claim "Hexing the moon is a good idea"; in that case it's probably false. Or we might deal with applications that let p represent a particular electronic component that can be on or off, with the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} standing for these two states. So rather than prejudging anything, we consider every possible way of assigning values to the atomics.

Compounds, on the other hand, give us something to work with: their main connective. By choosing a scheme for determining the values of compounds based on the values of their components, we endow our connectives with a certain kind of meaning. In other words, we're using valuations to give our connectives meanings, while leaving our atomic sentences fully unspecified. Let's see how it works.

The scheme we'll start with is a very standard one. These valuations are often called `Boolean valuations', after the logicians George and Mary Everest Boole, although there are some small differences from the logical approach they developed.

George and Mary Everest Boole lived and wrote in England and Ireland in the 19th century, and Mary into the 20th as well. George is typically credited alone for the logical work he published, but I think it's worth noting that Mary was an author in her own right, their relationship was clearly an intellectual one, and George's most influential book, The Laws of Thought, was written during the time their relationship was beginning. So I think it's entirely likely that Mary had some influence on George's work, although how much and of what kind is something I could only speculate on.
Incidentally, here's Mary Everest Boole's advice for students encountering infinity:

"Angels, and square roots of negative quantities, and the other things that have no existence in three dimensions [she later explicitly includes infinity as an angel], do not come to us to gossip about themselves; or the place they came from; or where they are going to; or where we are going to in the far future. They are messengers from the As-Yet-Unknown; and come to tell us where we are to go next; and the shortest road to get there; and where we ought not to go just at present. When square root of minus one comes to you, behave reasonably about him. Treat him logically, exactly as if he were six or nine; only always remember to keep well in front of you the fact of your own ignorance. You may never find out any more about him than you know now; but if you treat him sensibly he will tell you plenty of truths about your xs and ys, and other unknown things".
From Mary Everest Boole, Philosophy and Fun of Algebra, 1909.
And here's her advice for me (and maybe also for you):

"You may some day become a teacher. If ever you are teaching a class how to set down a sum or an equation, say 'This is my way', or 'This is the way which I think you will find most convenient', or 'This is the way in which the Government Inspector requires you to do the sums at present, and therefore you must learn it'. But do not take in vain the names of great unseen powers to back up either your own limitations, or your own authority, or the Inspector's authority. Never say, or imply, 'Arithmetic requires you to do this; your sum will come wrong if you do it differently'. Remember that arithmetic requires nothing from you except absolute honesty and patient work. You get no blessing from the Unseen Powers of Number by slipshod statements used to make your own path easy."
From Mary Everest Boole, Philosophy and Fun of Algebra, 1909.

This last one, I think, is of some importance when it comes to logic. Unfortunately, the world has no shortage of people who are tempted to say "Logic tells us..." and then they fill in something they're trying to convince you of. Don't fall for it. Logic is about carefulness, orientation to detail, and discerning what you do and don't have support for. It does not require you to believe or disbelieve anything. (Rant over.)

To calculate the value that a compound sentence takes on a given valuation, we need to know two things: the main connective of the sentence, and the values that its components take on the valuation. For Boolean valuations, here's how the dependence goes. If we have a negation---that is, a sentence whose main connective is 
eg ---then that sentence takes the opposite value from its component. So if we have a valuation v such that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and v(
eg 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and v(
eg 
eg 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and so on. I'll use a table like the following to record this:

This table tells you, for any value that a sentence A might have on a Boolean valuation, what value the sentence 
eg A must then have on that Boolean valuation. To use the table, if you know the value that a sentence A has on a Boolean valuation, find the row in the table that displays that value in the "A" column. Then you can read the value that that same row has in the "
eg A" column to see what value 
eg A must have. Boolean negation is simple enough that you probably won't need this table for very long, or maybe not at all; but we'll look at other options in a moment, so it's good to be able to read a table like this.

Why this table, rather than some other? That is, why have we chosen to set things up so that negations always take the opposite value from their components, rather than, say, always the same value, or always \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or always \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}? (Note that those are the only four options, given our setup.) Those other options are all perfectly good options for a unary connective. The reason we've gone with the one we have is that we want \neg to have something to do with negation, with the idea of something not being the case. The truth-value-reversal idea we've gone with is one way to try to get at that. In English, if I say that the moon is made of green cheese, then what I've said is false; and so if I say it's not made of green cheese, then what I've said is true. The idea is to try to endow \neg with a meaning at least something like the meaning of "not"; and this particular table is one attempt to do that, probably the best choice we can make given our current limitations.

The same idea comes to the fore when we look at the remaining connectives in this language. In each case, we'll choose how to deal with them based on their intended interpretation: \land as something like "and"; \lor as something like "or"; \rightarrow as something like "implies" or "if...then"; and \bot and \top as, well, we'll get to them in a minute. We're working with a very simple and coarse-grained setup here, so the match might not be perfect. But thinking about how "and" and "or" and "if...then" work can get us some way along the path to seeing what we're doing with these connectives, just like thinking about "not" can help with understanding \neg . Here's how Boolean valuations deal with conjunctions: a conjunction A \land B gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on a given valuation v exactly when both of its components A and B get value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on the valuation v. Otherwise, the conjunction A \land B gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

Why deal with conjunction this way? Think, for example, about the claim "The moon is made of green cheese, and Carl is a good dog". Is this claim true or false? Well, "the moon is made of green cheese" is false, and "Carl is a good dog" is true. So if someone said to us "the moon is made of green cheese and Carl is a good dog", then what they've said is false: the falsity of the first part undermines the whole claim. They in effect said that both of these were true, and that's not so; so what they've said is false. This is why, for \land , we go with a setup that requires both components to be true on a valuation for the conjunction itself to be true on that valuation. Just as with negation, we can record this information in a table:

Since this table is for a binary connective rather than a unary one, we read it slightly differently from the table for negation. Let's say you want to use this table to calculate w(p \land q) on some valuation w where you know that w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Use the left component of the conjunction to choose a row of the table, by finding the appropriate value along the left edge. In this case, the left component is p, and w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, so you choose the bottom row of the table. Then, use the right component of the conjunction to choose a column of the table, by finding the appropriate value along the top edge. In this case, the right component is q, and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so you choose the column headed by \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. By finding the entry where this row and column meet, you find the value your conjunction must take; in this case, this tells us that w(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. As with negation, you might find that you don't need the table for long, that it's enough to remember that both components must be true for the conjunction to be true. But as with negation, we're going to see more complex alternatives in a moment, so it's good to be able to read these tables.

The value of a negation or a conjunction, in a Boolean valuation, depends only on the values of its component(s). This is so even when the components are themselves compound sentences. So, for example, consider a valuation v where v(p) = v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; you now know enough to calculate v(
eg (p \land 
eg q)). Do it step by step: first v(
eg q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then v(p \land 
eg q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and finally v(
eg (p \land 
eg q)) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. So once you know the Boolean tables for each connective, you will be able to calculate the value of any formula on any Boolean valuation, no matter how complex the formula is, so long as you know the values of the atomic formulas it contains.

For disjunction, Boolean valuations work the following way: they make a disjunction A \lor B true exactly when they make at least one of the components A and B true. To see why, think about "the moon is made of green cheese or Carl is a good dog". Is this true or false? This seems to be true: the truth of the second part is enough for the whole thing to be true. So for disjunction, we want to be sure to pick a table that respects this. The only way for a disjunction to be false, on this way of proceeding, is for both of its components to be false; any other combination makes the disjunction true.

Note in particular the connection between \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and logicians' weird habit 1. Whenever I say "or" in these notes, I mean to include the possibility of both sides of the "or" being true. And that's just what \lor does as well, on this scheme. This is a case of life imitating art; what's happening here is that uses of the English word "or" among communities of logicians are strongly influenced by experiences with the connective \lor .
Just as with conjunction, we can record this policy in a table:

This is the weird one. I won't make excuses; Boolean implication is not a very good approximation of "if...then". Don't lean too hard, then, on the understanding you already have of "if...then"; just roll with it. Here's the table:

That is, v(p \rightarrow q) comes out to \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} in all cases except the one case where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. It's relatively uncontroversial that p \rightarrow q should be false when p is true and q is false; if p really did imply q, and p is true, then q should be true as well, or at least so goes a pretty plausible line of reasoning. So the upper-right corner of the table is pretty strongly supported. But all of the other three entries can be (and have been) contested.

Note that, given the above table, we have v(p ightarrow q) = v(
eg p \lor q) for every Boolean valuation v, no matter what v(p) and v(q) are. (Later this week, we'll pick up a word for that: equivalence.) This might be the best way to see what's behind the table above: if p implies q, then we might think that either p isn't the case or else it is, and if it is, then since it implies q we can conclude that q would be as well. So if p ightarrow q is true, this might lead us to think that 
eg p \lor q should also be true. Conversely, suppose that 
eg p \lor q is true. Then what could we conclude if p were to be true? Well, since p and 
eg p can't both be true, it would imply that q must be the true component of 
eg p \lor q. That is, p would imply q. There's a lot you could quarrel with in these lines of reasoning, but I think something like them is probably the best motivation for this table for implication. Regardless, this is how Boolean implication works, and we'll work with it in this form.

There was, alas, an embarrassing moment, mainly from the 1950s to the 1990s or so, where a decent number of philosophers genuinely defended the idea that the Boolean implication is not just an approximation, but actually fully gives the truth conditions for English "if...then" statements. To see that this doesn't work, here's an old facetious proof of the existence of God:
It's false to say that if there's a God, then the prayers of the wicked will be answered. So, by the Boolean truth table, the antecedent of that "if...then" must be true; so there is a God.

Even theists should (and do) reject this line of reasoning; it's pretty obviously bad reasoning. Embarrassments like this for the idea that English conditionals are Boolean implications can be multiplied more or less endlessly; the view is as refuted as anything ever gets. But if you happen across certain philosophy from around that time period, you should be aware that many authors did in fact believe this, despite the obvious problems.

If you're interested in learning more about this, I recommend the book Relevant Logics and their Rivals, vol. 1. Note that this was written during the period in question! It's good fun, and it gives a particularly vitriolic (and particularly Australian) takedown of what was at the time practically an accepted orthodoxy.

Finally, verum and falsum. These don't really have great natural-language equivalents. But the idea is pretty simple: \top is meant to be like the truest possible thing, MAXIMUM TRUE; and \bot is meant to be like the falsest possible thing, MAXIMUM FALSE.

What do we need to specify about these? Well, like with any connective, we need to say how to determine a value for any sentence with \top or \bot as its main connective, given values for all its components. And: sentences with nullary connectives as their main connectives have no components; these sentences are just the connectives themselves.

So we need to give values for \top and for \bot to take on every Boolean valuation. Given their intended interpretations, the choice is clear. For any Boolean valuation v at all, we have v(\top ) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(\bot ) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.
For uniformity, we can put these in "tables":

fr:title.text: Boolean valuations: putting it all together

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Boolean valuations: putting it all together

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the language we're currently focused on, that's the whole story about Boolean valuations. With the above tables, if you know what values a valuation gives to all the atomic sentences, you can calculate what values that valuation gives to every sentence in our language. Of course, you usually won't know, of any particular valuation, what values it gives to all the atomic sentences, since that's infinite! But to calculate what a valuation v says about any particular sentence, it's enough to know what v says about just the atomic sentences that actually occur in that sentence; and this is always finite.

For example, consider any valuation w such that w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and w(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and suppose you want to know what w(p \land 
eg (q \lor r)) is. By looking at the table for disjunction, you can see that w(q \lor r) must be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; then by looking at the table for negation, you can see that w(
eg (q \lor r)) must be \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; and finally by looking at the table for conjunction you can conclude that w(p \land 
eg (q \lor r)) is \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By contrast, if we were thinking about a valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and v(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then we would find that v(p \land 
eg (q \lor r)) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. (Make sure you think that one through, to see how I got the answer!)
You now know enough to be able to do this for any sentence in our language. It's a good idea to get some practice in; this is the topic of an exercise type on https://binchicken.one.

Now I'm going to give you a slightly different way to think about what we've just gone over. This way is conceptually a bit more out there, but I think it makes it easier to do the calculations, and eventually even makes it easier to consider other possibilities. Don't worry too much if this section seems a bit much at first; but if it does, make sure you return here after you've had some more practice! What's here will in fact make things easier, once you grok it.

The conceptual move is this: we don't have to think about connectives as only acting within the language, forming compound sentences from their components. It can help to think of connectives as living a double life, acting on truth values directly. Rather than saying something like "for any sentence A and any valuation v, if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}", we can say the same thing much more simply by just reporting that \neg \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By saying this, we're not saying that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} are sentences. They're not. They're still truth values, which are something else. What we're doing is extending our connectives so that they have the ability to act on truth values too. You can read all our earlier connective tables in this way. So, for example, you can consult the disjunction table to see that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or that \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

If you want to see this in action, you can check out https://negation.rocks/emu.html, which is another site I've made for logic practice. (You don't log in to this one, so it's not any part of your assessment; it can be a handy source of practice nonetheless, though.) If you head over there, you'll see that this is also the method I've used there to explain the correct answers for some exercises. Just as having a good notation for intermediate results can be useful in arriving at the right answer, it's also useful for explaining how the correct answer was arrived at, and hopefully it will be useful for you to be able to zero in on the missteps you make as you practice. (And of course you will make missteps! That's how learning works.)

So that's the practical benefit. The conceptual benefit is this: it lets us package up everything we know about Boolean valuations into something called a structure. A structure is a set of values, in this case \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, together with an account of how our connectives act on these values.
A structure for a language is a set of values, together with an account of how every connective in the language acts on those values.
And given a structure, we can say what it takes to be a valuation in that structure:

A valuation in a structure for a language is a function from sentences of the language to values in the structure, where the value of every compound sentence in the language is related to the values of its components in the way the structure indicates.
(Note that since any such function is a valuation, that means that valuations are not restricted at all when it comes to the values they assign to atomic sentences.)
This means that you can specify everything about how our Boolean valuations work just by putting down the tables we've already seen:
