In giving a structure via tables like this, there's no need to separately specify the set of values, since you can read those off the edges of the tables. By packaging everything up into a structure in this way, we make it easy to change from one structure to another, to think about different kinds of valuations we might want to draw on, beyond just Boolean ones. In a moment, we'll look at a few other example structures, besides this Boolean one. But first, I want to look a bit at some uses of Boolean valuations.

fr:title.text: Uses of Boolean valuations: possible worlds

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Uses of Boolean valuations: possible worlds

fr:mainmatter.html:p: One way Boolean valuations get put to use is as surrogates for possible worlds. In certain areas of philosophy, talk of possible worlds is very common. This began, as far as I know, as an attempt to explain what we mean by saying that some eventuality is possible. To understand what we mean when we say that something is possible, some philosophers have proposed that this is the same as to say that it's true somewhere---just maybe somewhere else, at some possible world. And to understand what we mean when we say that something is necessary, some philosophers have proposed that this is the same as to say that it's true everywhere---that is, at every possible world. But even philosophers who don't accept this view as an analysis of possibility and necessity sometimes find it convenient to frame their views as involving possible worlds.

Exactly what these possible worlds are is a topic that much exercises metaphysicians, and for our purposes is best left to one side. Whatever possible worlds are, one thing they are often understood to do is to determine a truth value for every proposition, every claim about how things are. Moreover, there are meant to be many different possible worlds, determining these truth values in many different ways. These two features make them a lot like our valuations: every valuation fixes a truth value for every sentence in our language, and there are lots of different valuations, fixing these truth values in different ways.

For this reason, these Boolean valuations are often brought in for study as surrogates for possible worlds. This is particularly common among thinkers who actually believe, what we have only supposed, that every sentence really is either true or false, and no sentence is both---and moreover, who believe that this not only happens to be the case, but is necessary. That view fits well with the two-valued setting Boolean valuations provide.

These valuations are only surrogates, though and not the worlds themselves. This is because possible worlds are meant to settle actual claims. For example, Ellie likes to dance is true in the actual world, but not in some other possible worlds; but nothing in our toy logical language has to represent Ellie likes to dance. We just have ps and qs and such, and their compounds, not any real claims. Still, the analogy is close enough that it has sustained a lot of philosophical work on possible worlds.

Another way Boolean valuations get used is in understanding certain kinds of electrical circuits. In particular, Boolean valuations are of most use for considering circuits where every component has only one of two states: on or off. Consider, for example, a light installed in the ceiling that can be controlled from either of two switches. Each switch has two possible states: on or off. And the light has two possible states: on or off. These states are related to each other in a somewhat complex way, and we can use our language, and Boolean valuations, to understand it.

Let's understand the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} now, not as truth values at all, but instead as the states on and off, respectively; and let's understand our components---the two switches and the light---as sentences in our language. Call one switch p and the other switch q. Just as we have valuations that give us every possible combination of values for p and q, so too we can run around the room and set up every possible combination of states of the two switches.

What sentence should we associate with the light? Well, how should the light respond to the switches? We want to set things up so that, no matter what state things are currently in, and no matter which switch we're nearest to, we can always change the state of the light by changing the nearest switch. A diagram might help here. We have four possible states of the switches, which I'll call v_{1}--v_{4}, and we can move between these states as shown here:

We can think of these states as Boolean valuations on a language with the two atomic sentences p and q. For example, we'd have v_{2}(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, since switch p is on in state v_{2}; and v_{2}(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, since switch q is off there.

We want to wire up a light now so that, whichever one of these states we happen to be in, flipping p will change whether the light is on or off, and so that, whichever one of these states we happen to be in, flipping q will also change whether the light is on or off. That is, we want the light either to be on in states v_{1}, v_{4} and off in states v_{2}, v_{3}, or vice versa. Can we build a sentence, then, using the atomic sentences p, q and our connectives \land , \lor , \neg , that works like this? If we can, the structure of such a sentence will tell us how to wire up the light. (That is, it will tell us how to wire up the light if we have electrical components that correspond to the connectives \land , \lor , and \neg ! Such components are standardly called AND, OR, and NOT gates, respectively.)
Can you find such a sentence?

In fact, there are infinitely many sentences that would work here (although there are also infinitely many that don't work!). Consider, for example, the sentence (p \lor q) \land \neg (p \land q). If you do your Boolean calculations out, you will see that this sentence takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on valuations v_{2} and v_{3}, and takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} on valuations v_{1} and v_{4}; that makes it an example of what we're looking for. But there are infinitely many other sentences that work equally well here; it's great if you came up with a different one!

If you've ever made even a small redstone device in Minecraft, you've almost certainly made use of Boolean valuations in this way; redstone torches are the component corresponding to \neg , and just running two lines of redstone together corresponds to \lor .

There is no simple block in Minecraft that calculates \land ; instead, there are various ways to combine 
eg and \lor that produce the same results. For example, consider the two sentences p \land q and 
eg (
eg p \lor 
eg q). By checking all the possible values p and q can take, you can see that these two compound sentences have the same value as each other in every valuation. So even if you don't have a component that calculates \land directly, so long as you have components for 
eg and \lor , you can get the job done.

However, Minecraft redstone, like real wiring, is way more complex than this! Only some Minecraft devices are on/off the way Boolean valuations are. Basically anything involving a comparator, you'd want to be thinking in terms of a sixteen-valued logic, not a two-valued logic. (For an introduction to this sixteen-valued logic, you can see this talk by the logician Roy Cook.) Concerns about timing and spatial arrangement, which are central to redstone (and real!) wiring, are also not captured by Boolean valuations.

And the flash memory in your phone is almost certainly what's known as NAND flash memory, because it's based on a circuit that calculates something known as NAND. NAND is another binary connective, one we won't be taking much of a look at this semester. But it too can be built from what we do have: p NAND q always has the same value as \neg (p \land q). Indeed, this is why it's called "NAND"; the name comes from "not-and".

You might have heard that computers, at their most basic level, deal only with zeroes and ones. Taken literally, this is just plain not true. But you're now in a position to understand the thing that is true that explains why people say this.

An electrical conductor can carry varying amounts of electric potential (this is the thing that's measured in volts). In most computers, however, the conductors that carry and hold information are not treated in a way that varies smoothly; they are kept either at a high voltage or at a low voltage. That's what computers deal with at a basic level: high voltage and low voltage.

It's conventional to represent that high voltage with the number 1 and the low voltage with the number 0 for human readability. So it's not that computers use lots of zeroes and ones; it's that we use lots of zeroes and ones to represent differing voltage levels. Computers use these differing voltage levels.
But why zero and one? Why not the actual voltage measurements, or the symbols L and H, or anything else? The answer goes back (again) to the Booles.

In The Laws of Thought, George Boole draws a connection between conjunction between properties and multiplication on numbers. But he notices a disanalogy: for most numbers n, we do not have n \times n = n. However, for any property P, being P and P is the same (so Boole says) as being P. This might seem to threaten the analogy between conjunction and multiplication---but Boole pulls a tricky turn.

Note that we do have n \times n = n in the special cases where n is either zero or one. So Boole says that properties behave not like just any numbers, but like the numbers zero and one in particular, because these are the numbers that are their own squares. As far as I can tell, this is the origin of the zero/one notation that we still see today.

Now, let's turn to a different structure. In the last section, we thought about two truth values: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. As part of that, we supposed that every claim was either true or false, and not both. But this might be too restrictive. Lots of people think that there are claims that are neither true nor false, or claims that are both true and false.
For example, think about certain claims about the future, like "In 2036 the ALP will no longer exist". Here's a view someone might hold about sentences like this:

"For such a claim to be true, there would have to be something right now that guarantees its truth. But there isn't any such guarantee. Sure, the ALP might go out of existence in the nearish future, as its urban base goes more and more to the Greens; but this trend might reverse, or the ALP might adapt to it and find other ways to survive. Similarly, for such a claim to be false, there would have to be something right now that guarantees its falsity. But there isn't any such guarantee. Sure, the ALP might manage to survive until 2036, but they also might continue to stand for the rapidly-dissolving status quo and so fall apart. Or climate change might completely upend the Australian political system. So the claim isn't true, and it isn't false."

Now, there's lots to disagree with in the above passage. Maybe you think that the ALP is guaranteed to survive the next 15 years, or alternately that it's already doomed. If you think either of those things, though, you can probably just change the example: pick something about the future that you think isn't guaranteed. (Of course, that only works if you think there's something that isn't guaranteed! So there's another place you might disagree.)

But even if you think the ALP's future isn't guaranteed either way, you still might resist the claim that something has to be guaranteed to be true/false in order to be true/false. You might think instead that, even though there's not now any guarantee which one it is, still either the ALP will be around in 2036 or not, and that's all it takes for the claim to be either true or false. You don't have to think that we can now know the truth value of a sentence to think that it has one, and similarly you don't have to think that something is determined in order to think that it's true.

Whether you agree or disagree with the point of view represented in the offset passage, though, we can use logic to try to understand the point of view. To do this, we go along with the passage. The values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} alone aren't enough to do this. According to the passage, there is another option: some sentences are neither true nor false. We can use the symbol \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} for this third option.

For a different kind of example, picture a colour that's right on the border of what you'd call "blue". Pick a colour where, if someone asked you whether it was blue yes or no, you wouldn't be sure how to answer. Thinking about this colour, consider the claim "The colour is blue". Here's a view someone might hold about claims like this:

"There are lots of different places we can reasonably draw the line between what's blue and what's not blue. For a claim like this to be true, it's enough that there is some way to draw the line that makes the claim come out true. Since the colour at least isn't obviously not blue, there is a reasonable way to draw the line that includes the colour. So the claim is true. At the same time, for a claim like this to be false, it's enough that there is some way to draw the line that makes the claim come out false. Since the colour isn't obviously blue, there is a reasonable way to draw the line that excludes the colour. So the claim is also false. The claim, then, is both true and false."

Again, there's lots to disagree with in this passage. Maybe you think that there's no colour that could be reasonably classified as blue and also reasonably classified as not blue. Or you might think that it isn't enough for the claim to be true that the colour could be classified as blue, or not enough for the claim to be false that the colour could be classified as not blue. But whether you agree or disagree with the view in this passage, we can use logic to try to understand it. Again, to do so we go along with the passage. Now, we should think that even the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} alone aren't enough; we need some value for claims that are both true and false. Let's use a new value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} for this purpose.

So now we've got four truth values to think about: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Of these, \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} are as before; we use these for things that are true (without also being false) and things that are false (without also being true), respectively. The new value \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} is for things that are neither true nor false, and the new value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} is for things that are both true and false.

Let's make this into a structure for our language. To do so, we need to say how our connectives are meant to work on the four values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. For this, there are tons of options; too many to realistically survey them all. For example, for negation alone we need to make four choices (what values are 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, and 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}?), and for each of these there are four possible answers. That gives 4^4 = 256 possibilities, and that's just for negation alone. Each binary connective requires sixteen choices, giving 65,536 possible tables to choose from for each of \land and \lor .
Again, though, we're just going to look at one particular structure from this mess of options. The idea will be to imitate, as well as we can, the way things worked in the Boolean structure.

Here's an example of what I mean: in the Boolean structure, we have 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so we'll bring those over into our four-valued structure as well. What about 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}? Well, recall that \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} is meant to represent being both true and false. We've decided that a negation should be true when its component is false, and false when its component is true. So if its component is both true and false, the negation itself should also be both: false because its component is true, and true because its component is false. So we have 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. And similarly for 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}: this can't be false because \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} isn't true, and it can't be true because \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} isn't false, so it must not be either. That is, 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. Different structures might go different ways here; it's not like this is forced on us or anything. But it's at least a reasonable place to work from, and it's what we'll go with. This gives us the table:

For \land , we can adopt the same strategy. Our guiding principles will be this: a conjunction is true exactly when both of its components are true, and false exactly when at least one of its components is false. That gives us the following table:

Think, for example, about \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Is it true? Is it false? Well, its left component is true, and its right component is both true and false, so both of its components are true; the overall conjunction must be true as well. Also, since its right component is both true and false, at least one component is false, so the overall conjunction must also be false. That means the overall conjunction is both true and false, and so has the value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, as the table says. All the other entries in this table can be arrived at in the same way.

This does mean that we've got to distinguish between being true and taking the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. On this kind of structure, there are two different ways to be true: one represented by \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and the other by \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Just like being both tall and happy is a way of being tall, being both true and false is a way of being true. Similarly, we've got to distinguish between being false and taking the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. On this kind of structure, there are two different ways to be false: one represented by \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and the other by \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Just like being both tall and happy is a way of being happy, being both true and false is a way of being false.

And we can do the same for disjunction, with these guiding principles: a disjunction is true exactly when at least one of its components is true, and false exactly when both of its components are false. That gives us the following table:

For implication, let's stick to what we noticed for Boolean valuations: that v(p \rightarrow q) = v(\neg p \lor q), no matter what values v(p) and v(q) are. Let's require that here as well; it gives the following table for implication. (Compare the implication table to the disjunction table; note that the difference is just in swapping the first and last rows with each other. Why is that?)

Finally, let v(\bot ) always be \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}---still MAXIMUM FALSE, and now not at all true either; and let v(\top ) always be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}---MAXIMUM TRUE. So now we've got a full structure for our language: four truth values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and a full specification of how our connectives operate on those truth values. I'll call this structure the Dunn-Belnap structure, since it is often associated with the logicians J. Michael Dunn and Nuel Belnap.

Belnap and Dunn were US logicians; Dunn was Belnap's PhD student. My own logical lineage (and so now yours as well) traces back to them; Dunn taught Gary Hardegree, who taught Jc Beall, who taught me.

With the Dunn-Belnap structure in hand, we have a new kind of valuation available: Dunn-Belnap valuations. These valuations work just like our Boolean valuations did, except they make use of the Dunn-Belnap structure instead of the Boolean structure.

That is, we again allow total freedom for atomic sentences: every possible way of assigning one of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to each atomic sentence is a valuation. And we again use this to make it so that each valuation determines a value for every sentence in the language, by following along with the Dunn-Belnap structure.

So, for example, consider a valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. What is v(p \land (\neg q \lor r))? Pause for a moment and try to answer this question before you read on; you have enough information to do it.

Ok, here we go. To calculate the value that the valuation v assigns to p \land (\neg q \lor r), I'll first replace the atomic sentences with the values v assigns to them. So we're looking at \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land (\neg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}). With that done, we just go step by step, following the tables:
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land (\neg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {N}})
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land (\mathsf {\mathbf {B}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {N}})
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} So v(p \land (\neg q \lor r)) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Evaluating sentences in the Dunn-Belnap structure like this is an exercise type over at https://binchicken.one.

Just like Boolean valuations are sometimes used as surrogates for possible worlds, so too are Dunn-Belnap valuations. The difference between them, in this use, amounts to a difference in what counts as possible. For example, in every Boolean valuation v, we have v(p \land 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; it doesn't matter whether v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So using Boolean valuations for possible worlds fits well with a view where contradictions like p \land 
eg p---sentences that say that something both is and isn't the case---are impossible. On the other hand, in Dunn-Belnap valuations, when v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, then v(p \land 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} as well. That is, there are Dunn-Belnap valuations in which contradictions can count as true (and also false). So using Dunn-Belnap valuations for possible worlds fits well with a view where contradictions like p \land 
eg p are possibly true. That is, we can use different logical tools to try to understand different views about what's possible.

This gives one example of a very common phenomenon to see in uses of logic. Once we've set up a particular logical approach to something, we get firm results that follow within that approach. In the example, if we choose to use Boolean valuations for possible worlds, we get the result that all contradictions are impossible. But that's not because "logic itself" forces those results on us (whatever that would mean). It's just because we set up the particular approach we did. If we had set up a different approach, we could have gotten different results. In the example, if we instead use Dunn-Belnap valuations for possible worlds, we get the result that contradictions are possible. So the study of logic alone is probably not going to teach us much about, say, whether contradictions are or are not possible. Logic is rich and varied, and can be used to explore both options. If we want to reach firm conclusions about whether contradictions are or are not possible, those conclusions are going to have to come from somewhere else. Logic will be happy either way.

That doesn't mean, though, that people who think that contradictions are always impossible can have no use for Dunn-Belnap valuations. It just means that they'd use them in a different way. Indeed, Belnap himself was among the people who think that contradictions are always impossible, and the application I'm about to lay out is due to him.

For example, imagine that you only know what people tell you, that people only tell you about atomic sentences, and that the people who tell you things only use the Boolean values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. (That's a pretty significant idealization, given that you're a human, but it's perhaps not too far off the situation certain databases find themselves in.) Since different people might tell you different things, this gives you four possibilities for each atomic sentence: either you've just been told that it's true; or you've been told both that it's true and that it's false; or you've just been told that it's false; or you haven't been told anything at all. We can use the Dunn-Belnap values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, respectively, to record these states. So a Dunn-Belnap valuation can now be seen as giving a record of what you've been told about what atomic sentence.

By calculating the values of compound sentences on this valuation, we can see what you've been implicitly told about these compounds. For example, if you've been told both that p is true and that it's false, and you've been told just that q is false, then we can represent your information state with valuation v such that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. What have you been implicitly told about p \land q? Well, you've been implicitly told that it's false, since any conjunction with at least one false component is false, and you've been told that both components are false. And you haven't been implicitly told that it's true, since it takes two true components for a conjunction to be true, and nobody's told you that q is true. So you've implicitly been told that p \land q is false, and not that it's true. And indeed, v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, following the Dunn-Belnap structure. In this way, we can see Dunn-Belnap valuations as representing certain kinds of epistemic states: states that can be conflicted (through the value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}), or that can remain neutral (through the value \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}), as well as simply believing something to be true or false.
I'll pause here to introduce a useful idea: the idea of sentences being equivalent to each other in a structure. (Think "equi-valent" like "equal-valued".)
Sentences A and B are equivalent in a structure iff, for every valuation v in that structure, v(A) = v(B).

Note two things: first, we need to have a structure in mind to say when two sentences are equivalent, and we might get different results by looking at different structures. Second, once we have a structure in mind, equivalence looks at every valuation for that structure, at every possible way of assigning values to atomic formulas.

For example, p and 
eg 
eg p are equivalent in the Boolean structure: no matter what Boolean valuation v we look at, v(p) = v(
eg 
eg p). But p and q are not equivalent in the Boolean structure. Yes, there are some Boolean valuations that give the same value to p as to q, but that doesn't matter. Since there is at least one Boolean valuation w with w(p) 
eq w(q), that means that p and q are not equivalent in the Boolean structure.

We've set up our implication in the Dunn-Belnap structure so that p \rightarrow q comes out equivalent in this structure to \neg p \vee q. That was inspired by the Boolean structure, where these same two sentences are also equivalent. And it will continue into our next structure as well. However, this is not required; structures can set up their connectives any which way. If you want to play with some structures where p \rightarrow q and \neg p \vee q are not equivalent, you can check out some of the structures at https://negation.rocks/emu.html.

As you might have noticed, in presenting Dunn-Belnap valuations, I started from the Dunn-Belnap structure. That's how I'll continue to present different kinds of structures and valuations: by presenting the structure first. That determines what the valuations are: the different ways of assigning the values in the structure to the atomic sentences, extended to all sentences by following what the structure says about the connectives.
Let's look now at the following structure, known as the strong Kleene structure, named after the logician Stephen Kleene:

Kleene (pronounced "CLAY-nee") was a 20th-century US logician. His son would later say, of the pronunciation CLAY-nee, "As far as I am aware this pronunciation is incorrect in all known languages. I believe that this novel pronunciation was invented by my father". (See https://foldoc.org/Stephen+Kleene.)
This is a very widely used and studied structure, and it's one we'll use a lot this semester.

Indeed, this structure is so widely used and studied that there's not really any single fixed interpretation we can give to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Sometimes people think of it as "neither true nor false", sometimes as "both true and false", sometimes as "half true", sometimes as "an unknown truth value", and sometimes in other ways. But however it is interpreted, the structure is the same. For now, pay attention just to the structure itself.

fr:title.text: Looking at a structure without interpreting it

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Looking at a structure without interpreting it

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Even without any interpretation at all for the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, we can still see how the strong Kleene structure works, and we can build up strong Kleene valuations using this structure in just the way you would expect. (Again, you have exercises on this at https://binchicken.one.) The same is true of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; you don't need to think of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} as connecting to truth, or of \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as connecting to falsity, to be able to see how the strong Kleene structure works in full.

Indeed, you don't need to think of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as truth values at all; let's just call them "values". All you need, in order to be able to use the structure, is to know how connectives act on these values. That information is contained entirely in the above tables. So you don't need to interpret this structure at all to be able to use it.

The same is true of all our structures. Although I've tried to help you get the feel for the Boolean structure and the Dunn-Belnap structure by having you think about truth and falsity, the structures themselves don't depend on this way of interpreting them. All the structures really are is some collection of values and a way to calculate on them using the connectives.

This is why we're able to use these structures for purposes other than thinking about truth. Recall our electrical interpretation of the Boolean structure. A switch that's on isn't true, and a switch that's off isn't false; they're just on or off. But we used the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to represent being on and the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to represent being off. This required divorcing \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} from their initial interpretation as truth values.

Similarly when we think about the epistemic interpretation of the Dunn-Belnap structure. You might have been told both that Carl is a good dog and that Carl's not a good dog, and we can use the value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} to keep track of that. But that doesn't mean that "Carl is a good dog" really is both true and false. (In fact, it's not false at all!) So in this interpretation, we're using the values as records of reports, again not as truth values. It's possible to interpret these structures in many different ways because the structures themselves don't depend on any particular interpretation; they're fully abstract.

So if \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} don't have to have anything to do with truth and falsity, why are they called "\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {F}}"? Here's the real answer: well, we had to call them something, and the suggestion of truth and falsity carried by the names "\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {F}}" can be useful for building your intuition, as long as you don't get stuck on the idea. The same goes for the values \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and the names "\mathsf {\mathbf {N}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {B}}" we're using for them. Once you're familiar with these structures, the names for the values don't matter at all. We could easily use different names.

For example, sometimes people will present the Boolean structure using the names "1" and "0" for its values, rather than "\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {F}}", as I've done here. But it's still the same structure, so long as all the connectives behave the same way. For example, \neg 1 = 0, and 1 \lor 0 = 1, and so on. We're just calling the values something different; as long as they act the same the difference doesn't matter.

In thinking about all of our structures, then, it can be helpful to open up a part of your mind that lets you think about them without any particular interpretation in view, and even without any particular names for the values in view, since these names are often linked to particular interpretations. As much as you can, try to just think about the values themselves and how the connectives operate on them, separate from any ideas about truth, or electricity, or possible worlds.

It's by proceeding in such an abstract way, separate from all applications, that we can get away with doing the work just once. It can then be applied over and over, wherever we want it. The logician Rósza Péter put the point this way (emphasis added):

"Mathematics is really a language, a queer language which speaks entirely in symbols. The above formula is only a symbol, and means nothing by itself; everybody can substitute into it [their] own experiences. For one it might mean the counting of the diagonals of a polygon, for another the counting of the number of possibilities for choosing the leading pair among [their] pupils. The writing down of a formula is an expression of our joy that we can answer all these questions by means of one argument."
From Rósza Péter, Playing With Infinity, 1955

Rósza Péter was a Hungarian logician in the mid-20th century. Her work was particularly important in the development of an area of logic known as "recursion theory". (See the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy article here for an introduction.)
"The writing down of a formula is an expression of our joy". I think Péter has hit the nail on the head.

One idea that we'll use a lot this semester is the idea of a set. This is an idea that nowadays is studied mainly in mathematics, but its history is closely intertwined with the history of logic itself. Logic and set theory grew up together, with their childhood in the late 19th/early 20th century, their awkward teen years through the mid to late 20th century, and they're really coming into their young adulthood well these days. Because they're so closely related, you can't really study either of them without learning at least a little about the other.

For this semester, we will need only some very basic set theory, but we do need it. In particular, the idea of a set is central to the ideas of an argument and a logic, and we need those ideas on the table to really get rolling this semester. So this week's first goal is to introduce you to these three ideas---first sets, then arguments, and then logics. The second goal is to look at some different questions we can ask about logics: what they're like and how they relate to each other.

A set is a collection of things, any things at all, thought about as if the collection were its own separate thing. For example, there is a set of the students enrolled in ATS2866, and there is a set of all the Facebook accounts, and there is a set of all the even natural numbers. (The "natural numbers" are 0, 1, 2, \ldots .)

Let's take the set of the students enrolled in ATS2866. You are (probably) a member of this set. (I mean, you're more than welcome to be reading this even if you're not! It's just that I suspect that most readers are.) The set itself is not a student; it is something else: a set. If you smell metaphysics lurking around the corner here, you're not mistaken. But let's not turn that corner; let's just see how we work with sets.

In working with sets, we need to know how to indicate which set we're talking about. There are two standard ways we can use to talk about particular sets: by listing the members, or by giving a description of something that the members have in common, and that nothing else has.

To give a set by listing the members, we give the list between curly braces. So, for example, \{p, q, r\} is a set that contains the three atomic formulas p, q, r, and doesn't contain anything else. And \{Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra\} is a set that contains those eight cities and doesn't contain anything else.

To give a set by description, we just say what's in it. So when I say "the set of students enrolled in ATS2866", I've specified a set in this way. I didn't list all the students; I just gave a description that applies to all and only the students enrolled in ATS2866. This is the other main way of specifying a set. As another example, we can think of the set of all the Australian state and internal territory capitals. This set contains the same eight cities as the set listed above. (The Jervis Bay Territory is another internal territory, but it has no capital.)

All that matters to a set is what's in it, what its members are. There isn't anything else to the identity of a set than the identities of its members. This property of sets is called extensionality. This means that the set \{Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra\} is the very same set as the set of Australian state and internal territory capitals. Same members, same set.

This also means that there's a potential for misunderstanding that we need to head off: although we can (and we very much will) use lists to specify some sets, sets are not lists. The reason is that there is more to lists than just what things they contain: lists also have order, and can contain the same thing multiple times. Sets, though, do not have order, and they do not contain things any particular number of times---they either contain them or they do not, full stop.

So think about the list "Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra". This is not the same list as "Canberra, Melbourne, Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Darwin, Hobart, Perth, Melbourne, Adelaide". The two lists differ in order, and the first one contains Melbourne just once, while the second contains it three times.

But although these are not the same list, the set \{Melbourne, Sydney, Brisbane, Adelaide, Perth, Hobart, Darwin, Canberra\} is the very same set as the set \{Canberra, Melbourne, Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane, Darwin, Hobart, Perth, Melbourne, Adelaide\}. That's because these sets contain the very same things: just these eight cities, and nothing else. Even though I've used two different lists to talk about this set, the set I'm talking about remains the same. This is key to thinking about sets: all that matters is which things are members. It doesn't matter what we call those things, or what order we list them in, or whether we list things more than once.

There's another upshot of this: a set can't change which things are its members. Any supposed change in membership just means we're talking about a different set. Like, consider the set of students enrolled in ATS2866. That's a set containing certain people and not others. If your friend enrols in ATS2866 tomorrow, it's not that that very set would come to contain a new member; that's not possible. It's rather that the description "the set of students enrolled in ATS2866" would come to indicate a different set than it currently does: one that includes your friend as well.

There is a set with no members, called the empty set. We can talk about the empty set using our list-the-members notation by writing \{\}---the curly braces with nothing listed inside them---or just by saying "the empty set". Note that, by extensionality, there can only be one empty set.
The most important kind of relationship between sets is the subset relation.
A set \Gamma is a subset of a set \Delta iff: every member of \Gamma is also a member of \Delta . There's a symbol for this: \Gamma \subseteq \Delta means that \Gamma is a subset of \Delta .

In the above definition, \Gamma and \Delta are capital Greek letters: \Gamma is Gamma and \Delta is Delta. It's common to use lots of different alphabets in logic, with the Greek alphabet probably the most common one other than our own Latin alphabet. I'll use capital Greek letters like these particularly for sets of things, usually sets of formulas.

When \Gamma \subseteq \Delta , we know that everything in \Gamma is in \Delta as well. There might be some other things also in \Delta as well, or there might not; that doesn't matter. (If there isn't anything else in \Delta , then by extensionality \Gamma and \Delta must be the same set.)

Some things to note about subsets:
There is an important difference between being a subset of a set and being a member of the set. For example, \{p, q\} is a subset of \{p, q, r\}, since every member of the former is also a member of the latter. But \{p, q\} is not a member of \{p, q, r\}; only p, q, and r are members of this set.
Every set is a subset of itself. For example, consider the set \{p, q \land r\}. This has two members: p and q \land r. And both of these members are indeed also members of \{p, q \land r\}, so the definition of subsethood is met. More generally, for any set \Gamma , all the members of \Gamma are indeed members of \Gamma , so \Gamma is always a subset of itself, for any \Gamma .
The empty set is a subset of absolutely any set. This might be a surprise, but remember logicians' weird habit number 2! To check whether the empty set is a subset of some set \Gamma , we need to check whether all the members of the empty set are also members of \Gamma . Since there are no members of the empty set, indeed it is true that all the members of the empty set are members of \Gamma ---no matter what \Gamma is. (Using logicians' weird habit number 2, it's even true that all the members of the empty set are square circles, that they're all plants with mammal-like brains, and that they're all behind you right now.)

There are a couple of operations on sets that are useful to know about, called intersection and union. These are not super-central to this unit, but they will come up now and again. (Intersection will turn out to be more useful for our purposes than union.)

Given sets \Gamma and \Delta , their intersection \Gamma \cap \Delta is the set whose members are exactly the things that are members of both \Gamma and \Delta . Their union \Gamma \cup \Delta is the set whose members are exactly the things that are members of either \Gamma or \Delta . (Remember logicians' weird habit 1 here!)
For example, the intersection of \{p, q, r\} and \{q, r, s\} is \{q, r\}; and the union of these same two sets is \{p, q, r, s\}.

Intersections and unions don't need to be between exactly two sets: you can take the intersection of a whole bunch of sets, and it contains whatever they all have in common; and you can likewise take the union of a whole bunch of sets, and it contains whatever is contained in any of them.

That barely scratches the surface of what can be said about sets, but it's enough for us to go on. One way sets are involved in what we'll do is through the idea of an argument, which will absolutely central for this semester. Just like our logical "languages" have something in common with real languages, but are highly idealized, our logical "arguments" too will have a very simple and very precise shape, unlike real arguments.

The kind of argument you should be thinking of here is not a debate or exchange between two parties. It's rather a collection of premises offered in support of a conclusion. (This is the most usual sense of the word "argument" in philosophy.) For our purposes, an argument is always taken from some particular language, and has two parts: a set of premises, which are all sentences of the language, and a single conclusion, which is a sentence of the language.
An argument has two parts: first, a set \Gamma of sentences, called the premises of the argument; and second, a sentence A, called the conclusion of the argument. It is written \Gamma \;/\; A.

In writing out particular arguments, I'll often abbreviate by omitting the set braces \{ and \}. So, for example, in the argument p \land q, 
eg r \;/\; 
eg p \lor r, there are two premises: p \land q and 
eg r; and the conclusion is 
eg p \lor r. The set of premises in this argument is \{p \land q, 
eg r\}.

fr:title.text: Do we have to use sets for premises?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Do we have to use sets for premises?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: There certainly are logics where premises have more texture than this! It's not like a law of nature or anything that we use sets here; we can do logic using lists instead, or things called multisets, or really any way you can think of to collect sentences into some larger whole; or we can abandon the idea of collecting up sentences and just look at a single sentence here; or we can do much more besides. But sets give us plenty to work with already for this semester, so we'll stick with them.

An argument always has one conclusion, but it can have any number of premises. That includes some extreme cases: an argument can have zero premises, when it has the empty set of premises; and it can have infinitely many premises. Even when an argument has zero premises, it's important to remember that the argument is still not the same thing as its conclusion. The conclusion is a sentence; the argument is an argument; and these are different kinds of things. For example, the argument \;/\; p \lor \neg p has no premises, and has the sentence p \lor \neg p as its conclusion; but the argument and the sentence remain distinct.

You might worry about how we're going to handle arguments with infinitely many premises. After all, I certainly can't list out infinitely many premises, so I won't be able to write out any such argument in full. But again, this isn't really a problem with infinity: just a big finite argument would already be too much to write out in full. We'll deal with this in the same old way we've dealt with other infinities: by not worrying about it. There are arguments with infinitely many premises; they're out there, they're harmless, and they mostly won't occupy us.

Given a language, we can build up lots of different arguments. Sticking for the moment with an interpretation where an argument is meant to contain premises that support its conclusion, you might notice that different arguments seem to do this more and less well.

For one example, consider the argument p \land q \;/\; p. Using your intuitions about "and" to interpret \land , you can see that this argument's premise seems to support its conclusion. If we suppose that the moon is made of green cheese and Carl is a good dog, we can conclude from that supposition that the moon is made of green cheese. Of course, the moon isn't really made of green cheese, but that's just a reason to question the premise of this argument. As long as we take the premise for granted, it really does support the conclusion.

For a different kind of example, consider the argument p \land q \;/\; r. Here, the conclusion doesn't seem to have anything to do with the premise. If we suppose that the moon is made of green cheese and Carl is a good dog, that doesn't give us any support at all for the claim that Melbourne is in Victoria. Of course, Melbourne really is in Victoria, but that's beside the point; what matters is that this isn't supported by the premise given. Even if we take the premise for granted, the conclusion just doesn't follow.

We're going to start drawing divisions between arguments, between those like p \land q \;/\; p, where the premises do seem to suffice for the conclusion, and those like p \land q \;/\; r, where they do not. But again, we're going to do this in an abstract, and so open-minded, way. Rather than fixing once and for all on a single division between good arguments and bad arguments, we're going to consider all such divisions. Each one is called a logic.
A logic is a set of arguments.

That is, a logic is a set of arguments to be counted as the good ones; any other arguments, outside the set, are to be counted as the bad ones. So any way of choosing a set of arguments gives you a logic. Since there are lots of different sets of arguments you could choose, there are lots of different logics.

How many? This gets further into some differences between different sizes of infinity that we've brushed up against before. The smallest infinity has a few names; it's called both \aleph _{0} and \beth _{0}, and it's the size of the natural numbers 0, 1, 2, \ldots . (\aleph and \beth are the letters aleph and beth, the first two letters of the Hebrew alphabet; the word "alphabet" comes from the names of these two letters. I told you logicians like our alphabets!)

Since we're using a language with infinitely many atomic formulas, if we're going to be precise we need to choose how many that is; let's say we use \beth _{0}, the smallest infinity, as the number of atomic formulas we start from. Then, even after we add all the compound formulas we can form with our connectives, we have \beth _{0} many formulas in total---we've added a lot more formulas (indeed, \beth _{0} many), but the total number has not gone up: \beth _{0} + \beth _{0} = \beth _{0}.

How many arguments can we form with \beth _{0} formulas? Well, each argument starts from a set of premises, and has a conclusion; it turns out that this means that there are \beth _{1} many different arguments. This is a new size of infinity, staggeringly large---it is the same as the number of real numbers (every number on the number line, including like - \pi and \sqrt {200}).

And then, given \beth _{1} arguments, how many different logics are there? The answer turns out to be \beth _{2}, and I have to tell you that my ability to even slightly comprehend the size of this infinity is now completely drowned out. This is beyond beyond unfathomably large. (And, as the notation might suggest, this is really just getting started. How about \beth _{23}?)

This is the kind of thing that set theorists do end up working with, but we won't need it here; I just put it in in case you're interested. If you do want to learn more, you might start at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aleph_number or https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beth_number.
The arguments contained in a logic, the ones counted as "good" according to that logic, are called valid in that logic, and the others are called invalid.
An argument is valid in a logic iff it is a member of the logic; otherwise it is invalid in that logic.

Note that it doesn't make sense, on this definition, to talk about an argument's being just plain valid or just plain invalid: it's only with some logic in mind that notions of validity or invalidity make sense. Saying that an argument is valid is like saying that someone is ready. Ready for what? Usually, people are ready for some things and not for others. Similarly, any argument is valid in some logics and not in others. It's only with some particular logic in view that calling an argument valid or invalid is meaningful.

There's not much to say in full generality about validity and invalidity; they really ar just tags for "in a logic" and "out of a logic". We'll look at particular logics with particular applications, though, and then we'll be able to see what validity in these particular cases amounts to; this will vary from logic to logic and application to application.

In full generality, though, I can at least warn you off a potential confusion. As we'll use the terms, it is only arguments that can ever be valid or invalid. It doesn't make any sense, for example, to call a sentence valid.

If you read around, you might find some older sources that will sometimes call a sentence valid. They usually mean by that what we would mean by considering the argument with no premises and that sentence as its conclusion, and calling that argument valid. Here, we'll just avoid ever calling sentences valid or invalid, which makes things simpler.

So if you ever find yourself saying something like "I think this sentence is invalid", you know something's gone wrong. Only arguments can ever be valid or invalid, nothing else; and in its purest sense, this is just a different way of saying that the argument is in a particular logic or not in it.

Since a logic is a set of arguments, you can use everything you know about sets to understand logics. For example, any argument is either in a particular logic or not; it's not in it in any particular order or any particular number of times. There can't be two different logics that have exactly the same valid arguments as each other; that would be just one logic, by extensionality.

There is an empty logic: the empty set. According to the empty logic, no argument is valid, since no argument is in the empty set. And, once we fix a particular language (say our usual one), there is a universal logic, the set of all arguments in that language. According to the universal logic, every argument is valid, since every argument is in that set.

Since logics are sets, they can bear the subset relation to each other as well. For example, the empty logic is a subset of every logic (since the empty set is a subset of every set). As another example, every logic is a subset of the universal logic: any set of arguments must be a subset of the set of all arguments.
And logics have intersections and unions, again since they are sets.

In the weeks to come, we'll meet a number of particular logics and explore a lot of their individual quirks, commonalities, and applications. For now, though, I'll just give you a few more example logics, to help the idea come into focus.
There's some vocabulary that often gets used to talk about relationships between logics: we can say that some logic is stronger or weaker than another.
To say that a logic X is stronger than a logic Y means exactly the same as to say that Y is weaker than X, and they both mean exactly this: that Y is a subset of X.

This isn't a new idea; it's just a couple of new words that we use for the idea of one logic's being a subset of another. So, for example the empty logic is weaker than every logic, and the universal logic is stronger than every logic; that's just a way of saying what you already know about these logics and the subset relation.

Note that there's a potentially confusing feature here: saying that logic X is stronger than logic Y does not mean that X and Y are different logics! Indeed, every logic is both stronger and weaker than itself, since every set is a subset of itself. Again, there's nothing conceptually new here, but the phrasing can be confusing at first; make sure to be careful!

Note, too, that the only way for a logic X to be both stronger and weaker than a logic Y is for X and Y to be the very same logic (by extensionality). But it is common, given two different logics, for neither of them to be stronger or weaker than the other. All it takes is for each of them to contain some argument that the other does not.
Now, return to the five example logics above. Which of these, if any, are stronger or weaker than which of these? Take a moment to think this through before you go on.

html:ul.html:li: Logic 1 is weaker than logic 2, which is to say that 2 is stronger than 1.
Logic 1 is weaker than logic 4, which is to say that 4 is stronger than 1.
Each of logics 1--5, like any logic, is both stronger and weaker than itself.
There are no other relations of strength among these examples.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A whole lot of new ideas this week! The ideas from these first three weeks will be with us all semester long; these are the basic ideas we'll use to frame our study of logic. From this week, you should know what a set is, and how to specify a set, either by listing its members or by describing them. You should know what it means for a set \Gamma to be a subset of a set \Delta , and you should know we're talking about sets when letters like \Gamma and \Delta show up.

You should know what an argument is: a set of formulas (premises) plus a formula (conclusion). And you should know what a logic is: a set of arguments. Finally, you should know what it means for logics to be stronger or weaker.

One idea that we'll use a lot this semester is the idea of a function. This is something we'll use in a variety of different ways; we'll see a lot of functions as we move forward. One kind of functions that we'll start to use very soon: substitutions. The purpose of this lecture is to introduce you to these ideas: first functions in general, and then substitutions in particular.

A function is something that takes an input and produces an output based entirely on that input. Whenever a function receives a particular input, it must produce the very same output. If it can produce different outputs based on the same input, it's not a function.

For example, at the end of this semester you'll have some numeric grade in this unit: some number between 0 and 100 (inclusive). That numeric grade will determine a letter grade: HD, D, or whatever. There is a function that takes as inputs numeric grades, and gives as outputs letter grades. The output letter is fully determined by the input number, so this is a function.

As another example, we can define a function that takes people as inputs and gives their birthdays as outputs. This isn't a function from numbers to numbers; it's a function from people to days. But it's still a function: everyone has exactly one birthday, so the output of this function really is determined by its input.

(You might wonder about people born overnight, where the labor starts one day and ends the next. If such people don't really have a unique birthday, then this isn't really a function from people to days.)

Many of the functions we'll be concerned with this semester take sentences from our language as their inputs. For example, last time you learned how to find the main connective of a compound formula. There is a function that takes compound formulas as inputs and gives back their main connective as output: you learned how to calculate this function.

This main connective function takes compound sentences as inputs, not just any old sentences! Recall that atomic sentences have no main connective. So the main connective function could not return any output if it received, say, the atomic sentence r as input. That means that it can't take r as an input at all, if it is to be a function.

That caution leads me to an important point: as far as we're concerned in here, every function is from some type of thing and to some type of thing. You can see that in the examples we've looked at so far:
a function from numeric grades to letter grades
a function from people to days
a function from compound sentences to connectives
Whenever we look at a function in here, you can always ask yourself (or your tutor!): what type of thing does this function take as inputs? What type of thing does it return as outputs?

Building the habit of asking these questions will make your logic learning easier. That's because it can save you from exploring a lot of dead ends! If you know, for example, that a function only takes connectives as inputs, then you know not to worry about what it does with any other input---it just can't take any other input.

For example, last time we learned about arity. You can think of arity as a function that takes connectives as inputs and returns their arities as outputs: this is a function from connectives to numbers. So if you find yourself wondering something like "what is the arity of p \land q?", you can short-circuit that wonder. It doesn't make any sense: p \land q isn't a connective, so it isn't the right type of thing to have an arity. Or if you find yourself wondering: "is there a connective with arity \lor ?", again you can short-circuit that wonder: arities are numbers, not connectives, so the question doesn't make any sense.

I recommend making it a habit to pay attention to what type of thing you're looking at, whenever you're thinking about logic. Is it a connective? Is it a sentence? (Is it both, like \bot and \top are?) Is it a function? And so on. This habit of mind will save you lots of time along the way!

This way of thinking leads to a field of study known as type theory. Type theory is very cool and awesome, and has applications both in the design of programming languages (where management of different types of data is important) and also in developing computer-checkable proofs of mathematical claims. Type theory has deep links with logic, and we'll be learning some logic later in the semester that is right next door to it.

You may have encountered the idea of a function before, in mathematics. If you haven't, don't worry about it: you're encountering the idea now, in this lecture! But if you have seen the idea in mathematics, it really is the very same idea we're using here. Like, you might have at some point in your life drawn a graph of a function given to you by an equation like y = x^2; it looks like this:

This graph represents a function from numbers to numbers. Each point along the horizontal (x) axis represents one possible input to this function, and the height of the curve above that point represents the function's output for that input, which in this case is the square of the input.

The functions we'll use in this class are just the same kind of thing---except since we don't use numbers hardly at all, we won't have such pretty pictures. Still, the input/output idea is just the same.

fr:title.text: Many functions have the same types

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Many functions have the same types

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Saying what type of thing a function is from, and what kind of thing a function is to, is not enough to specify a particular function. Typically, there are lots of different functions with the same types of inputs and outputs.

For example, think of functions that take books as inputs and give numbers as outputs. Here are just a few examples of such functions:
the function that takes each book to its number of pages
the function that takes each book to its number of words
the function that takes each book to the number of the year of its first publication
the function that takes each book to the number 23
the function that takes each book to the number 24 And the list could go on. All of the above functions are different from each other, since they all disagree with each other on the output assigned to at least some book. So there is more to a function than just its input and output types!

When we use a function on an input, that's called applying the function to that input, and the result of that application is the output. (Note well: we're applying the function to the input, not the other way around.)

So, for example, think about the function this class will use at the end of the semester to turn numeric grades into letter grades; call this function f. Suppose, say, that you get an 87 for the semester (nice work!). Then some Monash system off in some inscrutable and unaccountable office will apply the function f to the number 87, and the result will be an HD.

Another way to say the same thing is this: the function f takes 87 to HD. This is kind of picturesque: you can think of a function as like an individualised transit system, where every passenger (input) gets taken to a particular place (output). Nothing needs to actually be moving in space for f to take the number 87 to the mark HD, of course. But it's a convenient figure of speech.

There's a standard piece of notation that we'll use a lot for applying functions. If we have a function f, and we apply it to an input x, the result is f(x). And we use the symbol = to mean equality, as usual. So, to continue the above example, we can say that f(87) = \text {HD}. This just says that the function f gives the result HD when it is applied to the input 87; and that's the same as saying that f takes 87 to HD. The notation f(87) = \text {HD} says this in a more efficient and compact way.
This notation for functions and application will be with us throughout the semester: it's very handy!
One special kind of function you'll need to know about this semester is substitutions.
A substitution for a language is any function from the atomic sentences of that language to the sentences of that language. (If no particular language is specified, assume our standard language.)
Note the types here: a substitution takes atomic sentences as inputs, but can produce any sentences at all as outputs.

There are very many substitutions for our standard language. Here are just a few examples:
the substitution f that takes every atomic sentence to itself
the substitution g that takes every atomic sentence to p \lor (q \rightarrow r)
the substitution h that takes:
p to q
q to p \land q
r to q, and
all other atomic sentences to q \lor q

Since a substitution needs to assign some sentence to every atomic sentence, and since there are infinitely many atomic sentences in our standard language, specifying any particular substitution for this language requires settling infinitely many questions. So there are very many substitutions that it wouldn't be practical to describe the way I've described the above three: these will work as examples for us, but they are just examples.

You may have heard that there are many different sizes of infinity. If you haven't, then let me tell you the good news: there are many different sizes of infinity. (Indeed, there are infinitely many!)

Last time, I told you that our standard language has infinitely many atomic sentences, but I didn't get any more specific than that. As I said then, for where we're going in this class it doesn't much matter; as long as there are at least like six or seven atomic sentences that's plenty for our purposes, and past that it's kind of a who cares.

But it turns out that whatever infinity we pick for how many atomic sentences to have, we end up with more substitutions than that! For example, we might pick the smallest infinity to be how many atomic sentences we have. (There is no biggest infinity, but there is a smallest!) This is called \aleph _0 or \beth _0 (these are the Hebrew letters aleph and beth); if you're interested to learn more, you can check out aleph numbers or beth numbers.

If we have \aleph _0 atomic sentences, then we end up also with \aleph _0 total sentences in the language; our connectives give us lots of new sentences (all the compound ones!), but no more in total. This is the kind of thing that happens around infinity.

Anyhow, if we have \aleph _0 atomic sentences, then to specify a substitution we need to make \aleph _0 different choices, and since each of these atomic sentences can be assigned to any sentence at all, each of these choices is a choice from among \aleph _0 options. So there are (\aleph _0)^{\aleph _0} many substitutions in total: this is a bigger infinity also known as \beth _1, which is terrifyingly large (but still quite small, as infinities go).

Since a substitution is just a certain kind of function, we can apply it just as we would apply any other function. Building on the example substitutions from above, we have some example results:
f(p) = p
f(q) = q
g(p) = p \lor (q \rightarrow r)
g(q) = p \lor (q \rightarrow r)
h(p) = q
h(q) = p \land q

So far so usual. But substitutions have a little trick to them: given a substitution, we often treat it like a function from all sentences to all sentences in a particular way. Once you see the idea, it's not too bad, but it takes a bunch of words to explain; I'll come at it in two ways.

fr:title.text: Method 1: preserve the main connective, apply to components

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Method 1: preserve the main connective, apply to components

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a sentence and a substitution, if the sentence is atomic we already know how to apply the substitution to it. But what if the sentence is compound? Then the result we're after is this: we preserve the main connective, and apply the substitution to each of the components.

So, for example, take our substitution h from above. What is h(q \rightarrow r)? This is compound, so we want to preserve the main connective \rightarrow , and apply the substitution to each of the components: our result should be h(q) \rightarrow h(r). And what is that? Well, both q and r are atomic, so we know how to apply h to them: h(q) = p \land q and h(r) = q. So h(q \rightarrow r) = h(q) \rightarrow h(r) = (p \land q) \rightarrow q. (Note the parentheses!)

For a more complex example, think about h(p \land (q \lor \bot )). This is compound, so we want to keep the main connective as it is and apply h to the components. This gets us to h(p) \land h(q \lor \bot ). Now, h(p) is good to go, since we know h(p) = q. So our result should be q \land h(q \lor \bot ); but q \lor \bot is not atomic, so we've got more work still to do.

What is h(q \lor \bot ), then? Well, we preserve the main connective and apply h to the components. So it's h(q) \lor h(\bot ). And we know h(q) = p \land q, so h(q \lor \bot ) = (p \land q) \lor h(\bot ). Putting this together with what we already knew, we're getting closer: now we have that h(p \land (q \lor \bot )) = q \land ((p \land q) \lor h(\bot )).

That just leaves h(\bot ) to sort out. Remember that \bot is compound, not atomic, so we still can't just apply h like any normal function; we need to stick to the plan. That is, we keep the main connective the same and apply h to all the components. In this case, the main connective of \bot is \bot , and there are no components. So h(\bot ) = \bot . (Remember Logicians' Weird Habit 2: we automatically count as succeeding at applying h to all the components: all zero of them.)

Putting this all together, we get our result: h(p \land (q \lor \bot )) = q \land ((p \land q) \lor \bot ). Note that it's not just the main connective of this sentence that stayed put: all the connectives in the sentence have stayed just as they were. In effect, we've just acted on the atomics inside the sentence, replacing each of them with whatever the substitution h says. That's the key idea of applying a substitution.

fr:title.text: Method 2: think about trees, replace the atomics directly

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Method 2: think about trees, replace the atomics directly

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Another way to think about it---and this will give the same results, as long as you're doing everything correctly---is to return to visualising formulas as trees, like we did in the previous lecture.

Remember that the leaves of these trees, the very tips of the branches, are all either atomic sentences or nullary connectives. To apply a substitution to a sentence, when you think of the sentence as a tree like this, you leave the whole innards of the tree in place, and you also leave the nullary connectives at the leaves in place. You only change the leaves with atomic sentences. At each of these atomic leaves, you graft on the tree for whatever sentence your substitution assigns to the atomic that was there.
Let's look at the same example, but from this new perspective. We want to find h(p \land (q \lor \bot )). The first step is to draw out the tree for p \land (q \lor \bot ):
Finally, we graft that tree for h(p) on to every leaf with a p in our input tree, and we graft the tre for h(q) on to every leaf with a q. This gives us:
That tree specifies the formula q \land ((p \land q) \lor \bot ): just the same result for h(p \land (q \lor \bot )) that we arrived at with Method 1.
Whichever way you want to think about substitutions, the result should be the same. Think about what makes the most sense to you!

If you want to be picky: One way to think about what we've done here is to say that we're not really applying the substitution to all sentences. Instead, we've picked a particular way, given any substitution, to determine a new function that goes from sentences to sentences. That's a fine way to think about it, too; but I'll keep talking about applying the substitution, even to compound sentences. You now know what I mean by that!
It gets worse/better! In effect what I've done here is to give you a particular function that takes a substitution as an input and gives you a function from sentences to sentences as its output.

Sometimes people use the term "higher-order function" to mean a function that takes functions as inputs: since a substitution is a kind of function, the way I've shown you to extend a substitution here is itself such a higher-order function. We won't be doing much with higher-order functions this semester, but this also isn't the last one we'll see.
Substitutions allow us to make useful generalisations about whole categories of sentences. The key idea here is the idea of a substitution instance:
A sentence A is a substitution instance of a sentence B iff: there is some substitution f with f(B) = A.
Now, that definition itself makes use of two important ideas we haven't looked at yet, so our first task is to break it down and see what it says.
The first thing to think about is that word "iff". That's not a normal word, and it's not a typo for "if".

"Iff" is an abbreviation for "if and only if", and that's how it's pronounced, too: just like "if and only if". (I mean, you can absolutely pronounce it "ifffffffff" if you want to! (or iff you want to?) But it's more usually pronounced "if and only if".)

It's common for logicians (and philosophers more broadly) to use "iff" in definitions. Like in this case, I want to tell you exactly what it takes for one sentence to be a substitution instance of another. I don't just want to tell you that "if such-and-such is the case, it's a substitution instance", because that would leave open what happens when such-and-such is not the case. I want to tell you "if such and such is the case, it's a substitution instance; and otherwise it isn't"; this is just what "iff" means.

Sometimes, people will just use "if" in definitions to mean "if and only if"; this is more common among mathematicians, in my experience. The idea is that, since you know when you're being given a definition, you should know to interpret "if" in that context as meaning "if and only if". But I won't do that, at least not deliberately; I'll say "iff" when I mean it. (Assuming no typos.)

fr:title.text: What are those capital letters about?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What are those capital letters about?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The other thing that might (should!) seem unfamiliar in the above definition is its use of capital letters: it talks about "a sentence A" and "a sentence B". Nothing in anything I've said about languages so far allows for this. So what's up?

Sometimes in logic, we want to talk about particular individual sentences, like r or \bot \rightarrow (p \lor s). That mostly happens in giving particular illustrative examples. More often, though, our concerns are more general: we want to say something about all sentences, or about all sentences with such-and-such a feature. To make these generalisations, in this class we'll use capital A, B, etc.

These are not specific sentences: there is no such sentence as just "A", for example. Rather, they allow us to talk about sentences in general. The above definition, then, applies to any sentences A and B at all, saying exactly what it takes for A to be a substitution instance of B.

So, for example, it follows from this definition that p \land q is a substitution instance of q, since there is a substitution that takes q to p \land q: our running example substitution h does this, for example.

And it also follows from this definition that p \land (r \rightarrow \bot ) is a substitution instance of q \land s. I haven't given you an example substitution that shows this, but it is indeed the case! Can you find a substitution that shows this?

It also follows from the above definition that q \rightarrow p is a substitution instance of q \rightarrow p. (Indeed, the substitution that takes every atomic sentence to itself also takes every sentence to itself, and this shows that every sentence is a substitution instance of itself.) This is an important example: it shows that A and B can even be the same sentence, as we interpret the definition, even though "A" and "B" are different letters.
This brings us to the last idea for this lecture: the idea of a sentence form. This is something we'll use all the time in the weeks to come.

Sentence forms (or just forms) mix capital letters with connectives, in ways that let us generalise over sentences more precisely. For example, I can ask you to consider all sentences of the form A \land B; this is another way of asking you to consider all the conjunctions. That is, to have the form A \land B, a sentence must have conjunction as its main connective, but then its conjuncts can be anything at all. Examples of sentences that have the form A \land B:
p \land q
p \land p
(q \rightarrow \bot ) \land (r \land (p \land q))

Here's another example: consider all sentences of the form \bot \rightarrow (A \lor B). These sentences include:
\bot \rightarrow (p \lor q)
\bot \rightarrow ((r \land s) \lor (r \rightarrow \neg s))
\bot \rightarrow (\bot \lor \bot )

It's not just the connectives in a form that matter, though; repeating a letter in a form can also matter. For example, consider all sentences of the form A \rightarrow (B \rightarrow A). Here are some example sentences of this form:
p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow p)
(r \land q) \rightarrow ((r \land q) \rightarrow (r \land q))
\bot \rightarrow ((p \rightarrow (q \rightarrow p)) \rightarrow \bot ) However, the following sentences do not have this form, since the two As in the form are filled in differently:
p \rightarrow (p \rightarrow q)
\bot \rightarrow ((p \rightarrow q) \rightarrow (p \rightarrow \bot ))

Forms allow us to make complex generalisations very efficiently, and we will use them constantly throughout the semester. But it's important to keep in mind that whenever you see A, B, C, \ldots , you're not looking at a particular sentence---you're looking at a form instead. Some kind of generalisation is afoot.
This time, we learned about functions and substitutions.

A function is something that takes an input and produces an output based entirely on that input. Functions take inputs of a certain type and give outputs of a certain type: for example, there are functions from numbers to letters, from people to days, from numbers to numbers, from atomic sentences to sentences, and so on.

Giving a function an input is called applying the function to its input; the result is the output that the function determines. When f is a function and x an input of the appropriate type, we write f(x) to mean the result of applying f to x.
